Cascode Amplifier: Cascoded Amplifier with Cascade Loads, Folded Cascode Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cascode Amplifier: Cascoded Amplifier with Cascade Loads, Folded Cascode Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Cascode Amplifier — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Cascode Amplifier** is a two-stage composite transistor configuration formed by cascading a **Common-Source (CS) stage** with a **Common-Gate (CG) stage**, where the output of the CS transistor is directly tied to the source of the CG transistor. The two transistors share the same bias current, and the CG stage effectively "shields" the output node from the input, providing a very high output impedance while preserving the transconductance of the input device.

Mathematically, the cascode is functionally identical to a single CS amplifier in terms of input behavior, but its output resistance is multiplied by the intrinsic gain of the cascode transistor.

### 1.2 Cascoded Amplifier with Cascade Loads

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **Cascoded Amplifier with Cascade Loads** (often called a **Cascode with Current Source Load** or **Telescopic Cascode**) uses a cascode-connected transistor pair at the *input* and a *mirror-image cascode pair* as the active load. This topology simultaneously maximizes the output impedance at both the top and bottom of the output node, dramatically boosting the DC gain of a single-stage amplifier.

The "cascade load" is typically a PMOS cascode mirroring a reference current, providing the high-impedance pull-up required for high gain.

### 1.3 Folded Cascode Amplifier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **Folded Cascode Amplifier** is a variant of the telescopic cascode where the signal current at the folding node is "folded" through a transistor of *opposite polarity* (e.g., NMOS input transistor folding into a PMOS cascode load). This architecture allows the input common-mode voltage to be set independently of the output common-mode voltage, making it ideal for **operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs)** and low-voltage analog design.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Story Building and the Bent Pipe":**
> - Imagine a **cascode** as a **two-story water tower**. The bottom tank (CS stage) collects water (signal current) from the input pipe. The top tank (CG stage) only *releases* water; it doesn't add more, but its narrow outlet pipe restricts water flow back, so the entire structure appears almost infinite in resistance to the output city. The gain (height of the water column) is huge.
> - A **cascoded amplifier with cascade loads** is like having **two such towers mirrored top-to-bottom**, with the output drawn from the narrow gap between them — the resistance is enormous in both directions.
> - A **folded cascode** is the same idea, but the output pipe *bends backward* (folds) and uses a pump of opposite type. This lets you input water from a low pipe and output it to a high pipe without mixing flows — perfect when the input and output "ground levels" must be different.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key standard metrics (highlighted for KTU board exam recall):
> - **Intrinsic Gain of a single MOSFET:** $A_{v0} = g_m \cdot r_o$ (typically **30 – 100 V/V** per device in sub-micron CMOS).
> - **Intrinsic Gain of a cascode:** $A_{v,\text{cascode}} \approx g_{m1} \cdot (g_{m2} r_{o2}) \cdot r_{o1} = (g_m r_o)^2$ — quadratically higher than a single transistor.
> - **Process transconductance parameter:** $k'_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$ (units: **A/V²**).
> - **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85~mV}$ at **300 K**.
> - **Cut-off frequency unity gain:** $f_T = g_m / (2\pi C_{gg})$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept of the Basic Cascode Amplifier

The cascode combines two transistors **M1** (common-source) and **M2** (common-gate) stacked in series. They share the same drain current $I_D$.

**Operational Logic — Step-by-Step:**

- **Bias Point Setup:** Both M1 and M2 are biased in saturation with $V_{DS1} = V_{GS2} - V_{TH2}$ (overdrive of M2), ensuring M1 stays in saturation.

- **Signal Injection:** The small-signal input $v_{in}$ is applied at the gate of M1. M1 converts this voltage into a small-signal drain current $i_d = g_{m1} v_{in}$.

- **Current Routing:** This current flows into the source of M2. Because M2 is a common-gate stage, it presents a low impedance ($1/g_{m2}$) at its source, which prevents the drain of M1 from "swinging" significantly — this is the **shielding effect** (Miller effect suppression).

- **Signal Recovery:** The current $i_d$ emerges largely intact at the drain of M2, where it develops a voltage across the load $R_L$ or current source load.

- **Gain Output:** $v_{out} = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{out} \cdot v_{in}$, where $R_{out}$ is now extremely large due to cascode action.

### 2.2 Operational Concept of Folded Cascode

- **Input Stage (NMOS M1):** Converts $v_{in}$ into signal current.
- **Folding Node:** A constant bias current $I_{bias}$ flows into the drain of M1; the signal current subtracts from this and is pushed into the PMOS cascode (M3-M4).
- **PMOS Cascode Load:** M3 (common-source PMOS) and M4 (common-gate PMOS) form the folded load; the output develops across the parallel combination of NMOS cascode output resistance and PMOS cascode output resistance.
- **Why "Folded"?** Because the AC signal path *folds back* through a different polarity device, the input CM range and output CM range are decoupled.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for this topic. Use `\vert` for absolute values (per protocol) to maintain table integrity.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transconductance (saturation) | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D} = \sqrt{2 k'_n (W/L) I_D}$ | Square-root dependence on bias current |
| 2 | Output resistance (single MOSFET) | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_D}$ | $\lambda$ = channel-length modulation |
| 3 | Intrinsic gain (single stage) | $A_{v0} = g_m r_o$ | Typical range 30–100 V/V |
| 4 | Cascode output resistance | $R_{out,\text{cascode}} \approx g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}$ | With ideal current source at source of M2 |
| 5 | Cascode voltage gain (no load) | $A_v = -g_{m1}\,(g_{m2} r_{o2}\, r_{o1}) = -g_{m1} r_{o1} \cdot g_{m2} r_{o2}$ | Quadratic in intrinsic gain |
| 6 | Cascode with load $R_L$ | $A_v = -g_{m1}\,[r_{o1} \vert\vert (g_{m2} r_{o2}\, R_S) \vert\vert R_L]$ | $R_S$ = source impedance seen by M2 |
| 7 | Miller capacitance at M1 drain | $C_{M1} \approx C_{gd1}(1 - A_{v1}) \approx C_{gd1}$ | Negligible — shielding effect |
| 8 | Telescopic cascode gain | $A_v = -g_{m,in}[(g_m r_o)^2_{NMOS} \vert\vert (g_m r_o)^2_{PMOS}]$ | Both stacks contribute |
| 9 | Folded cascode output resistance | $R_{out} = [r_{on1} \vert\vert (g_{mn} r_{on})^2] \vert\vert [r_{op} \vert\vert (g_{mp} r_{op})^2]$ | NMOS side $\vert\vert$ PMOS side |
| 10 | Folded cascode gain | $A_v = g_{m1}\, R_{out}$ | $g_{m1}$ from input transistor only |
| 11 | Input-referred thermal noise (cascode) | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 kT \gamma \left(\dfrac{1}{g_{m1}} + \dfrac{g_{m3}}{g_{m1}^2}\right) + \dfrac{K_f}{C_{ox} W L f}$ | $\gamma \approx 2/3$ for long-channel |
| 12 | Slew rate (folded cascode OTA) | $SR = \dfrac{I_{tail}}{C_L}$ | Tail current sets charging rate |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these topologies appear in industry:**
> - **Telescopic cascode:** Used as the gain stage in **high-gain op-amps**, **LDO regulators**, and **bandgap reference cores**.
> - **Folded cascode:** The workhorse of **transimpedance amplifiers (TIAs)** in optical receivers, **active pixel sensors (APS)** in CMOS image sensors, and the input stage of virtually every modern **multi-stage op-amp** (e.g., the classic **Miller two-stage op-amp** with folded-cascode first stage).
> - **Cascade-loaded cascode:** Forms the **OTA-C integrator** building blocks in **continuous-time $\Sigma\Delta$ modulators** and **G_m-C filters**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Resistance of the Basic Cascode

**Setup:** M1 (CS) drives M2 (CG). The drain of M2 sees load $R_L$. We need $R_{out}$ looking into the drain of M2 with $v_{in} = 0$ (gate of M1 grounded).

**Step 1 — Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the drain of M2.**

Since M1's gate is grounded, the small-signal $v_{gs1} = -v_{gs,M1-source}$. But the source of M1 is grounded (assuming ideal bias), so $v_{gs1} = 0$. Hence M1 contributes only its output resistance $r_{o1}$ as a passive element.

**Step 2 — Write the small-signal KCL at the source of M2 (call this node A).**

The current entering node A from $r_{o1}$ must equal the current leaving into the source of M2:

$$\frac{v_x - v_A}{r_{o2}} \approx \text{current through } r_{o1}$$

**Step 3 — Express the current through M2.**

For M2 (CG), the small-signal drain current is:

$$i_{d2} = g_{m2} v_{gs2} + \frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}}$$

With gate of M2 at AC ground, $v_{gs2} = -v_A$. And $v_{ds2} = v_x - v_A$.

**Step 4 — Combine equations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_x &= \frac{v_x - v_A}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_A}{r_{o1}} \\
v_A &= -i_{d2}\, r_{o1} \quad \text{(current through } r_{o1} \text{ develops } v_A \text{)} \\
i_{d2} &= g_{m2}(-v_A) + \frac{v_x - v_A}{r_{o2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v_A$ in terms of $v_x$ and substitute.**

Using KCL at node A (the only AC-coupled internal node):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_A}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2}\, v_A + \frac{v_A - v_x}{r_{o2}} &= 0 \\
v_A \left( \frac{1}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right) &= \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $g_{m2} \gg 1/r_{o1},\, 1/r_{o2}$, this simplifies to:

$$v_A \approx \frac{v_x}{g_{m2}\, r_{o2}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute total $i_x$ flowing out of the test source.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_x &= \frac{v_x - v_A}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_A}{r_{o1}} \\
&\approx \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} - \frac{v_x}{g_{m2}\, r_{o2}^2} + \frac{v_x}{g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}} \\
&\approx \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} \left(1 + \frac{1}{g_{m2}\, r_{o1}}\right) - \frac{v_x}{g_{m2}\, r_{o2}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $g_{m2}\, r_{o1} \gg 1$ and $g_{m2}\, r_{o2} \gg 1$, the dominant term is:

$$i_x \approx \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_x}{g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $R_{out}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} &\approx r_{o2} \left\| (g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}) \right. \\
&\approx g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1} \quad \text{(since } g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1} \gg r_{o2}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (Board Exam Answer):**
> $$\boxed{R_{out,\text{cascode}} \approx g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}}$$
> This is the **fundamental result**: the cascode boosts output impedance by a factor of $g_{m2}\, r_{o2}$ (the intrinsic gain of M2).

### 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Gain of the Basic Cascode

**Step 1 — Input stage delivers current.**

$$i_d = g_{m1}\, v_{in}$$

**Step 2 — This current flows into the cascode output node.**

$$v_{out} = -i_d \cdot R_{out} = -g_{m1}\, v_{in}\,(g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1})$$

**Step 3 — Final gain expression:**

$$
\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_{m1}\, g_{m2}\, r_{o1}\, r_{o2} = -(g_m r_o)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A cascode's voltage gain is the **square** of a single transistor's intrinsic gain — hence the expression $(g_m r_o)^2$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Folded Cascode Voltage Gain

**Setup:** NMOS M1 input, PMOS M3-M4 cascode load, $I_{tail}$ biases the NMOS.

**Step 1 — AC current from M1:**

$$i_{d1} = g_{m1}\, v_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Current folding at node A.**

The folding node has current $I_{tail}$ flowing in from a tail current source. AC-wise:

$$i_{d3} = -i_{d1} = -g_{m1}\, v_{in}$$

(neglecting the finite resistance of the tail current source).

**Step 3 — Output resistance at drain of M4 (PMOS cascode).**

Following the same procedure as Section 3.1, the PMOS cascode resistance is:

$$R_{out,P} \approx g_{m4}\, r_{o4}\, r_{o3}$$

The NMOS M1 side contributes $r_{o1}$ directly (since M1's drain is at AC ground through the low-impedance folding node — approximately $1/g_{m3}$):

$$R_{out,N} \approx r_{o1}$$

**Step 4 — Total output resistance:**

$$R_{out,\text{total}} = R_{out,N} \left\| R_{out,P} = r_{o1} \right\| (g_{m4}\, r_{o4}\, r_{o3})$$

**Step 5 — Output voltage:**

$$
v_{out} = -i_{d1} \cdot R_{out,\text{total}} = g_{m1}\, v_{in} \cdot R_{out,\text{total}}
$$

(Note the **positive sign** — phase inversion comes from the PMOS path.)

**Step 6 — Final gain:**

$$
\boxed{A_v = g_{m1}\,[r_{o1} \left\| (g_{m4}\, r_{o4}\, r_{o3})\right.]}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Telescopic Cascode | Folded Cascode |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage Gain | $g_m (g_m r_o)^2$ | $g_m \cdot [(g_m r_o)^2 \left\| r_o \right.]$ |
| Output Swing | **Limited** (stacked V_DS) | **Larger** (single stack at output) |
| Power Dissipation | **Lower** (single branch) | **Higher** (extra bias branch) |
| Input CM Range | Restricted | **Wide** (decoupled) |
| Noise | **Lowest** | Slightly higher (extra PMOS pair) |
| Suitable for | High-gain, low-power OTAs | General-purpose op-amp input stage |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Basic Cascode Amplifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Vin"] --> nodeB["M1 Common Source Stage"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["M2 Common Gate Stage"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Output Node Vout"]
    nodeE["VDD Supply"] --> nodeF["Current Source Load"]
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeG["Vbias for M2 Gate"] --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeH["Load RL to Ground"]
    nodeI["Ground"] --> nodeB
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Telescopic Cascode with Cascade Loads

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Differential Input VIN Plus and Minus"] --> nodeB["NMOS Input Pair M1a M1b"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["NMOS Cascode Stack M2a M2b"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Output Node Vout Differential"]
    nodeE["VDD"] --> nodeF["PMOS Cascode Load M3a M3b"]
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeG["Reference Current Source"] --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeH["Tail Current Sink to Ground"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Folded Cascode OTA

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Differential Input VIN Plus Minus"] --> nodeB["NMOS Transconductance Pair M1a M1b"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Folding Nodes A and B"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PMOS Common Source Stage M3a M3b"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PMOS Common Gate Cascode M4a M4b"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Differential Output Vout Plus Minus"]
    nodeG["VDD Supply"] --> nodeF
    nodeH["IBias Current Source"] --> nodeC
    nodeI["Common Mode Feedback CMFB"] --> nodeD
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Functional Architecture Flow — Signal Path in Folded Cascode

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["Input Voltage VIN"] --> stepB["V to I Conversion by NMOS Pair"]
    stepB --> stepC["Current Folding at Summing Node"]
    stepC --> stepD["PMOS Cascode Current Mirror Load"]
    stepD --> stepE["High Impedance Output Node"]
    stepE --> stepF["Vout to Next Stage or Load Capacitor CL"]
    stepG["Tail Current Source"] --> stepB
    stepH["CMFB Loop"] --> stepD
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Biasing & Frequency Response Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    biasA["VBias1 Sets M2 Gate"] --> biasB["M2 in Saturation"]
    biasC["VBias2 Sets PMOS Cascode Gate"] --> biasD["PMOS in Saturation"]
    biasB --> biasE["All Devices Saturation Confirmed"]
    biasD --> biasE
    biasE --> freqA["Dominant Pole at Output Node"]
    freqA --> freqB["Unity Gain Bandwidth equals gm divided by 2 pi CL"]
    freqB --> freqC["Phase Margin greater than 60 degrees"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** The arrows indicate the **direction of signal flow**, while the labeled boxes represent either transistor stages (top section of each diagram) or auxiliary support circuits such as bias generators, CMFB loops, or load elements.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define a cascode amplifier. Why is it preferred over a simple common-source amplifier in analog IC design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cascode amplifier is a two-transistor composite stage consisting of a common-source (CS) transistor stacked on top of a common-gate (CG) transistor, sharing the same DC bias current. It is preferred over a simple CS amplifier because:

1. It provides a much higher output impedance, approximately $R_{out} \approx g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}$, leading to higher voltage gain.
2. It significantly reduces the **Miller effect** on the input transistor's $C_{gd}$, improving high-frequency bandwidth.
3. It offers better **reverse isolation** and stability when driving reactive loads.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q:** What is a folded cascode amplifier? State any two advantages over a telescopic cascode.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A folded cascode amplifier uses an input transistor of one polarity (e.g., NMOS) whose signal current is "folded" into a cascode load of opposite polarity (e.g., PMOS).

**Advantages over telescopic cascode:**
1. The input and output common-mode voltage ranges are **decoupled**, allowing wider input CM range.
2. The output voltage swing is **larger** because only one cascode stack sits between the rails at the output node (versus two in telescopic).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

> **Question:** With the help of a clear circuit diagram, derive the small-signal output resistance and voltage gain of a cascode amplifier loaded with an ideal current source $I_{bias}$. Compare its gain with a simple common-source amplifier.

**(a) Circuit and Output Resistance Derivation — 7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify nodes.**
The cascode has two transistors: M1 (CS) and M2 (CG). The output node is the drain of M2; we apply a test source $v_x$ there.

**Step 2 — Apply test voltage $v_x$ at drain of M2.**

The AC equivalent circuit replaces M1 with its output resistance $r_{o1}$ (since its gate is grounded, $g_{m1}$ is inactive), and M2 is replaced by its hybrid-$\pi$ small-signal model.

**Step 3 — KCL at the source of M2 (node A):**

$$
\frac{v_A}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2}\, v_A + \frac{v_A - v_x}{r_{o2}} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_A$:**

Assuming $g_{m2} \gg 1/r_{o1}$ and $1/r_{o2}$:

$$v_A \approx \frac{v_x}{g_{m2}\, r_{o2}}$$

**Step 5 — KCL at drain of M2 (output node):**

$$i_x = \frac{v_x - v_A}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_A}{r_{o1}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
i_x \approx \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_x}{g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}}
$$

**Step 7 — Final output resistance:**

$$
R_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} \approx r_{o2} \left\| g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1} \right. \approx g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}
$$

**(b) Voltage Gain and Comparison — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Input signal $v_{in}$ applied at gate of M1:**

$$i_{d1} = g_{m1}\, v_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Since the current source load is ideal ($R_L = \infty$), all $i_{d1}$ flows through $R_{out}$:**

$$v_{out} = -g_{m1}\, v_{in} \cdot R_{out} = -g_{m1}\, v_{in}\,(g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1})$$

**Step 3 — Voltage gain:**

$$
\boxed{A_v = -g_{m1}\, g_{m2}\, r_{o1}\, r_{o2} = -(g_m r_o)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with simple CS amplifier:**

| Parameter | Simple CS | Cascode |
|---|---|---|
| Gain | $-g_m r_o$ | $-(g_m r_o)^2$ |
| Output resistance | $r_o$ | $g_m r_o \cdot r_o$ |
| Bandwidth (Miller effect) | Severely limited | Largely unaffected |
| Power consumption | Same | Same (same bias current) |

> The cascode provides **quadratically higher gain** at no extra power cost, making it the architecture of choice for high-gain analog stages.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

> **Question:** Explain the operation of a folded cascode amplifier with a neat circuit diagram. Derive its output resistance and voltage gain. Mention its merits over a telescopic cascode.

**(a) Circuit Operation and Output Resistance Derivation — 7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Topology description.**

The folded cascode uses an NMOS input pair M1a/M1b driving the folding nodes. PMOS transistors M3a/M3b act as a common-source current mirror load, and M4a/M4b form the PMOS common-gate cascode at the top.

**Step 2 — Identify small-signal output resistance paths.**

Looking into the output node (drain of M4a), two paths contribute:

- **NMOS path:** The drain of M1a sees a near-AC ground through M3a's low-impedance source node ($1/g_{m3a}$). Hence M1a contributes only $r_{o1a}$ to the output resistance.

- **PMOS path:** M4a in CG configuration presents:

$$R_{out,P} \approx g_{m4a}\, r_{o4a}\, r_{o3a}$$

**Step 3 — Total output resistance:**

$$
R_{out,\text{total}} = r_{o1a} \left\| g_{m4a}\, r_{o4a}\, r_{o3a} \right.
$$

Since $g_{m4a}\, r_{o4a}\, r_{o3a} \gg r_{o1a}$, this simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{R_{out,\text{total}} \approx r_{o1a}}
$$

(Note: the NMOS input transistor limits the output resistance; this is a known design tradeoff.)

**(b) Voltage Gain and Merits — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Voltage gain:**

$$
A_v = g_{m1a} \cdot R_{out,\text{total}} = g_{m1a} \cdot [r_{o1a} \left\| g_{m4a}\, r_{o4a}\, r_{o3a}\right.]
$$

**Step 2 — Merits over telescopic cascode:**

1. **Wider input common-mode range:** Input transistors can have $V_{DS}$ set independently from output devices.
2. **Larger output swing:** Only one stack of transistors between $V_{DD}$ and $V_{out}$, leaving more headroom.
3. **Easier CMFB:** Output common-mode is sensed directly at the output node.
4. **Better for low-voltage operation:** Critical for modern sub-1V supply designs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the bias condition** ($V_{DS1} = V_{OV2}$) that keeps M1 in saturation. Examiners specifically test whether you remember that the cascode transistor's overdrive voltage becomes M1's $V_{DS}$. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing the cascode output resistance formula.** It is $g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o1}$, NOT $g_{m2}\, r_{o1} + r_{o2}$ or any other form. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 3. **Sign error in folded cascode gain.** The output voltage from PMOS load inverts relative to NMOS input, giving a *non-inverting* gain expression at the folding node — students often write a negative sign erroneously. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **Skipping the KCL steps.** You must show KCL at every internal node; do not write $R_{out} = g_m r_o^2$ without derivation in a 14-mark question. **(Lose 3–4 marks)**
> 5. **Not mentioning Miller effect suppression** as an advantage in 3-mark questions. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - A **cascode** stacks CS + CG; output resistance becomes **$g_m r_o \cdot r_o$** (boosted by intrinsic gain).
> - Cascode gain is $-(g_m r_o)^2$ — quadratic in intrinsic gain, with **no extra power**.
> - **Miller effect on $C_{gd1}$** is suppressed because the drain of M1 is held nearly AC-ground by the CG action of M2.
> - **Telescopic cascode with cascade load** uses PMOS cascode mirror; gives highest gain but **limits output swing**.
> - **Folded cascode** uses opposite-polarity devices at the folding node; gives **wider input CM range**, **larger output swing**, but slightly higher power and noise.
> - **Key design constraint:** all transistors must remain in **saturation** throughout signal swing — bias M2's gate such that $V_{DS1} = V_{OV2}$.
> - **Folded cascode** output resistance is **limited by the input transistor** ($r_{o1}$), which is a key design tradeoff vs. telescopic.
> - **Formulas to memorize verbatim:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 k'_n (W/L) I_D}$, $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$, $R_{out,\text{cascode}} = g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}$, $A_v = -(g_m r_o)^2$.
> - **Slew rate** of folded cascode OTA: $SR = I_{tail}/C_L$.
> - **CMFB (Common-Mode Feedback)** is mandatory in fully-differential folded cascode OTAs — examiners love asking why.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEECT754 – Mixed Signal Circuits (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Basic Circuit"** refers to the foundational **single-transistor amplifier configurations** and **current-mirror biasing cells** built using **MOS transistors** that form the atomic building blocks of every larger mixed-signal sub-system such as operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs), comparators, switched-capacitor integrators, and data-converter front-ends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEECT754 – Module 2):**
> *"Basic Circuit"* encompasses the three canonical single-stage MOS amplifiers — **Common Source (CS)**, **Common Gate (CG)**, and **Common Drain / Source Follower (CD)** — together with the **MOS Current Mirror**, which together form the design vocabulary of every analog and mixed-signal integrated circuit.

These basic circuits are characterized by four key small-signal parameters that the KTU examiner almost always tests:

- **Small-Signal Voltage Gain** $A_v$
- **Input Resistance** $R_{in}$
- **Output Resistance** $R_{out}$
- **Frequency Response** (dominant pole, $-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth)

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Basic Circuits

| # | Circuit | Signal Input Node | Signal Output Node | Common Terminal | KTU Tag |
|---|---------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------------|---------|
| 1 | **Common Source (CS)** | Gate | Drain | Source (AC-GND) | Inverting amplifier |
| 2 | **Common Gate (CG)** | Source | Drain | Gate (AC-GND) | Non-inverting, low-$R_{in}$ |
| 3 | **Source Follower (CD)** | Gate | Source | Drain (AC-GND) | Unity-gain buffer |
| 4 | **Current Mirror** | Reference branch | Mirror branch | — | Current scaling / biasing |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Water-Pipe Analogy" for the three single-stage amplifiers:**
>
> Imagine a **water valve (MOSFET)** controlling the flow of water (current) through a horizontal pipe (channel) connected to a pressurized tank (drain) and a lower reservoir (source).
>
> - **Common Source** = You turn the valve handle (gate) a little, and the water pressure at the far end (drain) **inverts** — twist clockwise, pressure drops. This is your everyday *inverting amplifier*.
> - **Common Gate** = The handle is bolted fixed; you instead push water in from the *source* side. Whatever you push in comes out amplified at the drain **without sign change** — like a non-inverting preamp.
> - **Source Follower** = The handle controls the valve, but you measure the water level *just after* the valve. The level just **follows** your handle setting, scaled by ~1 — a *buffer* that prevents loading.
> - **Current Mirror** = Two identical valves share the same handle setting; whatever current flows in the *reference* valve is *copied* into the *mirror* valve. It's the "current photocopying machine" of every analog IC.

### 1.4 Why "Basic Circuit" Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Approximately **25–30 %** of the total marks in PEECT754 (across Modules 1, 2, 3) are built directly on the small-signal analysis of these four basic circuits. The KTU board examiner uses them as the **first filter question** to separate rote learners from design thinkers. Mastery here is non-negotiable.

**Key Transistor Small-Signal Parameters You Must Memorize:**

- Transconductance: $\boxed{g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}}$
- Output resistance of transistor: $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$
- Body-effect transconductance: $g_{mb} = \chi \, g_m$ (where $\chi \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ for modern CMOS)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating-Region Prerequisite

> [!NOTE]
> Every analysis that follows assumes the MOSFET is in **saturation (active) region**, i.e.,
> $$V_{DS} \;\geq\; V_{GS} - V_{TH} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{GS} > V_{TH}$$
> For an n-channel device. Violating this assumption invalidates *all* gain expressions.

### 2.2 Common Source (CS) Amplifier — Step-by-Step Theory

**Circuit topology:** Input $v_{in}$ applied at the gate through a coupling capacitor; the source is tied to ground (AC); output is taken at the drain through $R_D$.

**Step-1 — DC Bias Point:** Set by gate bias $V_{GS}$ and drain resistor $R_D$ to establish quiescent $I_D$ and $V_{DS}$ such that the transistor sits comfortably in saturation with at least $\approx 100\text{ mV}$ of $V_{DS,sat}$ margin.

**Step-2 — AC Small-Signal Model:** Replace the MOSFET by its hybrid-π model: a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ from drain to source, in parallel with $r_o$. The gate draws no current (ideal MOSFET input).

**Step-3 — Voltage Gain Derivation:** With output open-circuited,

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot (R_D \Vert r_o)$$

Since $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ (no gate current, no source degeneration), the **small-signal voltage gain** is:

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$$

**Step-4 — Why the Negative Sign?** Increasing $v_{in}$ raises $v_{GS}$, which raises $i_D$, which pulls the drain voltage *down* against $V_{DD}$. Hence the **180° phase inversion** — the single most-tested KTU fact.

**Step-5 — Input & Output Resistance:**
- $R_{in} = \infty$ (gate of MOSFET is insulated)
- $R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$

**Step-6 — Real-World Utility:** The CS stage is the **workhorse voltage-gain cell** in op-amp input stages, RF low-noise amplifiers (LNAs), and switched-capacitor gain stages. Its high gain and infinite input resistance make it the *de facto* first stage in virtually every analog signal chain.

---

### 2.3 Common Gate (CG) Amplifier — Theory

**Topology:** Input at the *source*, gate tied to AC ground, output at the drain.

**Why use CG?**
- Provides **low input resistance** $R_{in} = \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ — ideal for current-mode input or as a *current buffer*.
- **Non-inverting** voltage gain.
- Excellent **high-frequency** response because the **Miller effect** is absent (the gate is not connected to the output node).
- Used as the input stage of **current-mode logic**, **wideband amplifiers**, and the **cascode** in op-amp designs.

**Gain expression:**

$$A_v = g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$$

(positive sign — *non-inverting*.)

---

### 2.4 Source Follower (Common Drain) — Theory

**Topology:** Input at the gate, drain tied to $V_{DD}$ (AC ground), output at the source.

**Why use SF?**
- Provides **unity-gain voltage buffer**: $A_v \approx 1$.
- **Very high input resistance** ($\to \infty$).
- **Low output resistance** $R_{out} = \dfrac{1}{g_m} \Vert r_o \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ — ideal for driving low-impedance loads.
- Used as the **output buffer** in op-amps, on-chip **reference buffers**, and **level shifters**.

**Exact gain expression (with body effect):**

$$A_v = \frac{g_m (R_S \Vert r_o)}{1 + g_m (R_S \Vert r_o) + \frac{R_S \Vert r_o}{r_o}} \;\approx\; \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

If the source bulk is connected to the *source* (not to substrate), $g_{mb}=0$ and the formula simplifies to $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$.

---

### 2.5 MOS Current Mirror — Theory

**Concept:** A reference current $I_{REF}$ flowing through a diode-connected MOSFET $M_1$ sets a gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$. This $V_{GS}$ is shared with a second identical MOSFET $M_2$, causing it to "mirror" the same current (or a scaled version if $W/L$ ratios differ).

**Mirror ratio:**

$$I_{out} = I_{REF} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$$

**Why is the current mirror ubiquitous?**
- Provides **bias currents** to every amplifier stage.
- Acts as an **active load** (replacing $R_D$ with a pMOS mirror) to boost gain by orders of magnitude.
- Used as a **current-mode signal conveyor** in translinear loops and analog multipliers.

**Output resistance of the mirror:**

$$R_{out,mirror} = r_{o2} \;\Vert\; \bigl(g_{m2} r_{o2} \cdot r_{o1}\bigr) \approx g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}$$

This high $R_{out}$ is precisely *why* current mirrors are used as active loads.

---

### 2.6 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet — Print This Page.**

| S.No. | Circuit | Voltage Gain $A_v$ | Input Resistance $R_{in}$ | Output Resistance $R_{out}$ | Phase |
|------:|---------|:-------------------:|:--------------------------:|:----------------------------:|:-----:|
| 1 | **Common Source** | $-g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ | $\infty$ | $R_D \Vert r_o$ | 180° |
| 2 | **Common Gate** | $+g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ | $\dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $R_D \Vert r_o$ | 0° |
| 3 | **Source Follower** | $\dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{g_m} \Vert r_o$ | 0° |
| 4 | **Current Mirror (1:1)** | N/A (current-mode) | N/A | $r_{o2} + g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1} + r_{o1}$ | — |
| 5 | **Cascode (CS+CG)** | $-g_m (R_D \Vert r_{o,cascode})$ | $\infty$ | $\approx g_m r_o^2$ | 180° |
| 6 | **Differential Pair** | $g_m R_D$ (differential) | $\infty$ | $R_D \Vert r_o$ | 0° (diff.) |

| Key Design Constant | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---------------------|---------|-----------------|
| Transconductance $g_m$ | $\sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$ | Sets gain of every stage |
| $g_m/I_D$ efficiency | $\dfrac{2}{V_{GS}-V_{TH}} = \dfrac{2}{V_{OV}}$ | Maximize for low-power design |
| Output resistance $r_o$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Sets intrinsic gain ceiling |
| Intrinsic gain $A_{v0}$ | $g_m r_o = \dfrac{2}{\lambda (V_{GS}-V_{TH})}$ | Max possible gain of a single transistor |
| Miller Capacitance $C_M$ | $C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$ | Dominant pole in CS stage |
| $-3\text{ dB}$ Bandwidth | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{out} C_L}$ | Sets speed of stage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Field Where This Is Used:**
> These four basic circuits form the analog "alphabet" of every mixed-signal IC shipped in 2024–2025 — from the **TI ADS131M04** 24-bit ΔΣ ADC, to the **Analog Devices AD8232** biopotential AFE, to the **Qualcomm PMIC** biasing network inside your smartphone. Mastering these is the same as learning the *atomic elements* before doing *chemistry*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation-1: Voltage Gain of Common Source Amplifier with Source Degeneration

**Problem setup:** A CS stage has $R_D$ at the drain and a *source-degeneration resistor* $R_S$ at the source. Derive the exact small-signal voltage gain $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$.

**Step-1: Write KVL on the gate-source loop.**

$$v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_s$$

where $v_s$ is the small-signal voltage at the source node.

**Step-2: KCL at the source node.**

The current leaving the source node through $R_S$ equals the drain current of the MOSFET (no gate current).

$$\frac{v_s}{R_S} = g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_s}{r_o}$$

**Step-3: Substitute $v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_s$.**

$$\frac{v_s}{R_S} = g_m (v_{in} - v_s) + \frac{v_s}{r_o}$$

$$\frac{v_s}{R_S} + g_m v_s + \frac{v_s}{r_o} = g_m v_{in}$$

$$v_s \left( \frac{1}{R_S} + g_m + \frac{1}{r_o} \right) = g_m v_{in}$$

$$v_s = \frac{g_m \, v_{in}}{\dfrac{1}{R_S} + g_m + \dfrac{1}{r_o}}$$

**Step-4: Express output voltage at the drain.**

The drain current is $i_d = g_m v_{gs} + \dfrac{v_{ds}}{r_o}$, but more cleanly, we use the load-line:

$$v_{out} = -i_d \cdot (R_D \Vert r_o)$$

For the small-signal analysis (ignoring $r_o$ on the drain side for clarity):

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot R_D = -g_m (v_{in} - v_s) \cdot R_D$$

**Step-5: Substitute $v_s$ from Step-3.**

$$v_{out} = -g_m R_D \left( v_{in} - \frac{g_m v_{in}}{g_m + \dfrac{1}{R_S} + \dfrac{1}{r_o}} \right)$$

$$v_{out} = -g_m R_D \, v_{in} \left( 1 - \frac{g_m}{g_m + \dfrac{1}{R_S} + \dfrac{1}{r_o}} \right)$$

**Step-6: Simplify the bracket.**

$$1 - \frac{g_m}{g_m + \dfrac{1}{R_S} + \dfrac{1}{r_o}} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{R_S} + \dfrac{1}{r_o}}{g_m + \dfrac{1}{R_S} + \dfrac{1}{r_o}}$$

**Step-7: Final gain expression.**

$$\boxed{\,A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S + \dfrac{R_S}{r_o}}\,}$$

**Step-8: Special case validation.**

- If $R_S = 0$ (no degeneration): $A_v = -g_m R_D$ ✓ (matches standard CS formula).
- If $R_S \to \infty$ (degenerate): $A_v \to -\dfrac{R_D}{R_S}$ (resistive divider, gain becomes $R_D/R_S$).
- If $R_S \gg r_o$: $A_v \approx -\dfrac{g_m R_D}{g_m R_S} = -\dfrac{R_D}{R_S}$ ✓.

**Engineering insight:** Source degeneration **trades gain for linearity** — a fundamental principle that every KTU examiner loves to test.

---

### 3.2 Derivation-2: Current-Mirror Output Resistance Using Small-Signal Analysis

**Problem setup:** Find the output resistance $R_{out}$ of a simple 1:1 nMOS current mirror, looking into the drain of $M_2$, with $M_1$ diode-connected.

**Step-1: Apply test voltage $v_x$ at the drain of $M_2$ and measure the resulting current $i_x$.**

**Step-2: Small-signal equivalent circuit.**

The diode-connected $M_1$ has small-signal resistance $r_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{g_{m1}} \Vert r_{o1} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_{m1}}$ (ignoring $r_{o1}$ for now).

**Step-3: Gate is shared between $M_1$ and $M_2$.**

Current flowing into the gate node:

$$i_x = i_{d2} + i_{gate\;leakage}$$

Since gate leakage is zero (ideal MOSFET), $i_x$ must flow entirely through $M_2$'s channel and out through $M_1$'s small-signal resistance $r_{eq1} \approx 1/g_{m1}$.

**Step-4: Voltage at the gate of $M_2$.**

$$v_{g2} = -i_x \cdot \frac{1}{g_{m1}}$$

(the minus sign: current flows from gate node to ground through $M_1$, so gate voltage is negative when $i_x$ flows *into* the drain of $M_2$).

**Step-5: $v_{gs2}$ for $M_2$.**

$$v_{gs2} = v_{g2} - v_{s2} = -\frac{i_x}{g_{m1}} - 0 = -\frac{i_x}{g_{m1}}$$

**Step-6: Current through $M_2$.**

$$i_{d2} = g_{m2} v_{gs2} + \frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}} = -\frac{g_{m2}}{g_{m1}} i_x + \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}$$

**Step-7: KCL at drain of $M_2$.**

$$i_x = i_{d2}$$

$$i_x = -\frac{g_{m2}}{g_{m1}} i_x + \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}$$

$$i_x \left(1 + \frac{g_{m2}}{g_{m1}}\right) = \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}$$

For a 1:1 mirror, $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_m$:

$$i_x (2) = \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_x = \frac{v_x}{2 r_{o2}}$$

**Step-8: Re-derive more rigorously, including $r_{o1}$.**

The complete textbook result (re-doing the same KCL with both $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$ present):

$$\boxed{\,R_{out} \;\approx\; r_{o2} + g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1} + r_{o1} \;\approx\; g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}\,}$$

For $g_m r_o = 100$ (typical), this gives $R_{out} \approx 100 \, r_o$ — a **100× boost** in load-driving capability compared to a plain resistor. This is *exactly* why current mirrors are used as active loads in op-amp gain stages.

---

### 3.3 Derivation-3: Frequency Response (Dominant Pole) of CS Stage

**Problem setup:** CS amplifier with load capacitor $C_L$ at the output. Find the $-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth $f_{-3dB}$.

**Step-1: Output node time constant.**

Using **Miller's theorem**, the gate-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ appears at the output as $C_{gd}(1 - A_v) \approx C_{gd} \cdot \vert A_v \vert$ (the *Miller multiplier*).

**Step-2: Total output capacitance.**

$$C_{out,total} = C_{db} + C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) + C_L$$

where $C_{db}$ is the drain-bulk junction capacitance.

**Step-3: Output resistance seen by $C_{out,total}$.**

$$R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$$

**Step-4: Dominant pole frequency.**

$$\boxed{\,f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2 \pi (R_D \Vert r_o)(C_{db} + C_L + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D))}\,}$$

**Step-5: Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW).**

The mid-band gain magnitude is $g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$. Therefore:

$$\text{GBW} = \vert A_v \vert \cdot f_{-3dB} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi[C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D) + C_L + C_{db}]}$$

**Engineering insight:** The Miller effect *multiplies* $C_{gd}$ by the gain, making it the dominant bottleneck for high-speed CS design. This is precisely why **cascode** and **CGD-neutralization** techniques are used in RF and high-speed ADC design.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Metric | Common Source | Common Gate | Source Follower | Cascode |
|--------------------|:-------------:|:-----------:|:---------------:|:-------:|
| Voltage Gain | **High** | Moderate (positive) | ~1 (buffer) | Very High |
| Input Resistance | $\infty$ | $1/g_m$ (low) | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| Output Resistance | $R_D \Vert r_o$ | $R_D \Vert r_o$ | $1/g_m$ (low) | $\approx g_m r_o^2$ (high) |
| Bandwidth | Limited by Miller | **Wide** (no Miller) | Wide | Moderate |
| Phase Inversion | Yes (180°) | No (0°) | No (0°) | Yes (180°) |
| Use Case | Gain stage | Current buffer | Output buffer | High-gain IF/RF |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Four Basic Circuits (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VDD Supply Rail] --> B[Current Mirror M1-M2]
    B --> C[Bias Current IREF Generation]
    C --> D[Common Source Amplifier M3]
    C --> E[Common Gate Amplifier M4]
    C --> F[Source Follower M5]
    D --> G[Inverting Voltage Output Vout CS]
    E --> H[Non-Inverting Voltage Output Vout CG]
    F --> I[Unity-Gain Buffered Output Vout SF]
    G --> J[Mixed Signal Core - Comparator and ADC Front End]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Output Driver to Off-Chip Load]
    J --> L[Digital Post-Processing Block]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The current mirror at the top generates a stable **bias current** that is then distributed to all three single-stage amplifier blocks. The CS and CG stages feed the **mixed-signal core** (e.g., a comparator or sample-and-hold), while the source follower acts as an **output buffer** driving the off-chip load.

---

### 4.2 Signal-Flow for Common-Source Amplifier (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VIN[Input Vin] --> CIN[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    CIN --> GATE[Gate of M1]
    GATE --> GM[Transconductance gm]
    GM --> DRAIN[Drain Node]
    DRAIN --> RD[Resistor RD]
    RD --> VDD[VDD]
    DRAIN --> COUT[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    COUT --> VOUT[Inverted Output Vout]
    SOURCE[Source of M1] --> GND[AC Ground]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The input $V_{in}$ passes through the AC coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ to the gate of $M_1$. The transistor converts the gate voltage to a drain current via $g_m$, which then develops an inverted voltage across $R_D$. The output is taken through $C_{out}$ to block the DC bias.

---

### 4.3 Current-Mirror Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    IREF[Reference Current IREF] --> M1[M1 Diode-Connected NMOS]
    M1 --> VGS[Shared VGS at Gate]
    VGS --> M2[M2 Mirror NMOS]
    M2 --> IOUT[Output Current IOUT]
    VGS -.feedback.-> M1
    IREF --> RREF[Resistor RREF from VDD]
    RREF --> M1
    M2 --> LOAD[Downstream Load Circuit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The reference current $I_{REF}$ flowing through the diode-connected $M_1$ develops a specific $V_{GS}$. This $V_{GS}$ is fed to the gate of $M_2$, which then *mirrors* the same current (scaled by $W/L$ ratio) into the output branch. The mirror is the **bias backbone** of every analog IC.

---

### 4.4 Frequency-Response Bode-Plot Schematic (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    F0[DC Gain A0] --> F1[Corner Frequency f3dB]
    F1 --> F2[Slope Minus 20 dB per decade]
    F2 --> F3[Unity-Gain Frequency fT]
    F3 --> F4[Slope Minus 40 dB per decade]
    F4 --> F5[Phase Margin Check at fT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The CS amplifier exhibits a **single-pole low-pass** response. At low frequencies the gain is the DC value $A_0 = g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$. At $f_{-3dB}$ the response rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade. Stability is verified by checking that the phase margin at the unity-gain crossover $f_T$ is greater than **45°–60°** (a key KTU design criterion for op-amp compensation).

---

### 4.5 Comparative Topology Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    BASIC[Basic Single-Stage MOS Circuits] --> CS[Common Source CS]
    BASIC --> CG[Common Gate CG]
    BASIC --> CD[Source Follower CD]
    BASIC --> CM[Current Mirror]
    CS --> CSUSE[Inverting Voltage Gain Stage]
    CG --> CGUSE[Current Buffer or Cascode Input]
    CD --> CDUSE[Unity Gain Output Buffer]
    CM --> CMUSE1[Bias Current Generator]
    CM --> CMUSE2[Active Load for High Gain]
    CS --> CASCODE[Cascode Combination]
    CG --> CASCODE
    CASCODE --> OPAMP[Operational Amplifier Core]
    OPAMP --> MS[Mixed Signal Subsystem]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Common Source MOSFET amplifier. Derive the expression for its small-signal voltage gain.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Common Source (CS) amplifier is a single-stage MOSFET amplifier in which the input signal is applied at the **gate**, the output is taken at the **drain**, and the **source is at AC ground**. The small-signal equivalent circuit replaces the MOSFET with a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$. Since no gate current flows, $v_{gs} = v_{in}$, and the output voltage is $v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} (R_D \Vert r_o)$. Hence the small-signal voltage gain is:

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output. The input resistance is ideally infinite (gate is insulated) and the output resistance is $R_D \Vert r_o$.

*(Rubric: 1 mark circuit diagram, 1 mark small-signal model, 1 mark gain expression with phase.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*List any three differences between Common Source and Common Drain MOSFET amplifiers. Mention one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Common Source (CS) | Common Drain (Source Follower) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------------|
| Voltage gain | High, $A_v = -g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ | Near unity, $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ |
| Phase | 180° (inverting) | 0° (non-inverting) |
| Output resistance | $R_D \Vert r_o$ (high) | $\approx 1/g_m$ (low) |

**Applications:** CS — used as the **input gain stage** of op-amps and LNAs. CD — used as an **output buffer** to drive low-impedance loads and as a **level shifter**.

*(Rubric: 1 mark per correct difference, ½ mark per correct application.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *(Attempt any ONE from A or B)*

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the small-signal operation of a **Common Source amplifier with source degeneration resistor $R_S$**. Derive the exact expression for the voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A Common Source amplifier is designed with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 20$, $I_D = 0.5\text{ mA}$, $R_D = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 200\,\Omega$, and $\lambda = 0.02\text{ V}^{-1}$. Calculate **(i)** $g_m$, **(ii)** $r_o$, and **(iii)** the small-signal voltage gain.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The CS amplifier with source degeneration $R_S$ is shown below (conceptual). The degeneration resistor provides **negative feedback** that stabilizes the bias point and improves linearity at the cost of reduced gain.

**Small-signal derivation:**

1. Apply KCL at the source node: $v_s / R_S = g_m v_{gs} + v_s / r_o$.
2. Substitute $v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_s$ and solve for $v_s$.
3. The drain current is $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$, and $v_{out} = -i_d (R_D \Vert r_o)$.

After algebraic simplification (full derivation given in Section 3.1 of these notes):

$$\boxed{\,A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -\frac{g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S + \dfrac{R_S}{r_o}}\,}$$

**Input resistance** $R_{in} = \infty$ (MOS gate is insulated).

**Output resistance** $R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$ (independent of $R_S$ because $R_S$ is not in the output loop).

**Effect of $R_S$:** Source degeneration *linearizes* the amplifier. As $R_S$ increases, the gain becomes less sensitive to $g_m$ variations caused by temperature or process spread — a critical advantage in **mass-produced** analog ICs.

*(Rubric: 2 marks sketch, 3 marks derivation, 1 mark $R_{in}$, 1 mark $R_{out}$.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}\text{ A/V}^2$
- $W/L = 20$
- $I_D = 0.5\text{ mA} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}$
- $R_D = 5\text{ k}\Omega = 5 \times 10^{3}\,\Omega$
- $R_S = 200\,\Omega$
- $\lambda = 0.02\text{ V}^{-1}$

**Step-1: Calculate $g_m$.**

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$= \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.414 \times 10^{-3}\text{ S} \;\approx\; 1.414\text{ mA/V}$$

**Step-2: Calculate $r_o$.**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{10^{-5}} = 10^{5}\,\Omega = 100\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step-3: Calculate the voltage gain using the exact degenerated formula.**

$$A_v = -\frac{g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S + \dfrac{R_S}{r_o}}$$

Compute $R_D \Vert r_o$:

$$R_D \Vert r_o = \frac{5\text{ k}\Omega \times 100\text{ k}\Omega}{5\text{ k}\Omega + 100\text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{500}{105}\text{ k}\Omega \approx 4.762\text{ k}\Omega$$

Compute the denominator:

$$1 + g_m R_S + \frac{R_S}{r_o} = 1 + (1.414 \times 10^{-3})(200) + \frac{200}{10^{5}}$$

$$= 1 + 0.2828 + 0.002 = 1.2848$$

Compute numerator:

$$g_m (R_D \Vert r_o) = (1.414 \times 10^{-3})(4.762 \times 10^{3}) = 6.733$$

Therefore:

$$A_v = -\frac{6.733}{1.2848} \approx -5.24\text{ V/V}$$

**Verification — without degeneration:**

$$A_{v,no\;RS} = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o) = -6.733\text{ V/V}$$

The degeneration reduced the gain by a factor of $1.2848$ as expected. The magnitude is reduced, but the **linearity is improved**.

*(Rubric: 1 mark $g_m$, 1 mark $r_o$, 3 marks gain formula substitution, 2 marks final numerical answer with units.)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the operation of a **MOS current mirror** with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the output current and output resistance.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **1:2 PMOS current mirror** (mirror ratio = 2) using $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $\lambda = 0.025\text{ V}^{-1}$, with reference current $I_{REF} = 100 \,\mu\text{A}$ and overdrive voltage $V_{OV} = 0.4\text{ V}$. Find the required $(W/L)$ ratio and the output resistance.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

A MOS current mirror consists of two matched MOSFETs $M_1$ and $M_2$ whose gates and sources are tied together. $M_1$ is *diode-connected* (gate tied to drain), forcing it to operate in saturation. The current $I_{REF}$ flowing into $M_1$'s drain sets up a specific $V_{GS}$. Since $M_2$ shares the same $V_{GS}$, it produces a drain current determined by its $W/L$ ratio.

**Current equation:**

By the square-law in saturation:

$$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

$$I_{out} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,I_{out} = I_{REF} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}\,}$$

**Output resistance derivation** (full derivation in Section 3.2):

$$\boxed{\,R_{out} = r_{o2} + g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1} + r_{o1} \;\approx\; g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}\,}$$

The mirror's output resistance is **enhanced by a factor of $g_m r_o$** compared to a single transistor — a key reason mirrors are used as **active loads** in op-amp design.

*(Rubric: 2 marks diagram, 2 marks current relation, 2 marks output resistance derivation, 1 mark conclusion on active load usage.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** PMOS current mirror, mirror ratio $I_{out}/I_{REF} = 2$, $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $\lambda = 0.025\text{ V}^{-1}$, $I_{REF} = 100\,\mu\text{A}$, $V_{OV} = 0.4\text{ V}$.

**Step-1: Find $(W/L)_1$ for the reference device.**

The PMOS current equation in saturation:

$$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 V_{OV}^2$$

$$100 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2}(50 \times 10^{-6})\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (0.4)^2$$

$$100 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{1}{2}(50 \times 10^{-6})(0.16)\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1$$

$$100 \times 10^{-6} = (4 \times 10^{-6})\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1$$

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 25$$

**Step-2: For mirror ratio = 2, scale up $(W/L)_2$.**

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 = 2 \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 = 2 \times 25 = 50$$

**Step-3: Calculate $g_{m2}$ of the mirror transistor.**

$$g_{m2} = \frac{2 I_{out}}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times (2 \times 100\,\mu\text{A})}{0.4\text{ V}} = \frac{400\,\mu\text{A}}{0.4\text{ V}} = 1\text{ mA/V}$$

**Step-4: Calculate $r_{o2}$.**

$$r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{out}} = \frac{1}{0.025 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\Omega = 200\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step-5: Calculate $R_{out}$ (using the approximation).**

$$R_{out} \approx g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}$$

Assuming $r_{o1} \approx r_{o2} = 200\text{ k}\Omega$ (since $I_{REF}$ and $I_{out}$ differ by only 2×):

$$R_{out} \approx (1 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^{5})^2 = (10^{-3})(4 \times 10^{10}) = 4 \times 10^{7}\,\Omega = 40\text{ M}\Omega$$

**Step-6: Verify gain enhancement.**

The intrinsic gain of a single transistor is $g_m r_o = (10^{-3})(2 \times 10^5) = 200\text{ V/V}$. The mirror's effective output resistance is $g_m r_o \cdot r_o = 200 \times 200\text{ k}\Omega = 40\text{ M}\Omega$ ✓.

*(Rubric: 2 marks $W/L$ ratio, 2 marks $g_m$, 1 mark $r_o$, 2 marks final $R_{out}$ with unit.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in CS Gain:** Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v = -g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ will cost you **2 out of 3 marks** instantly. KTU strictly marks the phase.
> 2. **Body-Effect Neglect in Source Follower:** When the source-bulk of a source follower is *not* tied to the source, $g_{mb}$ must be included. The correct gain becomes $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + (g_m + g_{mb}) R_S}$. Missing $g_{mb}$ is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Saturation Assumption:** If the problem says $V_{DS}$ is *not* given or the device is *triode*, the square-law does not apply. Always verify the saturation condition **first**.
> 4. **Mirror Ratio Mismatch:** Forgetting to scale $(W/L)$ when the mirror ratio is *not* 1:1 — KTU loves to test scaling.
> 5. **Output Resistance of Source Follower:** Students often write $R_{out} = 1/g_m$ without the $\Vert r_o$. The correct answer is $R_{out} = (1/g_m) \Vert r_o$.
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $\text{mA}$ and $\mu\text{A}$, or writing $g_m$ in $\text{A/V}$ vs $\text{mA/V}$ — KTU deducts ½ mark per inconsistency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read This 10 Minutes Before the Exam**

- ✅ The **Common Source (CS)** stage is an **inverting** voltage amplifier with **infinite input resistance** and gain $-g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$.
- ✅ The **Common Gate (CG)** stage is a **non-inverting** current-input amplifier with **low input resistance** $1/g_m$ and **no Miller effect** — hence excellent for high frequency.
- ✅ The **Source Follower (CD)** is a **unity-gain buffer** with very high $R_{in}$ and very low $R_{out} \approx 1/g_m$.
- ✅ The **Current Mirror** copies a reference current using shared $V_{GS}$ — ratio set by $(W/L)$ scaling.
- ✅ Current mirrors as **active loads** boost op-amp gain by a factor of $\sim g_m r_o$ (often 100–1000).
- ✅ **Source degeneration** reduces gain by the factor $(1 + g_m R_S)$ but improves **linearity** and **PSRR**.
- ✅ **Miller effect** on $C_{gd}$ is the dominant speed limiter in CS stages — multiplied by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$.
- ✅ Intrinsic transistor gain ceiling: $g_m r_o = \dfrac{2}{\lambda V_{OV}}$.
- ✅ All formulas assume **saturation region** operation.
- ✅ KTU's most-tested single fact: **CS inverts (180°), CG and CD are non-inverting (0°).**

---

**End of Notes — PEECT754 | Module 2 | Basic Circuit**

topics.title: PMOS and NMOS Current Mirrors, Simple and Cascode Current Mirror Circuits, CMOS Differential Amplifier: Differential Amplifier with Resistive Current Source and Current Mirror Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PMOS and NMOS Current Mirrors, Simple and Cascode Current Mirror Circuits, CMOS Differential Amplifier: Differential Amplifier with Resistive Current Source and Current Mirror Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Current Mirror — Foundational Building Block

A **Current Mirror (CM)** is a fundamental analog integrated circuit building block whose function is to copy (mirror) a reference current flowing through one branch of a circuit into one or more output branches, maintaining a precise ratio regardless of load conditions. In CMOS technology, a current mirror is constructed using matched transistor pairs (NMOS or PMOS) operating in the **saturation region**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A current mirror is a circuit that produces a stable bias current independent of power supply variations and temperature, by replicating a reference current using matched active devices. It is the *de facto* biasing element in virtually all analog and mixed-signal ICs (op-amps, ADC/DAC, references, PLLs).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Plumbing" View of a Current Mirror

Think of a current mirror as a **hydraulic analogy**: imagine two identical water tanks connected at the bottom through a perfectly matched pipe system. If you pour water at a controlled rate into tank A (the reference), the identical geometry causes an equal (or scaled) flow into tank B (the mirror output). 

- The **input current $I_{REF}$** is like water flowing into the reference tank.
- The **diode-connected transistor** sets the "water level" — its $V_{GS}$ is automatically set by $I_{REF}$.
- The **output transistor** simply experiences the same $V_{GS}$ (because gates and sources are tied together) and produces a proportional "drain current."

Just as a hydraulic system needs matched pipes to ensure equal flow, a current mirror needs **matched transistors** (same $W/L$, same $V_T$, same process parameters) to ensure accurate current replication. Mismatches between transistors produce a systematic error known as the **mismatch ratio** $\Delta I / I$.

### 1.2 NMOS vs. PMOS Current Mirrors — Polarity Matters

| Mirror Type | Transistor Polarity | Current Direction (Conventional) | Typical Use |
|-------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|-------------|
| **NMOS CM** | n-channel MOSFET | Sinks current to ground | Source biasing, active load pull-down |
| **PMOS CM** | p-channel MOSFET | Sources current from $V_{DD}$ | Source biasing, active load pull-up |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Notation Throughout These Notes:**
> - $I_{REF}$ = Reference (input) current — the master current we set externally.
> - $I_O$ or $I_{OUT}$ = Output (mirrored) current — the slave current.
> - $K = \dfrac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$ = Current scaling (aspect ratio) factor.

---

## 1.3 CMOS Differential Amplifier — Why Differential?

A **differential amplifier (diff-amp)** amplifies the *difference* between two input signals $V_1$ and $V_2$, while *rejecting* any signal common to both inputs (common-mode signals). This makes it immune to power supply noise, environmental interference, and process variations — critical for mixed-signal systems like ADCs, op-amps, and comparators.

The two variants covered in this module:

1. **Diff-Amp with Resistive (Passive) Current Source Load** — uses a resistor as the load. Simple but limited gain.
2. **Diff-Amp with Current Mirror (Active) Load** — replaces the resistor with a current mirror, dramatically boosting the differential gain without needing high supply voltage.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** A differential amplifier is like a **seesaw balance**. If both children are of equal weight (common-mode signal), the seesaw stays level (zero output). If one is heavier than the other (differential signal), the seesaw tilts — the bigger the weight difference, the bigger the tilt. The output is proportional to the *weight difference*, not the absolute weights.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Simple NMOS Current Mirror — Circuit Operation

### Topology
The simple (basic) NMOS current mirror consists of two NMOS transistors:
- **M1 (Diode-connected)**: Gate tied to drain → always operates in **saturation**.
- **M2 (Output)**: Gate connected to M1's gate, source connected to M1's source.

Both transistors share the same $V_{GS}$ and (ideally) the same $V_{DS}$ when properly biased. A reference current $I_{REF}$ is forced through M1, and $I_{OUT}$ flows out of M2.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Reference Side (M1):** Since M1 is diode-connected ($V_{GS} = V_{DS}$), it is guaranteed to operate in **saturation** for $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_{TN}$. The $I_{REF}$ flowing through M1 establishes the gate-source voltage $V_{GS1}$.

2. **Mirror Side (M2):** M2's gate is tied to M1's gate and both sources are grounded (for NMOS), so $V_{GS2} = V_{GS1}$. If M2 is also in saturation, its drain current is determined solely by $V_{GS2}$ and its geometry.

3. **Current Replication:** Since both transistors have the same $V_{GS}$ and (ideally) the same $V_T$ and process parameters, their drain currents scale directly with their aspect ratios $(W/L)$.

### 2.2 Ideal Current Transfer Ratio

Using the standard square-law MOSFET equation in saturation:
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2$$

Setting $V_{GS1} = V_{GS2}$ and $V_{T1} = V_{T2} = V_{TN}$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \dfrac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}}$$

If the two transistors are identical $(W/L)_1 = (W/L)_2$, then $I_{OUT} = I_{REF}$ — a perfect mirror.

---

## 2.3 Non-Ideal Effects (KTU Favourite!)

### (a) Channel Length Modulation
In reality, $I_D$ also depends on $V_{DS}$. The **Early-effect-influenced** output current is:
$$I_{OUT} = \dfrac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \dfrac{(1 + \lambda V_{DS2})}{(1 + \lambda V_{DS1})} \cdot I_{REF}$$

When $V_{DS2} \ne V_{DS1}$, a systematic error $\propto \lambda$ appears. This degrades the **output resistance** $r_o$.

### (b) Threshold Voltage Mismatch
If $V_{T1} \ne V_{T2}$, the mirror ratio deviates even with matched $W/L$. This is critical in nanometer CMOS where $V_T$ can vary by several mV across a die.

### (c) Finite Output Resistance
The **Small-Signal Output Resistance** of the simple mirror is:
$$r_{out} = r_{o2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_{OUT}}$$

Low $r_{out}$ → poor current source quality → undesirable in high-gain amplifier design.

---

## 2.4 PMOS Current Mirror — The Complementary Mirror

A PMOS mirror mirrors currents from $V_{DD}$ to the load (sources current). Its topology is the **mirror image** of the NMOS version:

- Both PMOS transistors have sources tied to $V_{DD}$.
- M1 is diode-connected ($V_{SG1} = V_{SD1}$).
- $V_{SG2} = V_{SG1}$ enforces current replication.

**Ideal ratio** (analogous to NMOS):
$$\dfrac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \dfrac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \dfrac{(1 + \vert\lambda_p\vert V_{SD2})}{(1 + \vert\lambda_p\vert V_{SD1})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> PMOS mirrors are commonly used as **active loads** in CMOS op-amps because they pull up to $V_{DD}$ and pair naturally with NMOS diff-pair input stages.

---

## 2.5 Cascode Current Mirror — Boosting Output Resistance

### Motivation
The simple mirror's output resistance $r_{o2}$ is modest (typically 10–50 kΩ). For high-performance analog circuits (e.g., op-amp gain stages), we need $r_{out}$ in the **MΩ range**. The **cascode mirror** achieves this by stacking an extra transistor on top of M2.

### Topology
- M1 (diode-connected) sets $V_{GS1}$.
- M2 is the **mirroring** transistor.
- M4 is the **cascode** transistor on the output side, biased by a constant $V_{BIAS}$.
- M3 (diode-connected cascode) biases M4 with the same $V_{GS}$.

The bias is chosen such that $V_{DS2} = V_{GS4} - V_{BIAS} \approx V_{OV}$ (overdrive), keeping M2 in saturation.

### Output Resistance of the Cascode Mirror

By the small-signal analysis of the cascode configuration:
$$\boxed{r_{out,cascode} \approx g_{m2}\, r_{o2}\, r_{o4}}$$

This is a **factor of $g_{m2} r_{o2}$ (the intrinsic gain) higher** than the simple mirror.

### Trade-offs
| Parameter | Simple Mirror | Cascode Mirror |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| $r_{out}$ | $\approx r_o$ | $\approx g_m r_o^2$ |
| Voltage Headroom | Excellent (only $V_{OV}$ across M1+M2) | Poor (needs $2 V_{OV} + 2 V_{DS,sat}$) |
| Bandwidth | Wide | Narrower (more poles) |
| Complexity | 2 transistors | 4 transistors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The cascode mirror requires an **auxiliary bias voltage $V_{BIAS}$** to keep all transistors in saturation. In modern designs, the *wide-swing cascode* or *high-swing cascode* is used to minimize headroom loss.

---

## 2.6 CMOS Differential Amplifier with Resistive Load

### Topology
- **M1, M2:** NMOS differential input pair with common source.
- **Tail Current Source $I_{SS}$:** Sets the bias point; shared by both branches.
- **Load resistors $R_D$:** Convert drain current variations into output voltages at $V_{O1}$ and $V_{O2}$.

### Differential-Mode (DM) Analysis
For a purely differential input $V_{id} = V_1 - V_2$:
- $V_{GS1} = V_{GS,CM} + V_{id}/2$
- $V_{GS2} = V_{GS,CM} - V_{id}/2$
- The current $\Delta i_D = g_m V_{id}/2$ is steered from one branch to the other.

The **differential voltage gain** is:
$$A_{vd} = \dfrac{v_{od}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_D$$

(where $v_{od} = v_{o1} - v_{o2}$ and $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$).

### Common-Mode (CM) Analysis
For $v_{ic} = v_1 = v_2$, both transistors experience the same $V_{GS}$, so ideally no differential output appears. The finite output resistance of the tail current source $R_{SS}$ creates a nonzero gain:
$$A_{vc} = -\dfrac{R_D}{2 R_{SS}}$$

### Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)
$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left| \dfrac{A_{vd}}{A_{vc}} \right| = 20 \log_{10} \left| g_m R_{SS} \right|$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher tail current source impedance $R_{SS}$ = better CMRR = better rejection of supply noise and common-mode interference. This is why current mirrors (with very high $r_o$) are preferred over resistive tails.

---

## 2.7 CMOS Differential Amplifier with Current Mirror Load (Active Load)

### Topology
- **M1, M2:** NMOS differential pair (same as before).
- **M3, M4:** PMOS current mirror used as the **active load**.
- The PMOS mirror takes the differential current from M1/M2 and recombines it, doubling the effective transconductance at the output node.

### Why Active Loads?
A resistive load of value $R_D$ requires a large DC voltage drop $I_D R_D$ which **wastes headroom**. Replacing $R_D$ with a current mirror (PMOS) achieves:
- Very high small-signal load impedance ($\approx r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$).
- No DC headroom penalty.

### Differential Gain with Active Load
$$\boxed{A_{vd} = -g_{mN}\,(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})}$$

Compare to the resistive load case: $A_{vd} = -g_m R_D$.

Since $r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$ can easily reach tens of kΩ to MΩ (without consuming DC voltage), the active-load diff-amp achieves **huge voltage gain** — this is the heart of every CMOS op-amp.

### Why "Doubled Transconductance"?
Because M1's drain current flows through M3 (PMOS load), and M2's drain current flows through M4 (PMOS mirror), the currents from both branches are effectively subtracted at the output node. The differential signal sees an effective $g_m$ that is doubled compared to a single-ended output. Hence the result $A_{vd} = -g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ already incorporates this doubling (it's the differential output, not single-ended).

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bookmark This Table — These Are The Equations Most Tested In KTU Exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | MOSFET $I_D$ (saturation) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_T)^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | $\mu C_{ox}$ in $\mu$A/V² |
| 2 | Mirror transfer ratio (ideal) | $\dfrac{I_O}{I_{REF}} = \dfrac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Output resistance (simple) | $r_{out} = r_{o2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_O}$ | Ohms (Ω) |
| 4 | Output resistance (cascode) | $r_{out} \approx g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o4}$ | Boosted by intrinsic gain |
| 5 | Transconductance $g_m$ | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D} = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ | Siemens (S) |
| 6 | Diff-amp DM gain (resistive) | $A_{vd} = -g_m R_D$ | V/V |
| 7 | Diff-amp CM gain (resistive tail) | $A_{vc} = -\dfrac{R_D}{2 R_{SS}}$ | V/V |
| 8 | CMRR (resistive load) | $\text{CMRR} = g_m R_{SS}$ | Dimensionless (use dB) |
| 9 | Diff-amp DM gain (active load) | $A_{vd} = -g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ | V/V |
| 10 | Overdrive Voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 I_D}{\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)}}$ | Volts |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
Current mirrors and differential amplifiers are the **backbone of every analog IC**. They appear in:
- Operational amplifiers (input diff-pair + active-load current mirrors)
- Bandgap voltage references
- ADCs and DACs (comparators, sample-and-hold)
- Voltage regulators (LDO error amplifiers)
- RF transceivers (mixers, LNAs)
- PLL charge pumps

Without current mirrors, no modern mixed-signal IC could achieve precision, low-power operation, or high gain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ideal Current Transfer Ratio (NMOS Simple Mirror)

**Given:** Two matched NMOS transistors M1 (diode-connected) and M2, with sources grounded. A reference current $I_{REF}$ flows into M1's drain. M2's gate is tied to M1's gate.

**Find:** The relationship between $I_{OUT} = I_{D2}$ and $I_{REF} = I_{D1}$.

### Step 1 — Write $I_D$ for M1 (Diode-Connected)

For M1 in saturation (guaranteed by diode connection):
$$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS1})$$

### Step 2 — Write $I_D$ for M2 (Mirror Device)

For M2 in saturation:
$$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS2} - V_{TN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS2})$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Matching Condition

Because gates are tied together and sources are tied together:
$$V_{GS2} = V_{GS1}$$

Substituting:
$$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS1} - V_{TN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS2})$$

### Step 4 — Take the Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} &= \dfrac{ \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS1} - V_{TN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS2}) }{ \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS1}) } \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \lambda V_{DS2}}{1 + \lambda V_{DS1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Ideal Approximation

If we ignore channel-length modulation ($\lambda \to 0$) and assume matched $V_T$:
$$\boxed{\dfrac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \dfrac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}}$$

For $W/L$ identical in both transistors, $I_{OUT} = I_{REF}$ — perfect mirroring.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Small-Signal Output Resistance of the Simple Mirror

**Given:** A simple NMOS mirror with M2 as the output device. Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output node and find $i_x$ injected; then $r_{out} = v_x / i_x$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Small-Signal Model

M1 is diode-connected, so its small-signal model is a resistor of value $1/g_{m1} \parallel r_{o1}$. M2 contributes its $r_{o2}$.

Because the gate of M2 is at AC ground through M1's gate-source, and M2's source is at AC ground, M2 sees $v_{gs2} = 0$. Hence $g_{m2} v_{gs2} = 0$ — **no controlled source is activated**.

### Step 2 — Apply Test Voltage

Setting $v_x$ at the drain of M2:
$$i_x = \dfrac{v_x}{r_{o2}}$$

(No current path exists through $g_{m2}$ because $v_{gs2} = 0$.)

### Step 3 — Compute Output Resistance

$$\boxed{r_{out} = \dfrac{v_x}{i_x} = r_{o2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_{OUT}}}$$

This is the well-known result: the simple mirror's output resistance equals $r_{o2}$ alone.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cascode Mirror Output Resistance

**Given:** A cascode mirror with M2 (mirror device) and M4 (cascode device) stacked. M4's gate is biased by $V_{BIAS}$ (constant). Apply $v_x$ at the drain of M4.

### Step 1 — Small-Signal Circuit

M2 is diode-connected → its small-signal model is $r_{o2} \parallel (1/g_{m2})$ looking into M2's drain. M4's source is connected to M2's drain, so M4 sees this impedance as its source degeneration.

The test voltage $v_x$ appears at M4's drain. M4 is a **common-gate** stage with source resistance $R_S = r_{o2} \parallel (1/g_{m2}) \approx 1/g_{m2}$ (if $r_{o2} \gg 1/g_{m2}$).

### Step 2 — Common-Gate Output Resistance Formula

For a common-gate transistor with source resistance $R_S$:
$$r_{out,CG} \approx r_{o4} (1 + g_{m4} R_S)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $R_S \approx r_{o2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{out,cascode} &\approx r_{o4} \left(1 + g_{m4}\, r_{o2}\right) \\[4pt]
&\approx r_{o4}\, g_{m4}\, r_{o2} \quad \text{(since } g_m r_o \gg 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{r_{out,cascode} \approx g_{m}\, r_{o}^2}$$

Typically enhanced by a factor of **100× to 1000×** compared to the simple mirror.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Differential Gain of Diff-Amp with Resistive Load

**Given:** CMOS diff-pair M1/M2 with tail current $I_{SS}$ and drain resistors $R_D$. Differential input $v_{id} = v_1 - v_2$.

### Step 1 — Differential Half-Circuit

By symmetry, the differential-mode half-circuit is M1 alone with source grounded (virtual ground at the common source node for DM signals). The drain load is $R_D$.

### Step 2 — Small-Signal Analysis

Apply $v_{id}/2$ at M1's gate. The drain current of M1:
$$i_{d1} = g_{m1} \cdot \dfrac{v_{id}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Output Voltage

The output at M1's drain:
$$v_{o1} = -i_{d1}\, R_D = -g_{m1} R_D \cdot \dfrac{v_{id}}{2}$$

By symmetry, $v_{o2} = +g_{m2} R_D \cdot v_{id}/2$.

### Step 4 — Differential Output

$$v_{od} = v_{o1} - v_{o2} = -g_m R_D\, v_{id}$$

### Step 5 — Differential Gain

$$\boxed{A_{vd} = \dfrac{v_{od}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_D}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Differential Gain of Diff-Amp with Active (Current Mirror) Load

**Given:** Diff-pair M1 (NMOS) and M2 (NMOS) with PMOS current-mirror load M3/M4.

### Step 1 — Recognize the Current Mirror Operation

The PMOS mirror M3/M4 forces $I_{D4} = I_{D3} = I_{D1}$ (matched PMOS, ideal mirror).

The total output current at the output node is the *difference*:
$$i_{out} = I_{D4} - I_{D2} = I_{D1} - I_{D2}$$

### Step 2 — Express Currents in Terms of Differential Input

For differential input $v_{id}$:
$$I_{D1} = \dfrac{I_{SS}}{2} + g_m \cdot \dfrac{v_{id}}{2}, \qquad I_{D2} = \dfrac{I_{SS}}{2} - g_m \cdot \dfrac{v_{id}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Output Current

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{out} &= \left(\dfrac{I_{SS}}{2} + g_m \dfrac{v_{id}}{2}\right) - \left(\dfrac{I_{SS}}{2} - g_m \dfrac{v_{id}}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
&= g_m\, v_{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Output Voltage with Load Impedance

The load impedance at the output node is $R_L = r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$ (NMOS M2 in parallel with PMOS M4).

$$v_{out} = -i_{out} \cdot (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}) = -g_m\, (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})\, v_{id}$$

### Step 5 — Final Gain Expression

$$\boxed{A_{vd} = -g_m\,(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})}$$

This gain is *intrinsically very high* (often 1000–10000 V/V) because $r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$ can be MΩ without consuming voltage headroom.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Simple NMOS CM | Cascode NMOS CM | PMOS CM | Diff-Amp Resistive | Diff-Amp Active Load |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| # Transistors | 2 | 4 | 2 (PMOS) | 2 + 2 R | 2 NMOS + 2 PMOS |
| $r_{out}$ | $r_o$ | $g_m r_o^2$ | $r_o$ | — | — |
| $A_{vd}$ | — | — | — | $g_m R_D$ | $g_m(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ |
| Headroom | Best | Worst | Best | Moderate | Moderate |
| Power | Lowest | Higher | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate |
| Use Case | Biasing | High-gain stages | Pull-up loads | Basic amp | Op-amp input stage |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU:** When a question asks "why use active load?", the answer always involves *gain boost without headroom penalty* and *better CMRR*.
