# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Simple NMOS Current Mirror

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[VDD Supply] --> nodeB[IREF Source via Resistor R]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Drain of M1 Diode Connected]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Gate tied to Drain M1]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Gate of M2 Mirror Transistor]
    nodeF[Source M1 Ground] --> nodeG[Source M2 Ground]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Drain of M2 Output IOUT]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Load Circuit External]
```

**Signal Flow:**
1. $I_{REF}$ is forced into M1's drain from a current source or resistor from $V_{DD}$.
2. M1's diode connection sets $V_{GS1} = V_{DS1}$.
3. M2's gate copies this voltage, forcing $I_{OUT} = K \cdot I_{REF}$ (with $K = (W/L)_2/(W/L)_1$).
4. Output node delivers $I_{OUT}$ to the external load.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Cascode Current Mirror

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[VDD] --> nodeB[IREF Injected at Top Node]
    nodeB --> nodeC[M1 Diode Connected NMOS]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Common Gate Node VG]
    nodeD --> nodeE[M3 Diode Connected Cascode]
    nodeE --> nodeF[VBIAS Reference]
    nodeG[M2 Mirror NMOS] --> nodeD
    nodeH[M4 Cascode NMOS] --> nodeF
    nodeI[Drain M4 Output IOUT] --> nodeJ[High Impedance Load]
```

**Key Concept:** M3/M4 are cascode devices biased by $V_{BIAS}$. The signal path is: $V_{DD} \to M1 \to M3 \to$ ground (reference); output $M2 \to M4 \to$ load. The stacked cascode transistors multiply the output impedance by $\approx g_m r_o$.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: PMOS Current Mirror (Source-Current Mirror)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Ground GND] --> nodeB[IREF Sunk via Resistor]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Drain M1 PMOS Diode Connected]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Gate of M1 tied to Drain]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Gate of M2 PMOS Mirror]
    nodeF[Source M1 VDD] --> nodeG[Source M2 VDD]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Drain of M2 IOUT]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Load to Ground]
```

**Note:** PMOS sources are at $V_{DD}$, drains are at the output node. Current *flows from $V_{DD}$ to load*.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Differential Amplifier with Resistive Load

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Input V1] --> nodeB[Gate M1 NMOS]
    nodeC[Input V2] --> nodeD[Gate M2 NMOS]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Drain M1 to RD1 to VDD]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Drain M2 to RD2 to VDD]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Output VO1]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Output VO2]
    nodeI[Tail Current Source ISS] --> nodeJ[Common Source Node M1 and M2]
```

**Functional Architecture:**
- M1/M2 form the differential pair.
- $R_{D1}/R_{D2}$ are drain load resistors (passive).
- $I_{SS}$ tail biases the pair; sets DC operating point.
- Differential output $V_{od} = V_{O1} - V_{O2} = -g_m R_D \cdot V_{id}$.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Differential Amplifier with Current Mirror (Active) Load

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[V1 Input] --> nodeB[Gate M1 NMOS Diff Pair]
    nodeC[V2 Input] --> nodeD[Gate M2 NMOS Diff Pair]
    nodeE[Tail ISS] --> nodeF[Common Source Node]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Drain M1 to Drain M3 PMOS Load]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Drain M2 to Drain M4 PMOS Load]
    nodeI[VDD] --> nodeJ[Sources M3 and M4 PMOS]
    nodeK[M3 and M4 Form Current Mirror Active Load]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Single Ended Output VOUT]
```

**Functional Flow:**
1. Inputs $V_1$, $V_2$ drive the differential pair.
2. M1/M2 steer the tail current differentially.
3. PMOS mirror M3/M4 senses M1's drain current and replicates it at the output node, where it subtracts from M2's drain current.
4. Net output current = $g_m \cdot V_{id}$, developed across $r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$.
5. Gain = $-g_m(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ — very high.

---

## 4.6 Conceptual Block: Why Active Load Beats Resistive Load

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Diff Pair] --> nodeB{Load Type?}
    nodeB --> nodeC[Resistive RD]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Active Current Mirror]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Gain g_m RD Limited by DC Drop ID RD]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Gain g_m roN Parallel roP Very High Zero DC Drop]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Used in Low Gain Stages]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Used in OpAmp Input Stages]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Active loads deliver *high gain at low supply voltage* — the cornerstone of low-power analog IC design (e.g., 1.2V op-amps in wearable/biomedical SoCs).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define a current mirror. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a simple NMOS current mirror and derive the current transfer ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

A **current mirror** is a two-transistor circuit that replicates a reference current $I_{REF}$ at one or more output branches with a precise ratio, by exploiting matched device characteristics in saturation.

**Operation:** Transistor M1 is diode-connected (gate tied to drain), forcing it into saturation. The reference current $I_{REF}$ flows through M1 and establishes $V_{GS1}$. Transistor M2 has its gate tied to M1's gate and source to ground, so $V_{GS2} = V_{GS1}$. If M2 is also in saturation, its drain current is determined by the same $V_{GS}$ but scaled by its $W/L$ ratio.

**Derivation:**

Using the MOSFET saturation equation for M1 and M2:
$$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TN})^2$$
$$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS2} - V_{TN})^2$$

Since $V_{GS2} = V_{GS1}$, dividing:
$$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$$

For matched transistors, $I_{OUT} = I_{REF}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Compare the simple current mirror and cascode current mirror in terms of output resistance and voltage headroom.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Simple Current Mirror | Cascode Current Mirror |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Output resistance $r_{out}$ | $r_{o2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_{OUT}}$ | $\approx g_m\, r_{o2}\, r_{o4}$ |
| Voltage headroom | $\approx 2 V_{OV}$ | $\approx 2 V_{OV} + 2 V_{DS,sat}$ (worse) |
| Complexity | 2 transistors | 4 transistors |
| Use case | Basic biasing | High-gain stages |

The cascode mirror boosts $r_{out}$ by a factor of $g_m r_o$ (intrinsic gain), making it ideal where high output impedance is critical, but at the cost of reduced voltage headroom. $\blacksquare$

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **CMOS differential amplifier with resistive load**. Derive the expression for the **differential-mode voltage gain** and **common-mode voltage gain**. Also derive the expression for **CMRR**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Redesign the same differential amplifier using a **PMOS current mirror as an active load**. Draw the circuit and derive the expression for the differential voltage gain. Discuss why this configuration provides higher gain compared to the resistive load version. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution for Q-A(a):

**Circuit:** The CMOS diff-amp with resistive load has two NMOS transistors M1, M2 with common-source connection to a tail current source $I_{SS}$, and drain resistors $R_D$ connected to $V_{DD}$.

**Differential-Mode Analysis:**

For DM input $v_{id} = v_1 - v_2$ applied, the half-circuit has M1 with source at virtual ground. The small-signal drain current of M1 is:
$$i_{d1} = g_m \cdot \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$

The differential output:
$$v_{od} = v_{o1} - v_{o2} = -g_m R_D\, v_{id}$$

Therefore:
$$A_{vd} = -g_m R_D$$

**Common-Mode Analysis:**

For CM input $v_{ic}$ applied to both gates, both transistors carry equal current $\approx I_{SS}/2$. The output at each drain is:
$$v_{oc} = -g_m R_D\, v_{ic}/2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}\right) \approx -\frac{R_D}{2 R_{SS}}\, v_{ic}$$

(Using $g_m R_{SS} \gg 1$ for simplification.)

**CMRR:**
$$\text{CMRR} = \left|\frac{A_{vd}}{A_{vc}}\right| = \left|\frac{-g_m R_D}{-R_D/(2 R_{SS})}\right| = 2 g_m R_{SS}$$

In decibels: $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log(2 g_m R_{SS})$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Solution for Q-A(b):

**Active-Load Diff-Amp Circuit:** Replace $R_D$ resistors with a PMOS current mirror (M3 diode-connected at M1's drain; M4 mirror at M2's drain).

**Operation:** The PMOS mirror forces $I_{D4} = I_{D3} = I_{D1}$. The total output current is the difference between currents flowing into and out of the output node:
$$i_{out} = I_{D4} - I_{D2} = I_{D1} - I_{D2}$$

For DM input:
$$I_{D1} = \frac{I_{SS}}{2} + g_m \frac{v_{id}}{2}, \quad I_{D2} = \frac{I_{SS}}{2} - g_m \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$

Therefore $i_{out} = g_m\, v_{id}$.

The output voltage is developed across the parallel combination of M2's and M4's small-signal output resistances:
$$v_{out} = -g_m (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}) v_{id}$$

$$\boxed{A_{vd} = -g_m (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})}$$

**Why Higher Gain?**
- The PMOS current mirror replicates $I_{D1}$ with virtually no DC voltage drop (no headroom penalty), unlike a resistor.
- The effective load impedance $r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$ is typically tens of kΩ to MΩ — far larger than the $R_D$ achievable without consuming supply headroom.
- Hence $A_{vd}$ can reach **1000–10000 V/V**, ideal for op-amp input stages. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **cascode current mirror** with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for its output resistance and show why it is higher than a simple current mirror. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a **PMOS current mirror** to deliver a $200\,\mu\text{A}$ output current from a $100\,\mu\text{A}$ reference current. Given $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $V_{TP} = -0.7\text{V}$, and $(W/L)_1 = 10$, find $(W/L)_2$ and the required $V_{SG}$. Assume $\lambda = 0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution for Q-B(a):

**Circuit:** A cascode current mirror consists of M1 (diode-connected reference), M2 (mirror device), M3 (diode-connected cascode on the reference side), and M4 (cascode on the output side). M3/M4 share the same gate bias $V_{BIAS}$, set externally.

**Operation:** M1 sets $V_{GS1} \approx V_{OV1} + V_{TP}$ for the reference current $I_{REF}$. M3 (diode-connected) has its own $V_{GS3}$ chosen so that $V_{DS2} = V_{GS3} - V_{BIAS} \approx V_{OV2}$ — this keeps M2 in saturation. The cascode transistor M4 isolates the drain of M2 from the output node, dramatically boosting $r_{out}$.

**Derivation of $r_{out}$:** Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at M4's drain. M2 is diode-connected through M1, so its small-signal drain-to-source is approximately $1/g_{m2}$. M4 is configured as a common-gate stage with source degeneration $R_S \approx 1/g_{m2}$.

Output resistance of common-gate stage:
$$r_{out,cascode} = r_{o4}(1 + g_{m4} R_S) = r_{o4}(1 + g_{m4} \cdot 1/g_{m2})$$

Assuming matched $g_m$: $r_{out,cascode} \approx 2\, r_{o4}$. More rigorously, accounting for M2's $r_{o2}$:
$$\boxed{r_{out,cascode} \approx g_m\, r_{o2}\, r_{o4}}$$

**Comparison:**
- Simple mirror: $r_{out} = r_{o2}$
- Cascode mirror: $r_{out} \approx g_m r_o \cdot r_{o2}$ — boosted by intrinsic gain $g_m r_o$ (≈ 100–1000).

The cascode mirror achieves MΩ-level output impedance, essential for high-gain amplifier design. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Solution for Q-B(b):

**Given:**
- $I_{REF} = 100\,\mu\text{A}$
- $I_{OUT} = 200\,\mu\text{A}$
- $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$
- $V_{TP} = -0.7\text{V}$
- $(W/L)_1 = 10$, $\lambda = 0$.

**Step 1 — Find the required mirror ratio:**
$$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$$
$$\frac{200}{100} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{10}$$
$$\boxed{(W/L)_2 = 20}$$

**Step 2 — Find $V_{SG}$ from the MOSFET equation:**
$$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$$

$$100 = \frac{1}{2}(50)(10)(V_{SG} - 0.7)^2$$
$$100 = 250 (V_{SG} - 0.7)^2$$
$$(V_{SG} - 0.7)^2 = 0.4$$
$$V_{SG} - 0.7 = \sqrt{0.4} = 0.6325 \text{ V}$$
$$\boxed{V_{SG} = 1.3325 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**
$$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2}(50)(20)(1.3325 - 0.7)^2 = 500 \cdot (0.6325)^2 = 500 \cdot 0.4 = 200\,\mu\text{A} \checkmark$$

**Design Complete.** $\blacksquare$

---

## KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Body Effect (Back-Gate Effect):** When the source of M2 in a current mirror is NOT at ground (e.g., in a cascode), the threshold voltage $V_{TN}$ increases. Students often use a constant $V_T$, leading to **wrong $I_{OUT}$ calculations**. Always include $\gamma (\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
> 
> 2. **Mixing Up NMOS vs. PMOS Conventions:** For PMOS, the equation uses $V_{SG}$ and $\vert V_{TP} \vert$, NOT $V_{GS}$ and $V_{TP}$. This is the **#1 mistake** in PMOS problems.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring $\lambda$ When Asked:** If the question specifies $\lambda \ne 0$, you MUST include the $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ factor. KTU explicitly tests this.
> 
> 4. **Confusing $A_{vd}$ Formulas:** Resistive load: $A_{vd} = -g_m R_D$. Active load: $A_{vd} = -g_m (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$. These are NOT interchangeable.
> 
> 5. **Not Drawing the Tail Current Source:** In diff-amp circuit diagrams, the tail $I_{SS}$ must be shown. Omitting it loses **1 mark** immediately.
> 
> 6. **Single-Ended vs. Differential Output:** A common error is to confuse $v_{out}$ (single-ended) with $v_{od}$ (differential). The active-load diff-amp gives a single-ended output, but its magnitude equals the differential output due to current mirror action. Clarify which one your formula represents.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

### Current Mirrors
- [x] **Simple NMOS mirror:** $I_{OUT}/I_{REF} = (W/L)_2/(W/L)_1$, $r_{out} = r_{o2}$.
- [x] **PMOS mirror:** Use $V_{SG}$ and $\vert V_{TP} \vert$; sources at $V_{DD}$.
- [x] **Cascode mirror:** $r_{out} \approx g_m r_{o}^2$; needs auxiliary bias $V_{BIAS}$.
- [x] **Channel-length modulation** ($\lambda$) degrades accuracy unless $V_{DS}$ matched.
- [x] **Body effect** modifies $V_T$ when sources differ from bulk.
- [x] Current mirrors are used for **bias generation** and **active loads**.

### Differential Amplifiers
- [x] **DM gain (resistive load):** $A_{vd} = -g_m R_D$.
- [x] **CM gain (resistive load):** $A_{vc} = -R_D/(2 R_{SS})$.
- [x] **CMRR:** $\vert A_{vd}/A_{vc} \vert = 2 g_m R_{SS}$ (in dB).
- [x] **DM gain (active load):** $A_{vd} = -g_m (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ — very high.
- [x] Active load doubles effective $g_m$ for single-ended output.
- [x] Higher tail resistance → better CMRR (use current source/mirror, not resistor).

### Engineering Trade-offs to Memorize
- [x] Cascode ↑ $r_{out}$ ↓ headroom.
- [x] Active load ↑ gain, 0 DC headroom penalty.
- [x] Larger $W/L$ → lower $V_{OV}$ → lower power, smaller area for same current.
- [x] $\lambda \propto 1/L$ — use **long-channel** devices for high-gain analog.

### Formulas to Memorize (Cold)
- [x] $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu C_{ox} (W/L) I_D} = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$
- [x] $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$
- [x] Intrinsic gain $g_m r_o = 2/(V_{OV} \lambda)$
- [x] Mirror ratio: $(W/L)_2 / (W/L)_1$

### Top Exam Question Templates
- [x] "Derive the current transfer ratio of a simple NMOS current mirror" → 7 marks
- [x] "Explain cascode current mirror and derive its output resistance" → 7 marks
- [x] "Compare active and passive load differential amplifiers" → 7 marks
- [x] "Design a PMOS current mirror for given $I_{REF}$ and $I_{OUT}$" → 7 marks
- [x] "Derive CMRR for a CMOS diff-amp" → 7 marks

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Advice:** Practice drawing the **circuit schematic** (with M1/M2/M3/M4 properly labeled) at least 5 times before the exam. KTU values **neat, complete, properly labeled diagrams**. A wrong or missing circuit diagram can cost you 2–3 marks even if the derivation is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOS Telescopic Cascode Amplifier (Only Voltage Gain and Input and Output Impedance of the Circuits)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MOS Telescopic Cascode Amplifier (Only Voltage Gain and Input and Output Impedance of the Circuits)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — MOS Telescopic Cascode Amplifier
## Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Telescopic Cascode Amplifier (Definition)**
> A *telescopic cascode amplifier* is a single-ended analog MOS gain stage in which a **common-source (CS) input transistor** is vertically stacked under a **common-gate (CG) cascode transistor**, and is biased by a **cascode current-mirror load** on the opposite side, so that the four transistors (two NMOS + two PMOS) appear to "telescope" into a tall, narrow column between the supply rails $V_{DD}$ and $V_{SS}$. The output node sits at the common drain of the two upper transistors.

Mathematically, the small-signal behaviour of the stage is governed by the following defining equation, which we will derive in §3:

$$
A_v \;=\; -g_{m1}\,\Bigl(\,R_{out,N}\;\Vert\;R_{out,P}\,\Bigr)
$$

where

$$
R_{out,N} \;\approx\; g_{m2}\,r_{o2}\,r_{o1}, \qquad R_{out,P} \;\approx\; g_{m4}\,r_{o4}\,r_{o3}
$$

and the **input resistance** is theoretically infinite because the signal enters the **gate** of a MOSFET.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **two-storey water pump**:

- The **ground-floor pump (M1)** pulls water in from a pipe and pushes it upward — this is the *common-source* stage that does the basic amplification.
- The **first-floor pump (M2)** sits directly on top of M1. Instead of letting the water slosh back down, it grabs the flow and *channels* it steadily into the next pipe — this is the *cascode* (common-gate) stage.
- A *mirrored* pump on the other side (M3/M4) acts as a rigid counter-pressure, refusing to let the output pressure change much.

The combined "tall stack" gives a much **higher output impedance** (i.e., a stiffer pressure source) than either pump alone — that stiffness is exactly what produces the large voltage gain of a telescopic cascode.

### 1.3 Circuit Topology in One Glance

The four transistors and their roles:

| Transistor | Type | Role | Connection of Gate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **M1** | NMOS | Input CS — converts $v_{in}$ to $i_d$ | Driven by $v_{in}$ |
| **M2** | NMOS | Cascode (CG) — passes $i_d$ to output | Held at DC bias $V_{BIAS,N}$ |
| **M3** | PMOS | Diode-connected current-mirror half | Gate tied to drain (self-biased) |
| **M4** | PMOS | Mirror output — provides high-Z load | Tied to M3's gate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Telescopic"?** — The drain-to-drain vertical alignment of M1–M2 (NMOS stack) and M3–M4 (PMOS stack) makes the two branches look like the slides of an old radio antenna, hence *telescopic*. This stacking **doubles the intrinsic gain** of a simple CS stage with very little extra power consumption.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- Intrinsic gain of a single MOSFET: $A_{v0} = g_m r_o$ (typical CMOS: **20 – 100 V/V**)
- Telescopic cascode stage gain: $|A_v| \approx (g_m r_o)^2$ (typical: **200 – 10 000 V/V**)
- Output resistance: $R_{out} \sim g_m r_o^2$ (typical: **100 kΩ – 10 MΩ**)
- Input resistance: $R_{in} \to \infty$ (ideal MOSFET gate) — practically **> 10¹² Ω**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

The telescopic cascode works in three coordinated sub-actions during small-signal operation:

1. **Voltage-to-Current Conversion (M1, CS):**
   The input voltage $v_{in}$ at the gate of M1 produces a small drain current
   $i_{d1} = g_{m1}\,v_{in}$. M1's drain is *not* the output — it is an internal high-impedance node.

2. **Current Buffering (M2, CG):**
   The current $i_{d1}$ entering M2's source is delivered almost losslessly to M2's drain, because the CG stage has **unity current gain** but a very high input impedance at its *source*. This isolates M1's drain (Miller-sensitive) from the output node.

3. **High-Impedance Summation at Output:**
   The same current $i_{d1}$ flows into the parallel combination of
   $R_{out,N}$ (looking up into M2) and $R_{out,P}$ (looking down into M4), producing an output voltage $v_{out} = -g_{m1}\,v_{in}\,(R_{out,N}\Vert R_{out,P})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of the gain boost** — A lone CS amplifier has $A_v = -g_{m1}\,r_{o1}$. Stacking M2 on top multiplies the *output resistance* by roughly a factor of $g_{m2}\,r_{o2}$ (the cascode gain), thereby multiplying the *voltage gain* by the same factor. This is the **central trade-off** exploited in analog design: *more headroom is consumed, but more gain is obtained for the same bias current.*

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Approximation (when $g_m r_o \gg 1$) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Gain (Magnitude) | $\vert A_v \vert$ | $g_{m1}\,(R_{out,N}\Vert R_{out,P})$ | $g_{m1}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}\,g_{m2}\;\Vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o4}\,r_{o3})$ | V/V |
| 2 | Ideal Telescopic Gain | $A_v^{\text{ideal}}$ | $-\dfrac{1}{2}\,(g_m r_o)^2$ (matched) | — | V/V |
| 3 | Output Resistance (NMOS cascode side) | $R_{out,N}$ | $r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o2}\,r_{o1}$ | $g_{m2}\,r_{o2}\,r_{o1}$ | Ω |
| 4 | Output Resistance (PMOS mirror side) | $R_{out,P}$ | $r_{o3} + r_{o4} + g_{m4}\,r_{o4}\,r_{o3}$ | $g_{m4}\,r_{o4}\,r_{o3}$ | Ω |
| 5 | Total Output Resistance | $R_{out}$ | $R_{out,N}\;\Vert\;R_{out,P}$ | — | Ω |
| 6 | Input Resistance | $R_{in}$ | $\infty$ (ideal) | $\geq 10^{12}$ | Ω |
| 7 | Short-Circuit Transconductance | $G_m$ | $g_{m1}$ | — | A/V |
| 8 | Intrinsic Device Gain | $g_m r_o$ | $\sqrt{2\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2 I_D}}$ | — | V/V |
| 9 | Cascode Gain Multiplier | $k$ | $g_{m2}\,r_{o2}$ | — | V/V |
| 10 | Lower bound supply | $V_{DD,min}$ | $V_{GS1}+V_{DS,sat,M2}+V_{DS,sat,M3}+V_{GS4}-V_{SS}$ | — | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table safety note:** vertical bars are replaced by `\Vert` so that the absolute-value symbols do not break the markdown column delimiters.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operational Transconductance Amplifiers (OTAs):** the telescopic cascode is the *workhorse* input stage of every high-gain CMOS op-amp (e.g., two-stage Miller, folded-cascode op-amps).
- **Data Converters:** the input transconductor of pipelined and continuous-time $\Delta\Sigma$ ADCs uses telescopic or folded-cascode stages because of their high $G_m$ efficiency (more $g_m$ per mA of bias).
- **RF Low-Noise Amplifiers:** when stacked with an inductive load, telescopic cascode LNAs achieve high gain at millimetre-wave frequencies.
- **Readout channels for sensors:** photodiode/charge amplifiers and biomedical front-ends leverage the near-infinite $R_{in}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Small-Signal Model of the Stage

Replace each MOSFET by its small-signal equivalent. The four transistors contribute:

| Transistor | $g_m v_{gs}$ source direction | $r_o$ from drain to source |
|---|---|---|
| M1 (CS) | $g_{m1}v_{in}$ leaves drain | $r_{o1}$ between drain of M1 and ground |
| M2 (CG) | $g_{m2}v_{gs2}$ leaves drain | $r_{o2}$ between drain of M2 (output) and drain of M1 |
| M3 (diode) | $g_{m3}v_{gs3}$ leaves drain | $r_{o3}$ between drain of M3 and $V_{DD}$ |
| M4 (mirror) | $g_{m4}v_{gs4}$ leaves drain | $r_{o4}$ between drain of M4 (output) and $V_{DD}$ |

The small-signal output node is the common drain of M2 and M4.

## 3.2 Derivation of $R_{out,N}$ — Resistance Looking Into the Drain of M2 (NMOS Cascode)

Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the drain of M2. Let the voltage at the source of M2 (which is the drain of M1) be $v_y$. Current entering the test source is $i_x$.

**Step 1 — KCL at the source of M2 (drain of M1):**
The only paths from node $v_y$ to AC ground are $r_{o1}$. M2's gate is at AC ground (bias). The current into the source of M2 must equal the current leaving through $r_{o1}$:

$$
i_x \;=\; \frac{v_y}{r_{o1}}
$$

**Step 2 — KCL at the drain of M2 (test node):**
Currents leaving node $v_x$: through $r_{o2}$ into $v_y$, plus the dependent source $g_{m2}v_{gs2}$ that *also* leaves the drain because $v_{gs2} = -v_y$ (gate grounded, source at $v_y$).

$$
i_x \;=\; \frac{v_x - v_y}{r_{o2}} \;+\; g_{m2}(-v_y)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1's expression for $i_x$ into Step 2:**

$$
\frac{v_y}{r_{o1}} \;=\; \frac{v_x - v_y}{r_{o2}} \;-\; g_{m2}\,v_y
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_y$ in terms of $v_x$:**

Multiply both sides by $r_{o1} r_{o2}$:

$$
v_y\,r_{o2} \;=\; v_x\,r_{o1} \;-\; v_y\,r_{o1} \;-\; g_{m2}\,v_y\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}
$$

Collect $v_y$ terms:

$$
v_y\,(r_{o2} + r_{o1} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}) \;=\; v_x\,r_{o1}
$$

Hence:

$$
v_y \;=\; \frac{v_x\,r_{o1}}{r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $i_x$ from Step 1:**

$$
i_x \;=\; \frac{v_y}{r_{o1}} \;=\; \frac{v_x}{r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Therefore the resistance looking up into the drain of M2 is:**

$$
\boxed{\,R_{out,N} \;=\; \frac{v_x}{i_x} \;=\; r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximation** — Because $g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} \gg r_{o1},\,r_{o2}$ in any well-designed CMOS stage, the dominant term is the last one:
> $R_{out,N} \;\approx\; g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}$.

## 3.3 Derivation of $R_{out,P}$ — Resistance Looking Into the Drain of M4 (PMOS Mirror)

The PMOS side is the topological dual of the NMOS side. M3 is diode-connected (acts as a small-signal resistance $\dfrac{1}{g_{m3}}\Vert r_{o3} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_{m3}}$ at its drain), and M4's gate is tied to M3's gate.

Performing the same test-voltage analysis (with PMOS current directions reversed and noting $v_{gs4} = -v_{z}$ where $v_{z}$ is the small-signal voltage at the M3 drain), we obtain the symmetric result:

$$
\boxed{\,R_{out,P} \;=\; r_{o3} + r_{o4} + g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4}\;\approx\;g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Total Output Resistance

The two halves appear in **parallel** at the output node:

$$
\boxed{\,R_{out} \;=\; R_{out,N}\;\Vert\;R_{out,P} \;=\; (g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2})\;\Vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4})\,}
$$

## 3.5 Short-Circuit Transconductance $G_m$

A short-circuit transconductance analysis at the output (replace $R_{out}$ by a short) yields a current of $g_{m1}\,v_{in}$ flowing *out* of M2's drain. M2's CG action delivers this current with unity ratio, and the same current flows *into* M4's drain. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,G_m \;=\; g_{m1}\,}
$$

## 3.6 Voltage Gain $A_v$

By the standard two-port formulation:

$$
A_v \;=\; -G_m \cdot R_{out}
$$

Substituting the results of §3.4 and §3.5:

$$
\boxed{\,A_v \;=\; -g_{m1}\Bigl[\,(g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2})\;\Vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4})\,\Bigr]\,}
$$

**Idealised form (when M1/M2 are matched to M3/M4 such that $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_{m3} = g_{m4} = g_m$ and $r_{o1} = r_{o2} = r_{o3} = r_{o4} = r_o$):**

$$
A_v^{\text{ideal}} \;\approx\; -g_m \cdot \frac{g_m r_o^2}{2} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2}\,(g_m r_o)^2
$$

This is the celebrated **$(g_m r_o)^2/2$** telescopic-cascode gain expression emphasised in KTU examinations.

## 3.7 Input Resistance $R_{in}$

The signal enters the **gate of M1**, which is insulated by the SiO₂ gate oxide. No DC current flows; in the small-signal model the input port is connected only to the gate capacitance $C_{gs1}$, not to any resistive path. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,R_{in} \;=\; \infty \;\;(\text{ideal})\quad \text{or}\quad \geq 10^{12}\ \Omega \;\;(\text{practical, due to ESD/leakage})\,}
$$

## 3.8 Quick Numerical Sanity Check

Assume: $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = 1\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_{o1} = r_{o2} = r_{o3} = r_{o4} = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$.

$$
R_{out,N} \;\approx\; (1\text{ mA/V})(20\text{ k}\Omega)(20\text{ k}\Omega) \;=\; 400\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{out,P} \;\approx\; 400\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{out} \;\approx\; 200\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

$$
A_v \;\approx\; -(1\text{ mA/V})(200\text{ M}\Omega) \;=\; -2 \times 10^{5}\ \text{V/V}
$$

That is $\approx 106\,\text{dB}$ — a perfectly plausible figure for a deep-submicron telescopic cascode.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧩 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The transistor-level drawing of a four-MOSFET stack is not ideal for Mermaid; the figure below is a **block-level functional architecture** showing the direction of signal flow and the role of each block. It is the standard way KTU examiners expect students to *label* the amplifier on the answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DC Bias VB1 for M2 Gate"] --> B2["M2 Common-Gate Cascode NMOS"]
    A3["DC Bias VB2 for M3 M4 Gate"] --> B3["M3 Diode-Connected PMOS"]
    A3 --> B4["M4 Common-Gate Cascode PMOS"]
    B3 --- B4
    VIN["vin Signal Source"] --> B1["M1 Common-Source Input NMOS"]
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> NODE_OUT["Output Node vout"]
    B4 --> NODE_OUT
    NODE_OUT --> RL["External Load RL"]
    B1 -. drains to .- GND["AC Ground VSS"]
    B3 -. sources to .- VDD["AC Ground VDD"]
    B4 -. sources to .- VDD
    B2 -. sources to .- B1
    B1 -. produces .-> ID["id = gm1 x vin"]
    ID --> B2
    B2 -. buffers unity current gain .-> NODE_OUT
```

## 4.2 Annotated Symbolic Schematic (ASCII)

```
              VDD
               |
               |
          +----+----+
          |         |
         M3 (P)   M4 (P)        <-- PMOS cascode current mirror
        diode-conn  cascode
          |         |
          +---Vbias2 (gate of M3 = gate of M4)
          |         |
          |         +---- Vout
          |              |
          |              |
         GND            M2 (N)        <-- NMOS cascode (CG, gate = Vbias1)
          |              |
          |              |
         M1 (N)----------+             <-- NMOS input (CS, gate = vin)
          |
         GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic** — Follow the *red* path in your head: $v_{in}$ at the gate of M1 generates $i_d$ at its drain; that current is *buffered* through M2 (which is biased by $V_{BIAS1}$ at its gate) to the output node. M3 and M4 form a **current-mirror load** that converts the input-referred current into a single-ended output voltage. M4's drain resistance, boosted by its cascode action, dominates $R_{out,P}$.

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Summary Table

| Block | Stage Type | Function | Output Impedance (looking into its drain) |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | Common-Source | $v_{in} \to i_d$ | $r_{o1}$ |
| M2 | Common-Gate (cascode) | Buffers $i_d$ | $g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}$ |
| M3 | Diode-connected PMOS | Sets mirror reference | $\approx 1/g_{m3}$ |
| M4 | Cascode PMOS | Active current source | $g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4}$ |
| Output node | — | Sums currents | $R_{out,N}\Vert R_{out,P}$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 3 marks]`
**Why is the input resistance of a MOS telescopic cascode amplifier theoretically infinite? Is it truly infinite in practice?**

**Model Answer:**
The signal is applied to the **gate** of M1, which is insulated from the channel by the thin SiO₂ gate oxide. In the small-signal model, no resistive path exists between the gate and any other node, so the gate draws **zero DC current**. Hence $R_{in} = \infty$ in the ideal low-frequency model.
In practice, $R_{in}$ is limited to roughly **10¹² – 10¹⁵ Ω** by (i) sub-threshold gate leakage (tunnelling through the oxide in nanometre CMOS), (ii) ESD protection diodes, and (iii) the parasitic gate-source capacitance $C_{gs1}$, which dominates at high frequency as $Z_{in} = 1/(j\omega C_{gs1})$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, 3 marks]`
**A MOS telescopic cascode has $g_{m1} = 0.5\,\text{mA/V}$ and $R_{out} = 80\,\text{M}\Omega$. What is the voltage gain in dB?**

**Model Answer:**

$$
A_v = -g_{m1}\,R_{out} = -(0.5\times 10^{-3})(80\times 10^{6}) = -4 \times 10^{4}\,\text{V/V}
$$

$$
A_v\Big|_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(4\times 10^{4}) = 20\,(4.602) \approx 92.0\,\text{dB}
$$

The negative sign simply denotes the **180° phase inversion** of the CS-M1 stage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** — Students *very frequently* lose **3 to 5 marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to add $r_{o1} + r_{o2}$** in the exact $R_{out,N}$ expression and only writing $g_{m2}r_{o1}r_{o2}$ (the approximation). The exact form is $r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}r_{o1}r_{o2}$.
> 2. **Computing $R_{out}$ as a series combination** instead of a parallel combination. The two cascode branches are *in parallel* at the output node, *not* in series.
> 3. **Ignoring the PMOS mirror side**. Many answers derive only $R_{out,N}$; in KTU valuation, the *PMOS* side must also appear and be combined in parallel.
> 4. **Writing the input resistance as $\approx 1/g_{m1}$** by mistake — that is the input resistance of a *common-gate* stage, *not* a telescopic cascode. The telescopic cascode input is at a *gate*, not a source.
> 5. **Skipping the ideal-gain specialisation** $\dfrac{1}{2}(g_m r_o)^2$ — the KTU expected-answer key almost always demands it as a final form.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the small-signal equivalent of a MOS telescopic cascode amplifier with a current-mirror load. **Derive** the exact and approximate expressions for the output resistance seen looking into the drain of the cascode transistor M2.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the result of part (a) and the corresponding PMOS side, **derive** the closed-form voltage gain of the telescopic cascode. State the ideal specialisation in terms of $(g_m r_o)^2$.

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of $R_{out,N}$ (7 marks)**

Apply a test source $v_x$ at the drain of M2. Let the source of M2 (= drain of M1) be $v_y$. From KCL at $v_y$:

$$
i_x \;=\; \frac{v_y}{r_{o1}}
$$

From KCL at $v_x$:

$$
i_x \;=\; \frac{v_x - v_y}{r_{o2}} \;-\; g_{m2}\,v_y
$$

Equating and solving (as shown in §3.2):

$$
R_{out,N} \;=\; r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}
$$

For $g_{m2}\,r_{o1} \gg 1$:

$$
R_{out,N} \;\approx\; g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}
$$

**Distribution (7 m):** (i) Test-source setup & node labelling — 1 m; (ii) KCL at $v_y$ — 1 m; (iii) KCL at $v_x$ — 2 m; (iv) Algebraic solution & exact form — 2 m; (v) Approximation — 1 m.

**Part (b) — Total $R_{out}$ and $A_v$ (7 marks)**

By symmetry, the PMOS side yields:

$$
R_{out,P} \;\approx\; g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4}
$$

At the output node these two branches are in **parallel**:

$$
R_{out} \;=\; (g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2})\;\Vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4})
$$

The short-circuit transconductance equals $g_{m1}$ (current from M1 is buffered 1-for-1 through M2):

$$
A_v \;=\; -g_{m1}\,R_{out} \;=\; -g_{m1}\bigl[(g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2})\;\Vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4})\bigr]
$$

**Ideal specialisation** — when all $g_m$ are equal to $g_m$ and all $r_o$ are equal to $r_o$:

$$
A_v^{\text{ideal}} \;\approx\; -\frac{1}{2}\,(g_m r_o)^2
$$

**Distribution (7 m):** (i) PMOS mirror $R_{out,P}$ by analogy — 1 m; (ii) Parallel combination — 1 m; (iii) $G_m = g_{m1}$ justification — 1 m; (iv) Final $A_v$ — 2 m; (v) Ideal $(g_m r_o)^2/2$ form — 1 m; (vi) Phase/sign discussion — 1 m.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With reference to a MOS telescopic cascode amplifier, **explain** why the input resistance is ideally infinite and state its practical lower bound. Show that the **input impedance** at high frequency is dominated by the gate-source capacitance of M1.

**(b) [7 marks]** A telescopic cascode has the following device parameters: $g_{m1} = 0.4\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_{o1} = r_{o2} = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$, $r_{o3} = r_{o4} = 30\,\text{k}\Omega$, $g_{m2} = g_{m4} = 0.4\,\text{mA/V}$. **Calculate** the magnitude of the voltage gain in dB.

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — $R_{in}$ discussion (7 marks)**

The input port of the telescopic cascode is the gate of M1. Because MOSFET gates are insulated by SiO₂, the small-signal gate current is identically **zero** at low frequency:

$$
R_{in,\text{DC}} \;=\; \frac{v_{in}}{i_{g}} \;\to\; \infty
$$

In practice, gate-leakage currents ($I_g \sim$ fA–nA) and ESD diodes clamp this to roughly $10^{12}\,\Omega$ to $10^{15}\,\Omega$.

At **high frequency** the only AC path between the gate and the rest of the circuit is the gate-source capacitance $C_{gs1}$ of M1 (Miller effect on $C_{gd1}$ is negligible because the drain of M1 is *not* the output). The high-frequency input impedance is therefore:

$$
Z_{in}(j\omega) \;\approx\; \frac{1}{j\omega\,C_{gs1}}
$$

This capacitive nature makes the telescopic cascode ideal for high-impedance, low-frequency sensor interfaces but limits its use as a wideband 50 Ω input stage.

**Distribution (7 m):** (i) Gate-oxide explanation — 2 m; (ii) DC $R_{in} = \infty$ — 1 m; (iii) Practical lower-bound numbers — 1 m; (iv) $C_{gs1}$ high-frequency model — 2 m; (v) Final $Z_{in}$ expression — 1 m.

**Part (b) — Numerical gain (7 marks)**

Step 1 — $R_{out,N}$ (NMOS cascode side):

$$
R_{out,N} \;\approx\; g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} \;=\; (0.4\times 10^{-3})(25\,000)(25\,000)
$$

$$
R_{out,N} \;\approx\; 250\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

Step 2 — $R_{out,P}$ (PMOS cascode side):

$$
R_{out,P} \;\approx\; g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4} \;=\; (0.4\times 10^{-3})(30\,000)(30\,000)
$$

$$
R_{out,P} \;\approx\; 360\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

Step 3 — Parallel combination:

$$
R_{out} \;=\; \frac{R_{out,N}\,R_{out,P}}{R_{out,N}+R_{out,P}} \;=\; \frac{(250)(360)}{250+360}\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{out} \;\approx\; \frac{90\,000}{610}\,\text{M}\Omega \;\approx\; 147.5\,\text{M}\Omega
$$

Step 4 — Voltage gain magnitude:

$$
\vert A_v \vert \;=\; g_{m1}\,R_{out} \;=\; (0.4\times 10^{-3})(147.5\times 10^{6}) \;\approx\; 5.9\times 10^{4}\,\text{V/V}
$$

Step 5 — Convert to dB:

$$
A_v \big|_{dB} \;=\; 20\log_{10}(5.9\times 10^{4}) \;\approx\; 20\,(4.771) \;\approx\; 95.4\,\text{dB}
$$

**Distribution (7 m):** (i) $R_{out,N}$ — 1.5 m; (ii) $R_{out,P}$ — 1.5 m; (iii) Parallel combination — 1.5 m; (iv) $\vert A_v \vert$ — 1.5 m; (v) dB conversion — 1 m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Telescopic cascode** = CS (M1) + CG (M2) input stack with a cascode current-mirror load (M3, M4). The output node is the common drain of M2 and M4.
- ⭐ **Voltage gain:** $A_v = -g_{m1}\bigl[(g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2})\;\Vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4})\bigr] \;\approx\; -\dfrac{1}{2}(g_m r_o)^2$ in the matched ideal.
- ⭐ **Output resistance:** $R_{out,N} = r_{o1}+r_{o2}+g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} \;\approx\; g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}$ and the dual for $R_{out,P}$; **total** $R_{out} = R_{out,N}\Vert R_{out,P}$.
- ⭐ **Input resistance:** $R_{in} \to \infty$ (gate of M1), $Z_{in}(j\omega) \approx 1/(j\omega C_{gs1})$ at high frequency.
- ⭐ The PMOS and NMOS cascode branches are in **parallel** at the output — *not* in series.
- ⭐ The cascode transistor **M2 multiplies the output resistance** by its own intrinsic gain $g_{m2}\,r_{o2}$, which is the fundamental reason telescopic cascode gain is proportional to $(g_m r_o)^2$.
- ⭐ **Short-circuit transconductance** $G_m = g_{m1}$ only — the cascode M2 and mirror M4 do *not* increase $G_m$ (current gain is unity in CG; current mirrors deliver the same $i_d$).
- ⭐ Telescopic cascode consumes a large **headroom** ($V_{DS,sat}$ of M1, M2, M3, M4 all in series), limiting its use in **low-voltage** designs.
- ⭐ Compared with a folded cascode, the telescopic variant offers higher $G_m$-per-mA and a smaller noise figure, but it cannot sink current into the output node (one-directional signal swing).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS Operational Amplifier, Two Stage Operational Amplifiers, Frequency Compensation of OPAMPS, Miller Compensation, Band Gap References
Supply Independent Biasing, Temperature Independent References - Band Gap Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supply Independent Biasing, Temperature Independent References - Band Gap Reference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Supply Independent Biasing — Formal Definition

**Supply Independent Biasing** refers to a class of analog biasing techniques in which the DC bias currents or voltages generated within an integrated circuit exhibit **zero (or negligible) sensitivity to variations in the power supply voltage $V_{DD}$**. The output of such a bias network remains effectively constant even as $V_{DD}$ fluctuates due to battery droop, line transients, or load regulation effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Supply-independent references are the *foundation* on which all precision analog, mixed-signal, and data-converter (ADC/DAC) blocks are built. Without them, gain, threshold, and timing parameters would drift with the battery.

### 1.2 Temperature Independent References — Formal Definition

A **Temperature-Independent Reference** is a circuit that produces a DC voltage (or current) whose nominal value remains **constant across the industrial temperature range** (typically $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+125^{\circ}\text{C}$). The canonical implementation is the **Bandgap Voltage Reference (BGR / BGRF)**, so named because its nominal output voltage is mathematically and physically tied to the **silicon bandgap energy $E_g \approx 1.205 \text{ eV}$**, yielding $V_{REF} \approx \mathbf{1.205 \text{ V}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Bandgap Reference):** A reference circuit that linearly combines a voltage with a *negative* temperature coefficient (CTAT) with one having a *positive* temperature coefficient (PTAT), such that the **first-order temperature derivatives cancel** at the chosen reference temperature, producing a near-flat output across temperature.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mixing Hot & Cold Water" Trick

Imagine you are running a bath. The hot tap water at $60^{\circ}\text{C}$ (think of $V_{BE}$ — it gets **cooler** as the room temperature goes up, a CTAT signal). The cold tap water at $15^{\circ}\text{C}$ (think of $\Delta V_{BE}$ across a resistor — it gets **hotter** with temperature, a PTAT signal). Neither alone gives a comfortable $37^{\circ}\text{C}$ bath, but **if you mix them in the right ratio**, you get a perfectly warm bath that does not flinch when someone turns the geyser up or opens a window.

- The **hot tap** = the Base–Emitter voltage $V_{BE}$ of a forward-biased BJT (≈ $-2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$).
- The **cold tap** = the **difference** of two $V_{BE}$'s operated at unequal current densities, $\Delta V_{BE} = V_{T}\ln(N)$ (≈ $+0.087 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ per unit $\ln N$ at $300\,\text{K}$).
- The **faucet ratio** = a resistor ratio $R_2/R_1$ chosen so that the slopes cancel exactly at $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$.
- The **steady bath temperature** = the bandgap voltage, **1.205 V**.

This is the elegant essence of the bandgap reference — a **thermostatic sum** of two opposite-slope voltages.

---

### 1.4 Why Do We Even Need a Reference?

| Application Block | Why It Needs a Temperature-Stable Reference |
|---|---|
| **ADC / DAC** | Sets LSB size and full-scale accuracy; drift corrupts every code. |
| **LDO Voltage Regulators** | Feedback set-point dictates output; drift = drift in $V_{OUT}$. |
| **DRAM Sense Amplifiers** | Decision threshold must match stored cell voltage. |
| **Oscillators / PLLs** | Reference current sets $C \cdot dV/dt$ slope → frequency drift. |
| **Sensor Read-out (IoT)** | Battery-powered; both line and temperature vary. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Trivia:** Many students confuse "supply independent" with "temperature independent." They are **two orthogonal axes** of stability. A reference can be supply-independent but temperature-dependent (e.g., a simple $V_{GS}$-biased current source), or temperature-independent but supply-dependent (e.g., a Zener). The bandgap is engineered to be **simultaneously** insensitive to *both* $V_{DD}$ and $T$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Building Blocks

#### Block A — The CTAT Source: A Single $V_{BE}$

The base–emitter voltage of a bipolar transistor biased by a **PTAT current** $I_C(T)$ exhibits a well-known temperature behavior. For a BJT carrying collector current $I_C$:

$$
V_{BE}(T) = V_{G0}\left(1 - \frac{T}{T_0}\right) + V_{BE0}\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) - \frac{\eta k T}{q}\ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) + \frac{kT}{q}\ln\left(\frac{I_C(T)}{I_{C0}}\right)
$$

- $V_{G0}$ = silicon extrapolated bandgap voltage at $0 \text{ K}$ ≈ **1.205 V**.
- $V_{BE0}$ = $V_{BE}$ evaluated at the reference temperature $T_0$ (typically $300 \text{ K}$).
- $\eta$ = technology-dependent constant (≈ 3 to 4 for typical IC BJTs).
- $kT/q = V_T$ = thermal voltage ≈ **25.85 mV at 300 K**.
- $\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T}\bigg|_{I_C = \text{const}} \approx -2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ (the canonical CTAT slope).

#### Block B — The PTAT Source: $\Delta V_{BE}$ Between Two BJTs

For two BJTs (Q1, Q2) operating at **different current densities** $J_1$ and $J_2$ (ratio $N = J_1/J_2$), the difference of their base–emitter voltages is:

$$
\Delta V_{BE} = V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{J_1}{J_2}\right) = V_T \ln(N)
$$

Because $V_T = kT/q$ is **linear in absolute temperature**, $\Delta V_{BE}$ is **PTAT** (Proportional To Absolute Temperature):

$$
\frac{\partial (\Delta V_{BE})}{\partial T} = \frac{k}{q}\ln(N) \approx 0.087 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C} \times \ln(N)
$$

At $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$: $V_T \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$.

---

### 2.2 The Core Trick — Linear Combination with Cancellation

We construct the reference as:

$$
V_{REF} = V_{BE}(T) + K \cdot \Delta V_{BE}(T)
$$

where $K$ is a dimensionless scaling factor (typically implemented as a resistor ratio $R_2/R_1$).

The temperature derivative is:

$$
\frac{\partial V_{REF}}{\partial T} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T}}_{\text{NEGATIVE (CTAT)}} + K \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\partial \Delta V_{BE}}{\partial T}}_{\text{POSITIVE (PTAT)}}
$$

**Setting this to zero at $T = T_0$** determines $K$:

$$
K_{opt} = -\frac{\partial V_{BE}/\partial T}{\partial \Delta V_{BE}/\partial T}\bigg|_{T_0}
$$

And the resulting reference voltage at $T_0$ simplifies (after careful algebra) to:

$$
\boxed{V_{REF}(T_0) \approx V_{G0} = 1.205 \text{ V}}
$$

This is the famous **bandgap voltage**, independent of $V_{DD}$, and (to first order) independent of $T$.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table.** Every KTU bandgap derivation question reduces to plugging numbers into one of these identities.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Slope at 300 K | Type |
|:-:|:--|:--|:-:|:--|
| 1 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | $+0.087 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ | PTAT |
| 2 | $V_{BE}$ of a BJT | $V_{BE} \approx 0.65 \text{ V}$ (typ.) | $\approx -2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ | CTAT |
| 3 | $\Delta V_{BE}$ | $V_T \ln(N)$ | $+0.087 \ln(N) \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ | PTAT |
| 4 | PTAT Voltage | $V_{PTAT} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_T \ln(N)$ | Slope $\propto \ln(N)$ | PTAT |
| 5 | Reference Output | $V_{REF} = V_{BE} + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_T \ln(N)$ | $\dfrac{\partial V_{REF}}{\partial T} = 0$ at $T_0$ | Reference |
| 6 | Optimal Scaling $K$ | $K = \dfrac{V_{G0} - V_{BE0}}{(V_T)_0 \ln(N)}$ | Dimensionless | — |
| 7 | Reference Voltage | $V_{REF} \approx V_{G0} \approx 1.205 \text{ V}$ | Temperature-stable | — |
| 8 | PTAT Current | $I_{PTAT} = \dfrac{V_T \ln(N)}{R_1}$ | Linear in $T$ | PTAT |
| 9 | Curvature Term | $-\dfrac{\eta k T}{q}\ln(T/T_0)$ | Higher-order TC | Curvature |
| 10 | Line Sensitivity | $\text{LS} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{REF}}{\Delta V_{DD}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{DD}}{V_{REF}}$ | $\text{LS} \ll 1\,\%$ / V | Supply-indep. |

> **Critical Engineering Constants** (memorize these):
> - Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.380 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
> - Electron charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
> - $k/q = 86.17 \text{ }\mu\text{V/K}$
> - Extrapolated Si bandgap at 0 K: $V_{G0} = \mathbf{1.205 \text{ V}}$
> - $V_T$ at 300 K: $\approx \mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV}}$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lithium-Ion Battery Powered IoT Sensors**: A battery at 100% SoC may deliver $4.2 \text{ V}$, at 10% SoC only $3.0 \text{ V}$. The bandgap tolerates this $\sim 30\,\%$ supply swing while holding the ADC reference rock-steady.
- **Automotive AEC-Q100 Grade-0**: Components must work from $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+150^{\circ}\text{C}$. A TC of $\pm 5 \text{ ppm/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ is achievable only with curvature-compensated bandgaps.
- **Precision Instrumentation (DMMs, Bench Meters)**: 6.5-digit multimeters need references stable to better than $1 \text{ ppm/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ — requiring bandgap + chopper stabilization + buried Zener.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Temperature Behavior of $V_{BE}$

We start from the Ebers–Moll relation for a BJT in forward active region:

$$
I_C = J_S \cdot A_E \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)
$$

Solving for $V_{BE}$:

$$
V_{BE} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_C}{J_S A_E}\right)
$$

The saturation current density $J_S$ has an Arrhenius temperature dependence:

$$
J_S(T) = K \cdot T^{(4-\eta)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{G0}}{V_T}\right)
$$

Plug back, expand, and collect the temperature-linear terms. The exact derivation yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{BE}(T) &= V_{G0} - \frac{T}{T_0}\big[V_{G0} - V_{BE0}\big] - (\eta - 1) V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) \\
&\quad + V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_C(T)}{I_{C0}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

If $I_C$ is itself PTAT, i.e. $I_C(T) = I_{C0}\cdot(T/T_0)$, then the last term becomes $(\eta)V_T \ln(T/T_0)$, and the slope simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T}\bigg|_{\text{PTAT bias}} = -\frac{V_{G0} - V_{BE0}}{T_0} + \frac{(\eta - 1) k}{q}}
$$

Evaluating at $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$ with $V_{BE0} = 0.65 \text{ V}$, $V_{G0} = 1.205 \text{ V}$, $\eta = 3.6$:

$$
\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T} \approx -\frac{1.205 - 0.650}{300} + \frac{(3.6 - 1)(86.17\,\mu\text{V/K})}{1}
$$

$$
\approx -1.85 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C} + 0.224 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C} \approx -1.63 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

(For constant $I_C$, the slope is closer to $-2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The $\Delta V_{BE}$ PTAT Voltage

Consider two BJTs Q1 and Q2 with emitter area ratio $N : 1$ ($A_{E1} = N \cdot A_{E2}$), both carrying the **same** collector current $I_C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{BE1} &= V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_C}{J_S A_{E1}}\right) = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_C}{J_S N A_{E2}}\right) \\
V_{BE2} &= V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_C}{J_S A_{E2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{BE} &= V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_C}{J_S N A_{E2}}\right) - V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_C}{J_S A_{E2}}\right) \\
&= V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{N}\right) \cdot (-1) \quad \text{(combining log terms)} \\
&= V_T \ln(N)
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$:

$$
\frac{\partial \Delta V_{BE}}{\partial T} = \frac{k}{q}\ln(N) = 86.17\,\mu\text{V/K} \cdot \ln(N)
$$

For $N = 8$: $\partial \Delta V_{BE}/\partial T \approx +0.179 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Classical Op-Amp Bandgap Reference Output

The classical **Brokaw / Widlar bandgap cell** uses an op-amp to force two nodes to the same potential. The simplified canonical schematic (the workhorse of every analog IC) is:

```
    VDD
     |
    R2
     |
     +------- VREF
     |
    [Op-Amp output drives this node through R2 from VDD; alternatively VREF is taken at the top of R2]
```

The op-amp's negative feedback enforces:

$$
V_{X} = V_{Y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{(both BJTs share the same collector voltage)}
$$

The currents through Q1 and Q2 are forced equal to $I$ by matched PMOS current mirrors. With Q1 having $N$ times the area of Q2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{BE2} - V_{BE1} &= I \cdot R_1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad V_T \ln(N) &= I \cdot R_1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad I &= \frac{V_T \ln(N)}{R_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This current is **PTAT**. It flows through both Q1 and Q2 (and is mirrored into R2).

The output voltage $V_{REF}$, taken at the top of $R_2$ above the collector of Q2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{REF} &= V_{BE2} + I \cdot R_2 \\
&= V_{BE2} + \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_T \ln(N)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master bandgap equation**:

$$
\boxed{V_{REF} = V_{BE}(T) + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\, V_T \ln(N)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Determining the Optimal Resistor Ratio

Differentiate $V_{REF}$ with respect to $T$:

$$
\frac{\partial V_{REF}}{\partial T} = \frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T} + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\ln(N) \cdot \frac{k}{q}
$$

Set this to zero at $T = T_0$:

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} \ln(N) = -\frac{\partial V_{BE}/\partial T}{k/q}\bigg|_{T_0}
$$

Using $\partial V_{BE}/\partial T \approx -2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $k/q = 86.17 \text{ }\mu\text{V/}^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1}\ln(N) = \frac{2 \text{ mV}}{0.08617 \text{ mV}} \approx 23.2
$$

For $N = 8$: $\ln(8) = 3 \ln(2) = 2.079$

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{23.2}{2.079} \approx 11.16
$$

Now evaluate $V_{REF}$ at $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{REF}(300\,\text{K}) &= V_{BE0} + \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_T \ln(N) \\
&= 0.650 + 11.16 \cdot (25.85 \text{ mV}) \cdot 2.079 \\
&= 0.650 + 0.600 \\
&= \mathbf{1.250 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Slightly above 1.205 V because we used the linear $-2\text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ approximation; exact evaluation with the full logarithmic formula gives $V_{REF} = V_{G0} = 1.205 \text{ V}$.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1.205 V?** This is the **silicon bandgap voltage at 0 K**, extrapolated back along the $V_{BE}$–$T$ line. The bandgap reference literally *outputs* the bandgap, hence the name.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Curvature (Higher-Order Temperature Coefficient)

Even with perfect first-order cancellation, a residual **bow** remains due to the $\ln(T/T_0)$ non-linearity in the $V_{BE}$ expression. This is the **curvature error**:

$$
V_{REF}(T) \approx V_{REF}(T_0) + c_2 (T - T_0)^2 + \dots
$$

Curvature-correction techniques include:

- **Non-linear PTAT current**: bias $I_C$ with $T^\alpha$ so its $\ln(I_C)$ compensates the $\ln(T/T_0)$ term in $V_{BE}$.
- **Piecewise-linear temperature compensation** (resistor trimming in segments).
- **High-order temperature compensation using multiple PTAT branches** with different $N$ ratios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** Curvature correction is the difference between a **5 ppm/°C** and a **50 ppm/°C** reference. Most board-exam questions only ask for first-order cancellation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of a Bandgap Reference

Below is the **block-level functional flow** showing how CTAT and PTAT combine into a temperature-independent output. This is the canonical mental model every KTU topper carries to the exam hall.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef ctatBox fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ptatBox fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#2c5fa8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sumBox  fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef refBox  fill:#d8f5d0,stroke:#1f7a1f,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    subgraph CTAT_SOURCE["CTAT VOLTAGE SOURCE"]
        BJT_Q["Single BJT Q2 biased by PTAT current"]
        VBE_OUT["V_BE signal, slope equals -2 mV per degree C"]
    end

    subgraph PTAT_SOURCE["PTAT VOLTAGE SOURCE"]
        PAIR["Two BJTs Q1 and Q2 with emitter area ratio N greater than 1"]
        DELTA["Differential pair amplifier extracts V_BE1 minus V_BE2"]
        DELTA_V["Delta V_BE equals V_T times ln of N, slope positive"]
    end

    subgraph SCALING["WEIGHTED SCALING NETWORK"]
        R1["Resistor R1 sets PTAT current I equals Delta V_BE divided by R1"]
        R2["Resistor R2 scales PTAT voltage by ratio R2 divided by R1"]
    end

    subgraph SUMMER["OPAMP SUMMING JUNCTION"]
        OP["Opamp enforces V plus equals V minus across the pair"]
        SUM_OUT["Sums V_BE and scaled PTAT component with correct polarity"]
    end

    OUTPUT["V_REF equals 1.205 V reference, supply and temperature independent"]

    BJT_Q --> VBE_OUT
    VBE_OUT --> OP

    PAIR --> DELTA
    DELTA --> DELTA_V
    DELTA_V --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> SUM_OUT

    OP --> SUM_OUT
    SUM_OUT --> OUTPUT

    class BJT_Q,VBE_OUT ctatBox
    class PAIR,DELTA,DELTA_V ptatBox
    class R1,R2,OP,SUM_OUT sumBox
    class OUTPUT refBox
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **red block** produces the CTAT signal ($V_{BE}$).
- The **blue block** produces the PTAT signal ($\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln N$).
- The **yellow block** scales the PTAT signal by $R_2/R_1$.
- The **opamp summer** combines them.
- The **green block** is the temperature-independent output $V_{REF} \approx 1.205 \text{ V}$.

---

### 4.2 Canonical Transistor-Level Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The op-amp–based bandgap reference consists of:
1. **Two BJTs** Q1 (large, area $N \cdot A_E$) and Q2 (unit, area $A_E$).
2. **A resistor $R_1$** connecting Q1's emitter to ground (or to the lower node), across which $\Delta V_{BE}$ develops.
3. **An op-amp** that senses the collector voltages of Q1 and Q2 and drives them equal.
4. **A PMOS current mirror** that copies the resulting PTAT current into $R_2$.
5. **Resistor $R_2$** in series with Q2's collector, across which the PTAT voltage develops.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef supplyStyle fill:#f0e8ff,stroke:#6b3fa0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bjtStyle    fill:#fff0e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rStyle      fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006978,stroke-width:2px
    classDef opStyle     fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#a04000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef refStyle    fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:3px

    VDD["V_DD supply rail, ideally constant"]:::supplyStyle
    M1["PMOS mirror device M1 sources current I into branch A"]:::supplyStyle
    M2["PMOS mirror device M2 sources current I into branch B, matched to M1"]:::supplyStyle

    NODE_X["Node X, the inverting opamp input, tied to collector of Q1"]:::opStyle
    NODE_Y["Node Y, the non-inverting opamp input, tied to collector of Q2"]:::opStyle

    Q1["BJT Q1, emitter area N times A_E, large device"]:::bjtStyle
    Q2["BJT Q2, emitter area A_E, unit device"]:::bjtStyle

    R1["Resistor R1 from emitter of Q1 to ground, develops Delta V_BE"]:::rStyle
    R2["Resistor R2 from collector of Q2 to ground, develops PTAT voltage"]:::rStyle

    OPAMP["Operational amplifier OA, output drives the gates of M1 and M2"]:::opStyle
    VREF_NODE["V_REF output node, equal to V_BE_Q2 plus I times R2, equals 1.205 V"]:::refStyle

    VDD --> M1
    VDD --> M2

    M1 --> NODE_X
    NODE_X --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1
    R1 --> GND1["Ground"]:::supplyStyle

    M2 --> NODE_Y
    NODE_Y --> Q2
    Q2 --> GND2["Ground"]:::supplyStyle
    NODE_Y --> R2
    R2 --> GND3["Ground"]:::supplyStyle

    NODE_X --> IN_MINUS["Opamp inverting input minus"]:::opStyle
    NODE_Y --> IN_PLUS["Opamp non inverting input plus"]:::opStyle
    IN_MINUS --> OPAMP
    IN_PLUS --> OPAMP
    OPAMP --> GATES["Drives gates of M1 and M2 to force V_X equals V_Y"]:::opStyle

    NODE_Y --> VREF_NODE
```

---

### 4.3 Topology Comparison: Widlar vs. Brokaw vs. Kuijk

| Topology | Year | Key Feature | Typical $V_{REF}$ | Modern Use |
|:--|:-:|:--|:-:|:--|
| **Widlar** | 1971 | Original; uses $\Delta V_{BE}$ across emitter resistor | $\approx 1.205 \text{ V}$ | Historical only |
| **Brokaw** | 1974 | Two-transistor cell with negative-feedback loop; better PSRR | $\approx 1.205 \text{ V}$ | Very common |
| **Kuijk** | 1973 | Uses a third BJT to generate PTAT current with high accuracy | $\approx 1.205 \text{ V}$ | Common in BiCMOS |
| **Banba** | 1999 | CMOS-only; uses parasitic vertical PNP in p-substrate | $\approx 1.205 \text{ V}$ | Mainstream CMOS |
| **Curvature-Compensated** | 1980s+ | Adds non-linear PTAT to cancel $\ln(T/T_0)$ term | $\approx 1.205 \text{ V}$ | High-precision (ppm/°C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Every topology outputs **the same magic voltage** — $V_{G0} \approx 1.205 \text{ V}$. The choice of topology only affects PSRR, start-up behavior, area, and current consumption.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (2 Marks each — short-answer)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain why the bandgap reference voltage is approximately $1.205 \text{ V}$ and not $1.5 \text{ V}$ or $5 \text{ V}$.**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**

The bandgap reference combines a CTAT voltage (single $V_{BE} \approx 0.65 \text{ V}$) with a weighted PTAT voltage ($K \cdot V_T \ln N$). At the optimum $K$, the first-order temperature coefficients cancel, and the resulting DC value equals the **extrapolated silicon bandgap voltage at 0 K**, which is $V_{G0} \approx 1.205 \text{ V}$. It cannot be $1.5 \text{ V}$ or $5 \text{ V}$ because those values are not physically tied to the silicon bandgap energy; they would be arbitrary and would not enjoy the natural temperature-coefficient cancellation property. Hence the name **"bandgap" reference** literally reflects the silicon bandgap energy.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between CTAT and PTAT voltages, giving one example of each from the context of bandgap references.**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**

| Property | CTAT | PTAT |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Full form | Complementary To Absolute Temperature | Proportional To Absolute Temperature |
| Slope | Negative ($\partial V/\partial T < 0$) | Positive ($\partial V/\partial T > 0$) |
| Example in BGR | Base–Emitter voltage of a single BJT ($V_{BE} \approx -2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$) | Difference of two $V_{BE}$'s ($\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln N \approx +0.087 \ln N \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$) |
| Physical origin | Exponential $I$–$V$ characteristic of p-n junction | Thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q$ in the difference |

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks each — full descriptive)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, students must answer ONE full 14-mark question out of an internal choice. Model solutions are provided below.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **bandgap voltage reference** using bipolar transistors. Derive the expression for the reference voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bandgap reference uses two BJTs with an emitter-area ratio $N = 10$. The op-amp forces equal currents $I = 100 \text{ }\mu\text{A}$ through both transistors at $T = 300 \text{ K}$. The resistor $R_1$ (developing $\Delta V_{BE}$) is $2 \text{ k}\Omega$, and the scaling resistor $R_2 = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate:
   - (i) $\Delta V_{BE}$
   - (ii) PTAT voltage across $R_2$
   - (iii) Total reference voltage if $V_{BE} = 0.65 \text{ V}$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Draw the circuit.

```
      V_DD
       |
      M1 (PMOS)
       |
      Node X ------- to inverting input of opamp
       |
       C
      Q1 (BJT, area = N·AE)
       |
       E --- R1 --- GND

      V_DD
       |
      M2 (PMOS), matched to M1
       |
      Node Y ------- to non-inverting input of opamp
       |              |
       C              +----- VREF output
      Q2 (BJT, area = AE)
       |
       E --- GND

   (R2 connects Node Y to ground; VREF taken at top of R2.)
```

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: Explain op-amp action. The op-amp in negative feedback forces $V_X = V_Y$. With identical PMOS mirror devices, currents through Q1 and Q2 are equal: $I_1 = I_2 = I$.

**Step 3 (2 marks)**: Apply KVL. The voltage across $R_1$ equals the difference in $V_{BE}$:
$$
V_{BE1} + I R_1 = V_{BE2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac{V_{BE2} - V_{BE1}}{R_1} = \frac{V_T \ln(N)}{R_1}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks)**: Derive $V_{REF}$:
$$
V_{REF} = V_{BE2} + I R_2 = V_{BE} + \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_T \ln(N)
$$

Equivalently, $V_{REF} \approx V_{G0} = 1.205 \text{ V}$ when the resistor ratio is chosen for first-order TC cancellation.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given**: $N = 10$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$, $I = 100 \text{ }\mu\text{A}$, $R_1 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{BE} = 0.65 \text{ V}$.

**(i) $\Delta V_{BE}$:**

$$
V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 25.87 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln(N) = 25.87 \text{ mV} \times \ln(10) = 25.87 \times 2.3026 = 59.57 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta V_{BE} \approx 59.6 \text{ mV}}
$$

**(ii) PTAT voltage across $R_2$:**

The PTAT current is:
$$
I_{PTAT} = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{R_1} = \frac{59.57 \text{ mV}}{2 \text{ k}\Omega} = 29.79 \text{ }\mu\text{A}
$$

This same current flows through $R_2$ (assuming the same mirror ratio):
$$
V_{R2} = I_{PTAT} \cdot R_2 = 29.79 \text{ }\mu\text{A} \times 20 \text{ k}\Omega = 595.8 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{PTAT,\,R2} \approx 596 \text{ mV}}
$$

**(iii) Total reference voltage:**

$$
V_{REF} = V_{BE} + V_{R2} = 0.650 + 0.596 = 1.246 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{REF} \approx 1.246 \text{ V}}
$$

> **Verification of supply independence**: Notice that $V_{REF}$ depends only on $V_{BE}$ (a semiconductor physics constant), $V_T$ (a function of absolute temperature only), and resistor ratios (set at fabrication). It is **completely independent of $V_{DD}$**, satisfying the supply-independent criterion.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **supply-independent biasing** in MOSFET circuits. Explain with a suitable circuit how a constant $V_{GS}$-referenced self-biased current source works. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the bandgap reference of part (a), the designer wants the output to be exactly $1.205 \text{ V}$ at $T = 300 \text{ K}$. The $V_{BE}$ of Q2 at this temperature is $0.65 \text{ V}$ and $\partial V_{BE}/\partial T = -2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$. Determine:
   - (i) The required PTAT slope in $\text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.
   - (ii) The required resistor ratio $R_2/R_1$ if $N = 8$.
   - (iii) The actual output at $T = 350 \text{ K}$, neglecting curvature. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Concept (3 marks)**: A supply-independent bias network generates a bias current (or voltage) whose value depends only on **device parameters and ratios**, not on the absolute value of $V_{DD}$. This is essential because $V_{DD}$ can vary by $\pm 10\,\%$ or more in real systems (battery droop, IR drop, regulator ripple).

**Self-biased $V_{GS}$-referenced current source (4 marks)**:

A MOSFET in saturation has $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$. If we can construct a circuit where the voltage across a resistor $R$ is forced to equal $V_{GS}$ of a diode-connected MOSFET, then:

$$
I_D = \frac{V_{GS}}{R}
$$

Because $V_{GS}$ is a device-physics quantity (set by $\mu_n$, $C_{ox}$, threshold, etc.), the current is **independent of $V_{DD}$**.

**Canonical circuit**: The "beta-multiplier reference" or "$V_{GS}/R$ reference":

- M1 (diode-connected, $W/L = S$) has its drain shorted to gate.
- M2 (mirror device, $W/L = K \cdot S$) carries a current $K$ times larger.
- Both transistors share the same $V_{GS}$ (because their gates are tied and sources are at ground).
- The difference in currents flows through resistor $R$ connecting M1's drain to $V_{DD}$.
- KVL on the gate-source loop: $V_{GS1} = V_{GS2} + I_2 R$.
- Because $V_{GS1} = V_{GS2} + V_{TH}$ (for $K > 1$), we get $V_{TH} = I_2 R$, i.e. $I_2 = V_{TH}/R$ — **supply-independent** and **weakly temperature-dependent** through $V_{TH}$.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Required PTAT slope (2 marks):**

For zero TC at $T_0$, the PTAT slope must **exactly cancel** the CTAT slope:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial V_{REF}}{\partial T}\right|_{T_0} = \frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T} + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\ln(N) \cdot \frac{k}{q} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1}\ln(N) \cdot \frac{k}{q} = -\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T} = +2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

So the **weighted PTAT slope** must equal $+2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
\boxed{\text{Required PTAT contribution} = +2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}}
$$

**(ii) Required resistor ratio (3 marks):**

With $N = 8$, $\ln(8) = 2.0794$, and $k/q = 86.17 \text{ }\mu\text{V/}^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot 2.0794 \cdot 86.17 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot 1.792 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.792 \times 10^{-4}} = 11.16
$$

$$
\boxed{R_2/R_1 \approx 11.16}
$$

**(iii) Output at 350 K neglecting curvature (2 marks):**

Under the assumption of perfect first-order cancellation, $V_{REF}$ is **constant** at $V_{G0} = 1.205 \text{ V}$ across all temperatures (to first order):

$$
\boxed{V_{REF}(350\text{ K}) \approx 1.205 \text{ V}}
$$

(In reality, curvature terms introduce a residual second-order drift of order $0.5$ to $5 \text{ mV}$ across this temperature range.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Bandgap Reference Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $\partial V_{BE}/\partial T$**: Many students write $+2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ (positive slope). It is **negative** (CTAT). Loss: **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_T$ at different temperatures**: $V_T = kT/q$ — it is NOT 25.85 mV at all temperatures. At $T = 350 \text{ K}$, $V_T = 30.16 \text{ mV}$. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the ratio direction**: $V_{BE}$ is *larger* on the small-area BJT (same current, smaller area = higher current density = larger $V_{BE}$). So $\Delta V_{BE} = V_{BE2} - V_{BE1} = V_T \ln N$ where Q2 is the *smaller* device. Drawing the wrong transistor as the larger one loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the supply-independent aspect**: A complete answer must mention that $V_{REF}$ depends only on **ratios** and **physical constants**, not on $V_{DD}$. Loss: **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Not stating the bandgap value**: Always conclude with "$V_{REF} \approx V_{G0} = 1.205 \text{ V}$" for full marks. Loss: **0.5–1 mark**.
>
> 6. **In a derivation, skipping the op-amp feedback argument**: Examiners want to see *why* $V_X = V_Y$ — explicitly mention "negative feedback forces the inverting and non-inverting inputs to be at the same potential." Loss: **1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Supply independence** = output independent of $V_{DD}$; relies on **device ratios** and **physical constants**.
- [x] **Temperature independence** = output independent of $T$; relies on **CTAT + PTAT cancellation**.
- [x] **CTAT signal** = single $V_{BE}$, slope $\approx -2 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- [x] **PTAT signal** = $\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln(N)$, slope $= \frac{k}{q} \ln N \approx 0.087 \ln N \text{ mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- [x] **Master bandgap equation**: $V_{REF} = V_{BE} + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_T \ln(N)$.
- [x] **Optimal $K = R_2/R_1$**: $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{2 \text{ mV}}{(k/q)\ln N} \approx \dfrac{23.2}{\ln N}$.
- [x] **Magic voltage**: $V_{REF} = V_{G0} = \mathbf{1.205 \text{ V}}$ (silicon bandgap at 0 K).
- [x] **PTAT current**: $I_{PTAT} = \dfrac{V_T \ln(N)}{R_1}$.
- [x] **Thermal voltage**: $V_T = kT/q = 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at $T = 300 \text{ K}$.
- [x] **Extrapolated Si bandgap**: $V_{G0} \approx 1.205 \text{ V}$; **physical bandgap energy**: $E_g \approx 1.12 \text{ eV}$ at 300 K (different quantity!).
- [x] **Op-amp feedback** forces $V_X = V_Y$, equal currents via matched mirror.
- [x] **Curvature term** $\propto \ln(T/T_0)$ causes residual 2nd-order drift — requires compensation for ppm/°C-grade references.
- [x] **Common topologies**: Widlar (1971), Brokaw (1974), Kuijk (1973), Banba (1999, CMOS-only).
- [x] **Typical achievable TC**: $5$ to $50 \text{ ppm/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ for untrimmed CMOS BGR; $<1 \text{ ppm/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ with trimming + curvature comp.
- [x] **Always include in your answer**: (1) circuit diagram, (2) op-amp feedback reasoning, (3) $\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln N$, (4) the master equation, (5) the numerical value $V_{G0}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAC Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** DAC Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of DAC Specifications

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a mixed-signal circuit that converts a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital word (typically an n-bit binary code) into a corresponding continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog voltage or current. The **DAC Specifications** constitute the standardized set of static and dynamic performance metrics used by KTU examiners to quantitatively benchmark the fidelity, accuracy, speed, and linearity of a DAC against its ideal transfer characteristics.

These specifications fall under two broad categories:
- **Static (DC) Specifications**: Resolution, Full-Scale Range, LSB Weight, Offset Error, Gain Error, Integral Non-Linearity (INL), Differential Non-Linearity (DNL).
- **Dynamic (AC) Specifications**: Settling Time, Glitch Energy, Slew Rate, Spurious-Free Dynamic Range (SFDR), Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), Total Harmonic Distortion (THD), Signal-to-Noise and Distortion (SINAD), and Effective Number of Bits (ENOB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: For the course PEECT754 (Mixed Signal Circuits), Module 4 explicitly tests the student's ability to *derive, define, and apply* these DAC specifications to evaluate architectural trade-offs in R-2R Ladder, Thermometer-Coded, and Current-Steering DACs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Digital Water Tap" Concept

Imagine a **digital water tap system** connected to a main overhead tank (the analog reference voltage $V_{REF}$). Each tap represents one binary bit of the DAC.

- A **1-bit resolution** tap can either be fully **ON** (full pressure) or fully **OFF** (zero pressure) — a binary tap.
- An **n-bit tap** has $2^n$ discrete combinations of opening/closing. Higher resolution = finer control over water flow = smoother analog output.

Now, real taps are imperfect:
- **Offset Error** is like the tap dripping slightly even when turned OFF (mechanical leakage).
- **Gain Error** is when the tap, when fully open, delivers slightly more or less water than the calibrated maximum.
- **DNL** is the inconsistency in how much water flows when you nudge the tap by one unit — some increments feel smaller, others larger.
- **Settling Time** is the delay between turning the tap and the water actually reaching a steady flow rate.
- **Glitch** is the unwanted momentary surge of water when transitioning between settings (e.g., the tap momentarily overshoots before settling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook**: Just as an engineer calibrates a tap for smoothness, linearity, and response time, a DAC designer calibrates the converter against the specifications defined below.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Overview

The **fundamental performance constants** that govern DAC behavior are:
- **Resolution (n)**: Expressed in **bits**, typically ranging from 8 to 24 bits.
- **Reference Voltage ($V_{REF}$ or $V_{FS}$)**: The full-scale reference, in **Volts (V)**.
- **LSB Weight (Δ)**: The smallest analog increment the DAC can produce, in **Volts**.
- **Quantization Noise Power ($P_Q$)**: An unavoidable theoretical noise floor, in **Watts** or **dB**.

These four parameters, along with sampling frequency $f_s$, form the foundation upon which every other specification is mathematically derived.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static (DC) Specifications — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Resolution (n)
The resolution of a DAC specifies the number of discrete output levels it can produce.

$$
\text{Number of levels} = 2^n
$$

A higher n implies finer granularity. Resolution does **not** alone define accuracy; it is merely a count of discrete steps.

### 2.1.2 Full-Scale Range (FSR or $V_{FS}$)
The maximum analog output excursion from minimum to maximum code.

$$
V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(\frac{2^n - 1}{2^n}\right) \approx V_{REF}
$$

For an ideal unipolar DAC, the output swings from **0 V** to $V_{FS}$.

### 2.1.3 Least Significant Bit (LSB) Weight (Δ)
The analog increment corresponding to a 1-LSB change in the digital input.

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention**: Some KTU textbooks use the strict definition $\Delta = V_{REF}/2^n$ (treating FSR as $V_{REF}$), while others use $\Delta = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$. Always verify the textbook convention when solving numerical problems.

### 2.1.4 Ideal Transfer Function
For an ideal unipolar n-bit DAC with digital input code $D$ (where $0 \leq D \leq 2^n - 1$):

$$
V_{OUT} = D \cdot \Delta = D \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}
$$

### 2.1.5 Offset Error ($E_{OFF}$)
The output deviation when the input code is **zero**.

$$
E_{OFF} = V_{OUT}(0)_{\text{actual}} - V_{OUT}(0)_{\text{ideal}}
$$

### 2.1.6 Gain Error ($E_{GAIN}$)
The deviation of the actual full-scale output from the ideal full-scale output, after offset nullification, expressed as a fraction of FSR or in LSB units.

$$
E_{GAIN} = \frac{V_{OUT}(\text{full-scale})_{\text{actual}} - V_{OUT}(\text{full-scale})_{\text{ideal}}}{V_{FS}}
$$

### 2.1.7 Integral Non-Linearity (INL)
The maximum deviation of the actual DAC transfer characteristic from the best-fit straight line (end-point or best-fit), measured at any code.

$$
\text{INL}(D) = \frac{V_{OUT}(D)_{\text{actual}} - V_{OUT}(D)_{\text{ideal}}}{V_{LSB}}
$$

Measured in **LSB units**.

### 2.1.8 Differential Non-Linearity (DNL)
The deviation of the actual step size from an ideal 1-LSB step between adjacent codes.

$$
\text{DNL}(D) = \frac{V_{OUT}(D+1) - V_{OUT}(D)}{V_{LSB}} - 1
$$

A DNL of **-1 LSB or worse** implies **non-monotonicity**.

### 2.1.9 Monotonicity
A DAC is **monotonic** if its output never decreases as the input code increases monotonically.

$$
\text{Monotonic} \iff \text{DNL}(D) \geq -1 \text{ LSB} \quad \forall D
$$

## 2.2 Dynamic (AC) Specifications — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Settling Time ($t_s$)
The time required for the output to settle within a specified error band (typically $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB or $\pm 0.1\%$) of its final value after a full-scale code transition.

### 2.2.2 Glitch Energy
Unwanted transient impulses that occur during major code transitions (e.g., 0111...1 → 1000...0). Quantified as the area under the glitch impulse in **V·s** (Volt-seconds).
