### 2.2.3 Spurious-Free Dynamic Range (SFDR)
The ratio of the RMS amplitude of the fundamental output signal to the RMS amplitude of the largest spurious harmonic in the output spectrum.

$$
\text{SFDR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\text{fundamental, rms}}}{V_{\text{largest spur, rms}}}\right)
$$

### 2.2.4 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)
For an ideal N-bit DAC sampled at $f_s$ producing a full-scale sinusoid of frequency $f_o$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

### 2.2.5 Signal-to-Noise and Distortion (SINAD)
The ratio of RMS signal to RMS noise **plus** all harmonic distortion components.

$$
\text{SINAD (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}} + P_{\text{distortion}}}\right)
$$

### 2.2.6 Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)
The actual usable resolution of a non-ideal DAC, computed from SINAD:

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}
$$

### 2.2.7 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)
The ratio of RMS harmonic distortion components to the RMS fundamental.

$$
\text{THD (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_k^2}}{V_1}\right)
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Specification | Formula | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resolution levels | $L = 2^n$ | Unitless |
| 2 | Full-Scale Range | $V_{FS} \approx V_{REF}$ | Volts |
| 3 | LSB Weight (Δ) | $\Delta = V_{REF}/2^n$ | Volts |
| 4 | Ideal Output | $V_{OUT} = D \cdot \Delta$ | Volts |
| 5 | Offset Error | $E_{OFF} = V_{OUT}(0)_{\text{act}} - V_{OUT}(0)_{\text{ideal}}$ | Volts / LSB |
| 6 | Gain Error | $E_{GAIN} = (V_{FS,\text{act}} - V_{FS,\text{ideal}})/V_{FS}$ | % / LSB |
| 7 | INL | $\text{INL}(D) = (V_{\text{act}} - V_{\text{ideal}})/V_{LSB}$ | LSB |
| 8 | DNL | $\text{DNL}(D) = \Delta V_{\text{act}} / V_{LSB} - 1$ | LSB |
| 9 | Monotonicity | $\text{DNL}(D) \geq -1 \text{ LSB}$ | Condition |
| 10 | Ideal SNR | $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | dB |
| 11 | SINAD | $\text{SINAD} = 10 \log(P_s / (P_n + P_d))$ | dB |
| 12 | ENOB | $(\text{SINAD} - 1.76)/6.02$ | Bits |
| 13 | SFDR | $20 \log(V_{\text{fund}} / V_{\text{spur}})$ | dB |
| 14 | THD | $20 \log(\sqrt{\sum V_h^2} / V_1)$ | dB |
| 15 | Quantization Noise | $P_Q = \Delta^2 / 12$ | $V^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: DAC specifications are critical in applications such as **audio playback (CD players, smartphones)** where ENOB > 16 bits is required, **high-speed telecommunications (5G base stations)** where SFDR > 80 dBc is mandated, and **precision instrumentation** where INL < 1 LSB is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ideal SNR Formula for an N-bit DAC

**Goal**: Derive $\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB.

### Step 1: Define the Quantization Error
The quantization error $e_q(t)$ is the difference between the analog input and the quantized output. For an ideal N-bit ADC (which models the inverse process of DAC quantization error), it is uniformly distributed in the range $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.

### Step 2: Compute the Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power
Since $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over a width of $\Delta$:

$$
P_Q = \overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral

$$
P_Q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[\frac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
$$

$$
P_Q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[\left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3\right]
$$

$$
P_Q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{4}
$$

$$
P_Q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Signal Power (Full-Scale Sinusoid)
For a full-scale sinusoid of amplitude $V_p$ where $V_p = 2^n \Delta / 2$ (peak-to-peak $V_{REF}$):

$$
P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{V_p^2}{2} = \frac{(2^{n-1} \Delta)^2}{2} = \frac{2^{2n-2} \Delta^2}{2}
$$

### Step 5: Compute SNR

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_Q} = \frac{2^{2n-2} \Delta^2 / 2}{\Delta^2 / 12}
$$

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{2^{2n-2} \cdot 12}{2} = 6 \cdot 2^{2n-2}
$$

### Step 6: Convert to dB

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(6 \cdot 2^{2n-2})
$$

$$
= 10 \log_{10}(6) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2n-2})
$$

$$
= 7.78 + (2n - 2)(3.01)
$$

$$
= 7.78 + 6.02n - 6.02
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification**: For a 16-bit ideal DAC, SNR = $6.02(16) + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB. This matches the well-known result for CD-quality audio.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ENOB from SINAD

**Given**: SINAD measured in dB.

$$
\text{SINAD} = 6.02 \cdot \text{ENOB} + 1.76
$$

Solving for ENOB:

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}
$$

### Numerical Example
A 12-bit DAC is measured with a SINAD of 70 dB.

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{70 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{68.24}{6.02} \approx 11.34 \text{ bits}
$$

This indicates the DAC is performing about **0.66 bits worse** than its nominal resolution due to noise and distortion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: DNL and Monotonicity Condition

**Scenario**: For an 8-bit DAC, the measured output codes are:
- Code 127 → 1.998 V
- Code 128 → 2.001 V
- Code 129 → 2.005 V

Given $V_{REF} = 5.12$ V.

### Step 1: Compute LSB Weight

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{5.12}{256} = 0.02 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Actual Step from 127 to 128

$$
\Delta V_{127 \to 128} = 2.001 - 1.998 = 0.003 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 3: Compute DNL at Code 128

$$
\text{DNL}(128) = \frac{0.003}{0.02} - 1 = 0.15 - 1 = -0.85 \text{ LSB}
$$

### Step 4: Check Monotonicity

$$
\text{DNL}(128) = -0.85 \text{ LSB} \geq -1 \text{ LSB} \quad \checkmark \text{ Monotonic}
$$

If DNL had been less than -1 LSB (e.g., -1.2 LSB), the output would have **decreased**, breaking monotonicity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Specification Dependency Tree

| Specification | Depends On | Affects | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution (n) | Architecture | All specs | 8–24 bits |
| LSB (Δ) | n, $V_{REF}$ | SNR, INL, DNL | μV–mV |
| INL | Matching, layout | Linearity | ±0.5 to ±16 LSB |
| DNL | Matching | Monotonicity | ±0.5 to ±1 LSB |
| Settling Time | RC, slew rate | Glitch, SFDR | ns–μs |
| SNR | n, jitter | SINAD | 50–110 dB |
| SFDR | Glitch, linearity | ENOB | 60–120 dBc |
| ENOB | SNR, THD | Usable resolution | < n bits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define INL and DNL of a DAC. How are they related to monotonicity? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer**:
- **INL (Integral Non-Linearity)**: The maximum deviation of the actual DAC output from the ideal straight-line transfer characteristic, expressed in LSB units.
- **DNL (Differential Non-Linearity)**: The deviation of the actual step size between adjacent codes from the ideal 1-LSB step, expressed in LSB units.
- **Monotonicity Condition**: A DAC is monotonic if DNL ≥ -1 LSB for every code. A DNL of less than -1 LSB at any code causes the output to decrease with increasing input code, violating monotonicity.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Question 2: What is ENOB? Derive its relationship with SINAD. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer**:
- **ENOB (Effective Number of Bits)**: The actual usable resolution of a non-ideal DAC after accounting for noise and distortion.
- **Derivation**: Starting from $\text{SINAD} = 6.02 \cdot \text{ENOB} + 1.76$, solving for ENOB:

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}
$$

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Question A (a)**: With the help of a neat diagram, explain the static transfer characteristics of an ideal 3-bit DAC. Define and derive expressions for Resolution, Full-Scale Range, and LSB Weight. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

**Diagram**:
```
V_OUT
  |
V_FS - - - - - - - - - - - - o (Code 111)
  |                           
  |                   o (Code 110)
  |            o (Code 101)
  |     o (Code 100)
  |o (Code 011)
  | o (Code 010)
  |  o (Code 001)
  |___o_(Code 000)___________________ Digital Code
      0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7
```

**Derivation**:
1. **Resolution**: Number of discrete levels $L = 2^n = 2^3 = 8$.
2. **Full-Scale Range**: $V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot (2^n - 1)/2^n = V_{REF} \cdot 7/8$.
3. **LSB Weight**: $\Delta = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1) = V_{REF}/8$.
4. **Ideal Output**: $V_{OUT}(D) = D \cdot \Delta$.

**Question A (b)**: A 10-bit DAC has $V_{REF} = 5.12$ V. Calculate: (i) LSB weight, (ii) Full-Scale output, (iii) Output voltage for input code 512, and (iv) Ideal SNR. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

**(i) LSB Weight**:
$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{5.12}{1024} = 5 \text{ mV}
$$

**(ii) Full-Scale Output**:
$$
V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2^{10}-1}{2^{10}} = 5.12 \cdot \frac{1023}{1024} \approx 5.115 \text{ V}
$$

**(iii) Output for Code 512**:
$$
V_{OUT} = 512 \times 5 \text{ mV} = 2.56 \text{ V}
$$

**(iv) Ideal SNR**:
$$
\text{SNR} = 6.02(10) + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}
$$

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Question B (a)**: Explain the following dynamic specifications of a DAC with relevant equations: (i) Settling Time, (ii) Glitch Energy, (iii) SFDR, and (iv) THD. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

**(i) Settling Time ($t_s$)**: Time required for the DAC output to settle within a specified error band (typically $\pm 0.5$ LSB or $\pm 0.1\%$) of its final value after a full-scale code transition. Depends on RC time constants and slew rate of the output amplifier.

**(ii) Glitch Energy**: Unwanted transient voltage impulses appearing at the output during major code transitions (e.g., 0111...1 → 1000...0) due to mismatched switching times. Measured in V·s (Volt-seconds) as the area under the glitch waveform.

**(iii) SFDR**: 
$$
\text{SFDR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\text{fundamental, rms}}}{V_{\text{largest spur, rms}}}\right)
$$

**(iv) THD**:
$$
\text{THD (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \dots + V_k^2}}{V_1}\right)
$$

**Question B (b)**: A 12-bit DAC is tested with a full-scale sinusoid. The measured SINAD is 68 dB. Calculate: (i) ENOB, (ii) The SNR penalty in dB compared to ideal, and (iii) The quantization noise power if $V_{REF} = 5$ V. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

**(i) ENOB**:
$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{68 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{66.24}{6.02} \approx 11.00 \text{ bits}
$$

**(ii) SNR Penalty**:
$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 73.99 \text{ dB}
$$
$$
\text{Penalty} = 73.99 - 68 = 5.99 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iii) Quantization Noise Power**:
$$
\Delta = \frac{5}{4096} \approx 1.221 \text{ mV}
$$
$$
P_Q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(1.221 \times 10^{-3})^2}{12} \approx 1.24 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V}^2
$$

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DAC Specification Questions**:
> 1. **LSB Formula Confusion**: Students often write $\Delta = V_{REF}/(2^n - 1)$ instead of $V_{REF}/2^n$ — verify your textbook convention before the exam.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.76 dB Constant**: The SNR formula is $6.02N + 1.76$ dB, NOT $6.02N$ alone. Missing the 1.76 costs **1 full mark** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Monotonicity Direction**: DNL < -1 LSB breaks monotonicity. Students sometimes incorrectly state "DNL = 0 implies non-monotonic" — this is wrong; DNL = 0 is the *ideal* linear case.
> 4. **Units of Quantization Noise**: $P_Q$ is in $V^2$, NOT in $V$. Express in V² or convert to dBm when required.
> 5. **ENOB Boundary**: ENOB can never exceed the nominal resolution N. If your calculation gives ENOB > N, you have made a sign or arithmetic error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Resolution (n)** determines the number of discrete output levels: $L = 2^n$.
- **LSB Weight** $\Delta = V_{REF}/2^n$ is the fundamental analog increment.
- **Offset Error** is the output at code zero; **Gain Error** is the slope deviation.
- **INL** measures deviation from the ideal straight line; **DNL** measures step-size deviation.
- **Monotonicity** requires DNL ≥ -1 LSB for all codes.
- **Ideal SNR** = $6.02N + 1.76$ dB — a cornerstone formula for KTU exams.
- **ENOB** = $(\text{SINAD} - 1.76)/6.02$ quantifies actual usable resolution.
- **SFDR** and **THD** are dynamic metrics critical for high-speed communication DACs.
- **Quantization Noise Power** $P_Q = \Delta^2/12$ is the theoretical noise floor.
- **Settling Time** and **Glitch Energy** dominate high-speed DAC performance.
- Always express errors in **LSB units** unless explicitly asked otherwise.
- Remember the **factor of 2** distinction: log power uses $10\log$, log voltage uses $20\log$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC Specifications, DAC Architecture - Resistor String
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ADC Specifications, DAC Architecture - Resistor String

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 ADC — Formal Definition
An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is a mixed-signal system block that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog input signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital output code. The conversion is governed by two fundamental operations: **sampling** (in time) and **quantization** (in amplitude).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim intent):** *An ADC is a device that maps a continuous range of input voltages into a finite set of discrete binary codes, characterized by resolution N (in bits), sampling rate fs, and a family of static/dynamic performance metrics.*

### 1.2 DAC — Formal Definition
A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** performs the inverse operation: it maps a discrete binary input word of N bits into one of $2^N$ discrete analog output levels (typically voltages). The **Resistor String DAC** is the most elementary, inherently monotonic voltage-mode DAC architecture, in which a string of $2^N$ equal-value resistors is placed between the reference voltages $V_{REF+}$ and $V_{REF-}$, and a decoder selects one of the $2^N + 1$ tap voltages to drive the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Property:** The resistor string DAC is **inherently monotonic** because increasing digital code always selects a tap at a higher (or equal) potential along the string. This property makes it indispensable as a building block inside sub-ranging, pipelined, and flash ADCs.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making Sense of It

**ADC Analogy — The Weighing Scale with Coarse Resolution:**
Imagine a digital weighing scale that can only display whole kilograms. If you place a 3.4 kg object on it, it shows "3". The **quantization error** is 0.4 kg — the scale cannot resolve it. Now, if the scale is upgraded to display 0.1 kg steps, the resolution improves. ADC specifications (DNL, INL, ENOB) are essentially ways of describing how *faithful* the scale is to the true weight.

**Resistor-String DAC Analogy — The Flight of Stairs:**
Think of a tall ladder between two floors (top = $V_{REF+}$, bottom = $V_{REF-}$). Each rung represents a discrete voltage level. A digital code (the "step number") selects which rung you stand on. The voltage at each rung is set by where the rung sits in the ladder — the top rung is high voltage, the bottom is low. Just as a ladder has $2^N$ evenly spaced rungs, an N-bit resistor-string DAC has $2^N$ equal resistors creating $2^N + 1$ equally spaced voltage taps. A **decode switch** is the foot that chooses which rung to stand on.

### 1.4 The Critical ADC Specifications (KTU 2024 Curriculum)

| Specification | Symbol | One-line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | N | Number of bits in the output code |
| **Least Significant Bit (voltage)** | $V_{LSB}$ | Smallest resolvable analog step |
| **Full-Scale Range** | FSR | $V_{REF} - V_{REF-}$ in volts |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | SNR | Signal power vs. total noise power (dB) |
| **Signal-to-Noise + Distortion** | SNDR / SINAD | Signal vs. noise + harmonics (dB) |
| **Effective Number Of Bits** | ENOB | Realized resolution after noise (bits) |
| **Spurious-Free Dynamic Range** | SFDR | dB difference between fundamental and largest spur |
| **Differential Non-Linearity** | DNL | Deviation of actual step from ideal 1 LSB |
| **Integral Non-Linearity** | INL | Cumulative deviation from ideal straight line |
| **Sampling Rate** | $f_S$ | Conversions per second (Hz / Samples per second) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden KTU Rule:** The boundary between a "good" and "bad" ADC for any application is set by **SNDR** and **SFDR**, NOT just the resolution N. A 12-bit ADC with 60 dB SNDR is effectively only a 10-bit converter (ENOB ≈ 9.6 bits).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Resolution, LSB and Full-Scale Range

An **N-bit ADC** partitions the analog input range FSR into $2^N$ equal sub-ranges.

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^N} = \frac{V_{REF} - V_{REF-}}{2^N}
$$

The **digital output code D** (unsigned, $0 \le D \le 2^N - 1$) maps to:

$$
V_{OUT,ideal} = V_{REF-} + D \cdot V_{LSB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **signed bipolar** converter, the mid-scale (code $2^{N-1}$) represents 0 V, and codes $0$ to $2^N - 1$ swing from $-V_{REF}/2$ to $+V_{REF}/2$ approximately.

### 2.2 Quantization Noise — The Fundamental Limit

A real ADC introduces an unavoidable **quantization error** $e_q$ bounded by:

$$
-\frac{V_{LSB}}{2} \le e_q \le +\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}
$$

Treating $e_q$ as a uniform random variable of width $V_{LSB}$, the **mean-square quantization noise power** is:

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{V_{LSB}^2}{12}
$$

This is the bedrock upon which all SNR derivations rest.

### 2.3 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) — The Heart of the Course

For a **full-scale sinusoidal input** of peak amplitude $V_{FSR}/2$:

$$
P_{signal} = \frac{V_{p}^2}{2} = \frac{(V_{FSR}/2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FSR}^2}{8}
$$

Substituting $V_{LSB} = V_{FSR}/2^N$ and dividing:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{FSR}^2 / 8}{V_{LSB}^2 / 12} = \frac{12 \cdot V_{FSR}^2}{8 \cdot (V_{FSR}^2/2^{2N})} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}
$$

In decibels, this yields the legendary KTU-grade formula:

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{ideal} = 6.02 \, N + 1.76 \;\; \text{dB}}
$$

### 2.4 Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)

When measured SNR is known:

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SNR}_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}
$$

This expresses "how many truly clean bits" the converter delivers.

### 2.5 DNL and INL — Static Linearity Errors

For the $k^{th}$ code transition:
- **DNL(k):** $\text{DNL}(k) = \dfrac{V_{T}(k+1) - V_{T}(k)}{V_{LSB,ideal}} - 1$
- **INL(k):** $\text{INL}(k) = \dfrac{V_{T}(k) - V_{T,ideal}(k)}{V_{LSB,ideal}}$

> [!WARNING]
> A DNL(k) of $-1$ LSB means the code $k$ is **missing** (a "sparkle code" or "non-monotonic" step). In a flash ADC this is catastrophic; in a resistor-string DAC this is impossible due to monotonicity.

### 2.6 Resistor-String DAC — Architecture and Operation

The architecture consists of:
1. A series string of $2^N$ identical resistors, $R$, between $V_{REF+}$ (top) and $V_{REF-}$ (bottom).
2. A **row decoder** (2-to-$2^N$ one-hot decoder) that activates one of $2^N$ switches.
3. The selected switch routes one of the $2^N + 1$ tap voltages to the output buffer.

Each resistor drops a voltage:
$$
V_{LSB,DAC} = \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{2^N}
$$

For digital input code $D$:
$$
V_{OUT}(D) = V_{REF-} + D \cdot V_{LSB,DAC} = V_{REF-} + D \cdot \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{2^N}
$$

The total resistance is $R_{total} = 2^N \cdot R$, and the quiescent current is:
$$
I_{ref} = \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{2^N \cdot R}
$$

### 2.7 Why Resistor-String DAC? Pros and Cons

| Property | Resistor String |
|---|---|
| Monotonicity | **Guaranteed** (selecting higher code always picks higher tap) |
| INL / DNL | Excellent (DNL $\le 0.5$ LSB) |
| Static Power | Proportional to $2^N$ — **scales poorly with N** |
| Area | $2^N$ resistors and a large decoder — **area-hungry** |
| Settling Time | Limited by string RC and output buffer — moderate |
| Typical N | Up to 8–10 bits (used as sub-DAC inside larger converters) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Summary:** Resistor string is rarely used standalone above 8 bits due to $2^N$ resistor area. However, it is the **sub-DAC of choice** in 8-bit segments of **sub-ranging** and **pipelined ADCs**, and as the **MDAC sub-DAC** in algorithmic converters.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| LSB voltage | $V_{LSB} = V_{FSR}/2^N$ | Volts (V) |
| Quantization noise power | $V_{LSB}^2 / 12$ | $V^2$ |
| Ideal SNR | $6.02\,N + 1.76$ | Decibels (dB) |
| SNR in linear | $1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| ENOB | $(SNR_{dB} - 1.76)/6.02$ | Bits |
| Dynamic Range (ideal) | $20 \log_{10}(2^N)$ | dB |
| Resistor string LSB | $(V_{REF+} - V_{REF-})/2^N$ | Volts (V) |
| Resistor string current | $V_{FSR}/(2^N \cdot R)$ | Amperes (A) |
| String total resistance | $2^N \cdot R$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Max DNL (string) | $\vert 0.5 \vert$ LSB ideal; can be worse with mismatch | LSB |
| SFDR | $20 \log_{10}(V_{signal}/V_{largest\_spur})$ | dBc |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - N-bit converter $\Rightarrow 2^N$ codes, $2^N + 1$ transition points in a resistor string.
> - For monotonicity: $V_{OUT}(D+1) \ge V_{OUT}(D)$ for all D — automatically satisfied by the string.
> - For Nyquist sampling: $f_S \ge 2 f_{in,max}$ (Shannon-Nyquist theorem).
> - For pure quantization-limited SNR, $f_S$ must be $\ge 2.76$ times the input frequency (Coherent sampling rule).

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Resistor-String DACs** are universally used as the reference ladder and sub-DAC inside the **MDAC** of pipelined ADCs (e.g., 14-/16-bit pipeline stages).
- ADC specifications like **ENOB** and **SFDR** are first-class figures of merit in **digital oscilloscopes, software-defined radios (SDR), medical ultrasound, and high-fidelity audio codecs**.
- Mastering these specifications is essential for **mixed-signal IC layout review**, **test bench characterization**, and **system-level noise budgeting**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ideal SNR = 6.02 N + 1.76 dB

**Step 1 — Define the full-scale input sinusoid.**

Let the analog input be:
$$
v_{in}(t) = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2} \sin(2 \pi f_{in} t)
$$

This peak amplitude exactly fills the converter's range (clipping-safe).

**Step 2 — Compute the signal power averaged over a period.**

For a sinusoid, $P_{signal} = V_{p}^2 / 2$:
$$
P_{signal} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{V_{FSR}}{2} \right)^2 = \frac{V_{FSR}^2}{8}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the LSB voltage in terms of full-scale range.**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^N}
$$

**Step 4 — Quantization noise power (uniform distribution on $[-V_{LSB}/2, V_{LSB}/2]$).**

The variance of a uniform random variable on $[a,b]$ is $(b-a)^2/12$. Here $b-a = V_{LSB}$:
$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{V_{LSB}^2}{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the SNR as a linear ratio.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = \frac{P_{signal}}{\overline{e_q^2}} = \frac{V_{FSR}^2/8}{V_{LSB}^2/12} = \frac{V_{FSR}^2}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{V_{LSB}^2} = \frac{12}{8} \cdot \frac{V_{FSR}^2}{V_{LSB}^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the LSB expression.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{FSR}^2}{V_{FSR}^2/2^{2N}} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to decibels.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 N \log_{10}(2)
$$

Using $10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.7609$ dB and $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.0206$ dB:

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \, N + 1.76 \;\; \text{dB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Every additional bit** improves SNR by exactly **6.02 dB** — a fundamental KTU result that must be memorized.

### 3.2 Derivation: Resistor-String DAC Output Voltage

**Step 1 — Tap voltages along the string.**

The string consists of $2^N$ resistors of value $R$ in series. Total string resistance:
$$
R_{string} = 2^N \cdot R
$$

**Step 2 — Quiescent reference current (no load on string):**

$$
I_{ref} = \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{R_{string}} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^N \cdot R}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage drop across any one resistor (the DAC's LSB):**

$$
V_{LSB,DAC} = I_{ref} \cdot R = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^N}
$$

**Step 4 — Tap voltage at the bottom of the $k^{th}$ resistor (counted from ground):**

$$
V_{tap}(k) = V_{REF-} + k \cdot V_{LSB,DAC} = V_{REF-} + k \cdot \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^N}
$$

**Step 5 — When digital code $D$ selects the $D^{th}$ tap from the bottom:**

$$
V_{OUT}(D) = V_{REF-} + D \cdot \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^N}, \quad D \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^N - 1\}
$$

**Step 6 — Output swing check:**
- $D = 0 \Rightarrow V_{OUT} = V_{REF-}$ ✓
- $D = 2^N - 1 \Rightarrow V_{OUT} = V_{REF-} + (2^N - 1) \cdot V_{FSR}/2^N = V_{REF+} - V_{LSB}$ ✓

The string never outputs exactly $V_{REF+}$, by design — this avoids switch ON-resistance drop errors at the top.

### 3.3 Worked Example: 10-bit ADC/DAC, $V_{REF} = 3.3$ V

**Step 1 — LSB voltage:**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{2^{10}} = \frac{3.3}{1024} \approx 3.223 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Ideal SNR:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{ideal} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — For a resistor-string DAC, R chosen as $100 \;\Omega$:**

Total string resistance:
$$
R_{string} = 1024 \times 100 = 102.4 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

Quiescent current:
$$
I_{ref} = \frac{3.3}{102.4 \text{ k}} = 32.23 \;\mu\text{A}
$$

Static power dissipation:
$$
P_{static} = V_{FSR} \cdot I_{ref} = 3.3 \cdot 32.23 \;\mu\text{A} = 106.4 \;\mu\text{W}
$$

**Step 4 — If measured SNDR is 58 dB, find ENOB:**

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{58 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{56.24}{6.02} \approx 9.34 \text{ bits}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The 10-bit ADC is effectively delivering only 9.34 clean bits. The loss of ~0.66 bits comes from clock jitter, comparator offset, finite op-amp gain, and thermal noise.

### 3.4 Derivation: ENOB from SFDR (Alternate Path)

In applications where distortion dominates noise (e.g., high-frequency ADCs):
$$
\text{ENOB} \approx \frac{\text{SFDR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}
$$

This is an exam-favourite KTU simplification and is a direct application of the SNR-ENOB formula with SNR replaced by SFDR.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: ADC Specification Inter-Relationships

| If You Measure... | You Can Compute | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| N (architecture bits) | Ideal SNR | $6.02N + 1.76$ |
| Measured SNR (dB) | ENOB | $(SNR - 1.76)/6.02$ |
| Measured SNDR (dB) | ENOB (most accurate) | $(SNDR - 1.76)/6.02$ |
| $V_{LSB}$, $V_{FSR}$ | N | $\log_2(V_{FSR}/V_{LSB})$ |
| $f_S$, $f_{in}$ | Coherent? | Ratio must be rational integer |
| $R$, $V_{FSR}$, N | Static current | $V_{FSR}/(2^N R)$ |
| $V_{tap}(D)$, $V_{REF-}$, $V_{REF+}$ | Code D | $D = (V_{OUT} - V_{REF-}) / V_{LSB}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Resistor-String DAC — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    vrefp[VREF PLUS TOP RAIL]
    vrefn[VREF MINUS BOTTOM RAIL]
    stringA[RESISTOR STRING 2 TO N EQUAL R RESISTORS IN SERIES]
    tapsA[TAP NODES 2 TO N PLUS 1 TAPS]
    decA[N TO 2 TO N ROW DECODER]
    swA[ANALOG SWITCH TREE ONE HOT SELECTED]
    bufA[OUTPUT UNITY GAIN BUFFER]
    outA[ANALOG V OUT]
    vrefp --> stringA
    stringA --> vrefn
    stringA --> tapsA
    tapsA --> swA
    decA --> swA
    swA --> bufA
    bufA --> outA
```

### 4.2 ADC Specifications — Relationship Flowchart

```mermaid
graph LR
    archBits[ARCHITECTURE BITS N]
    idealSNR[IDEAL SNR 6.02 N PLUS 1.76 dB]
    measSNR[MEASURED SNR]
    measSNDR[MEASURED SNDR]
    measSFDR[MEASURED SFDR]
    enobA[ENOB FROM SNR]
    enobB[ENOB FROM SNDR]
    enobC[ENOB FROM SFDR]
    appBits[EFFECTIVE APPLICATION BITS]
    archBits --> idealSNR
    measSNR --> enobA
    measSNDR --> enobB
    measSFDR --> enobC
    enobA --> appBits
    enobB --> appBits
    enobC --> appBits
    idealSNR --> appBits
```

### 4.3 ADC Functional Pipeline — Where Specs Apply

```mermaid
graph TD
    scaa[ANTI ALIAS FILTER]
    shaA[SAMPLE AND HOLD SHA]
    quantA[QUANTIZER 2 TO N LEVELS]
    encA[DIGITAL ENCODER]
    outBits[N BIT DIGITAL CODE]
    scaa --> shaA
    shaA --> quantA
    quantA --> encA
    encA --> outBits
    noiseSide[NOISE SOURCES JITTER, KT C, COMPARATOR OFFSET] -.-> shaA
    noiseSide -.-> quantA
```

### 4.4 Resistor-String Tap Selection — Conceptual View (3-bit example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rail3[VREF PLUS]
    r3a[R]
    r3b[R]
    r3c[R]
    r3d[R]
    r3e[R]
    r3f[R]
    r3g[R]
    r3h[R]
    rail0[VREF MINUS]
    dec3[3 TO 8 ONE HOT DECODER]
    out3[V OUT]
    rail3 --> r3a --> n3a[TAP 1]
    r3a --> r3b --> n3b[TAP 2]
    r3b --> r3c --> n3c[TAP 3]
    r3c --> r3d --> n3d[TAP 4]
    r3d --> r3e --> n3e[TAP 5]
    r3e --> r3f --> n3f[TAP 6]
    r3f --> r3g --> n3g[TAP 7]
    r3g --> r3h --> rail0
    dec3 --> out3
    n3a -.-> out3
    n3b -.-> out3
    n3c -.-> out3
    n3d -.-> out3
    n3e -.-> out3
    n3f -.-> out3
    n3g -.-> out3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the schematic:** The decoder enables one of 8 switches, connecting exactly one of the 7 inner taps to $V_{OUT}$. The two end taps ($V_{REF+}$ and $V_{REF-}$) are reference rails and are not normally switched in directly, since the topmost tap is at $V_{REF+} - V_{LSB}$ rather than $V_{REF+}$ itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Differential Non-Linearity (DNL) and Integral Non-Linearity (INL) of an ADC. Why is INL a cumulative measure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **DNL(k):** The difference between the actual step size at the $k^{th}$ code transition and the ideal step size of 1 LSB, expressed in LSB units. $DNL(k) = [V_T(k+1) - V_T(k)]/V_{LSB,ideal} - 1$.
- **INL(k):** The cumulative deviation of the actual $k^{th}$ transition voltage from its ideal straight-line position, expressed in LSB units. $INL(k) = [V_T(k) - V_{T,ideal}(k)]/V_{LSB,ideal}$.
- INL is cumulative because every transition's *position* is affected by the accumulated errors of all *previous* DNLs: $INL(k) = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} DNL(i)$. A small positive DNL followed by a small negative DNL can still leave a residual INL error. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is the Resistor-String DAC inherently monotonic? Mention one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Inherent monotonicity:** The output voltage is taken from a tap of a *passive* resistor ladder. As the digital input code increases by 1, the decoder selects a tap strictly *above* the previous one along the string. Therefore $V_{OUT}(D+1) \ge V_{OUT}(D)$ for all D, regardless of resistor mismatch (mismatch only affects linearity, not monotonicity).
- **Limitation (any one, 1 mark):**
  (a) Component count grows as $2^N$ — impractical beyond 8–10 bits due to silicon area.
  (b) Quiescent current flows continuously through the entire string — high static power dissipation.
  (c) Large output capacitance from the switch tree limits high-speed operation.

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 scheme: Part B questions on a module carry 14 marks and offer internal choice. Students must answer **one** of the two alternatives provided.*

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **ideal Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** of an N-bit ADC, starting from the assumption that quantization error is uniformly distributed. Hence compute the ideal SNR for a 12-bit converter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operation of a **3-bit Resistor-String DAC**. Show that its output for code $D$ is $V_{OUT}(D) = V_{REF-} + D \cdot (V_{FSR}/8)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Sinusoidal full-scale input has amplitude $V_{FSR}/2$, so signal power is $P_s = V_{FSR}^2/8$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Quantization error $e_q$ is uniform on $[-V_{LSB}/2, +V_{LSB}/2]$, giving $\overline{e_q^2} = V_{LSB}^2/12$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_{LSB} = V_{FSR}/2^N$ and form the ratio:

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = \frac{V_{FSR}^2/8}{V_{LSB}^2/12} = \frac{12}{8} \cdot \frac{V_{FSR}^2}{V_{FSR}^2/2^{2N}} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4:** Convert to dB: $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20N\log_{10}(2) = 6.02N + 1.76$. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5:** For N = 12: $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0$ dB. *(1 mark)*

---

**Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Architecture:** A 3-bit resistor-string DAC uses $2^3 = 8$ equal resistors of value $R$ connected in series between $V_{REF+}$ and $V_{REF-}$. A 3-to-8 one-hot decoder drives 8 CMOS switches; the closed switch connects its tap to the output buffer. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Reference current:**

$$
I_{ref} = \frac{V_{REF+} - V_{REF-}}{8R} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{8R}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Voltage per resistor (LSB):**

$$
V_{LSB} = I_{ref} \cdot R = \frac{V_{FSR}}{8}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Output for code D:**
The decoder routes the $D^{th}$ tap (counted from the bottom) to the output:

$$
V_{OUT}(D) = V_{REF-} + D \cdot \frac{V_{FSR}}{8}, \quad D \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 7\}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Verification:** $D=0$ gives $V_{REF-}$; $D=7$ gives $V_{REF-} + 7 V_{FSR}/8 = V_{REF+} - V_{LSB}$. ✓ *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Define **ENOB** and **SFDR**. An ADC is measured to have SNDR = 60 dB. Find the **ENOB**. If the largest spurious tone is observed at −70 dBc with the fundamental at −2 dBFS, compute the **SFDR** in dBc. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the resistor-string DAC is **inherently monotonic**. A 6-bit resistor-string DAC operates between $V_{REF-} = 0$ V and $V_{REF+} = 2.5$ V using $R = 1$ k$\Omega$ resistors. Find (i) the LSB voltage, (ii) the quiescent current, and (iii) the static power dissipation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**
- **ENOB (Effective Number of Bits):** The equivalent resolution of a real ADC after accounting for noise and distortion, computed from measured SNDR.
- **SFDR (Spurious-Free Dynamic Range):** The ratio, in dB, between the RMS amplitude of the fundamental output tone and the amplitude of the largest spurious tone (harmonic or otherwise) in the output spectrum.

**Step 2 — ENOB from SNDR (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SNDR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{60 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{58.24}{6.02} \approx 9.67 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — SFDR computation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SFDR} = P_{fundamental,dBc} - P_{largest\_spur,dBc} = (-2) - (-70) = 68 \text{ dBc}
$$

**Step 4 — Insight (1 mark):** Even though SNDR gives 9.67 bits, SFDR = 68 dB corresponds to $\text{ENOB}_{SFDR} = (68-1.76)/6.02 = 11.0$ bits. Hence distortion (not noise) is the dominant limitation in this converter — a classic KTU trade-off observation.

---

**Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Inherent Monotonicity (2 marks):** The resistor string is a *passive* voltage divider. Each successive code selects a tap point that is physically *higher* on the string. Hence $V_{OUT}(D+1) \ge V_{OUT}(D)$ is *guaranteed by the topology*, independent of resistor matching. Only INL/DNL are affected by mismatch — never the monotonicity.

**Step 2 — LSB Voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^6} = \frac{2.5 \text{ V}}{64} = 39.0625 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Quiescent Current (2 marks):**

$$
I_{ref} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^6 \cdot R} = \frac{2.5}{64 \times 1000} = \frac{2.5}{64000} = 39.0625 \;\mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 4 — Static Power Dissipation (1 mark):**

$$
P_{static} = V_{FSR} \cdot I_{ref} = 2.5 \times 39.0625 \;\mu\text{A} = 97.66 \;\mu\text{W}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.76 dB constant** in the SNR formula. It comes from $10\log_{10}(1.5)$ — write it explicitly; don't combine it into a "rounded 6.02".
> 2. **Mixing up SFDR and SNR units.** SFDR is in **dBc** (dB below carrier), SNR is in **dBFS** or **dB**. They are not the same!
> 3. **String DAC static current:** Many students forget that current flows *continuously* even when output is held, because the string is always across $V_{FSR}$. This is why high-N string DACs are power-hungry.
> 4. **Confusing DNL sign convention.** A negative DNL means a *smaller* step (or missing code), not a larger one.
> 5. **ENOB vs. Architecture N:** N is the *design* parameter; ENOB is the *measured* performance. Conflating them loses 2 marks easily.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **LSB voltage:** $V_{LSB} = V_{FSR}/2^N$ — apply to both ADC and DAC.
- ✅ **Ideal SNR:** $6.02 N + 1.76$ dB; each extra bit adds ~6 dB.
- ✅ **ENOB:** $(SNDR_{dB} - 1.76)/6.02$ — always use SNDR, not SNR, for the most truthful figure.
- ✅ **SFDR:** Difference (dB) between fundamental and largest spur; reported in **dBc**.
- ✅ **DNL/INL:** DNL is *local*; INL is *cumulative* (sum of DNLs).
- ✅ **Resistor-String DAC:** $2^N$ equal resistors, $2^N+1$ taps, decoder selects one tap.
- ✅ **Inherent monotonicity** is the #1 reason resistor string is used as a sub-DAC.
- ✅ **Quiescent current:** $V_{FSR}/(2^N R)$ flows *continuously* — limits scalability to high N.
- ✅ **Resistor string output:** $V_{OUT}(D) = V_{REF-} + D \cdot V_{LSB}$.
- ✅ **Shannon–Nyquist:** $f_S \ge 2 f_{in,max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- ✅ **Coherent sampling:** $f_S/f_{in}$ must be a rational number with prime numerator — for clean spectral testing.
- ✅ **Memorize:** $20\log_{10}(2) = 6.02$ dB, $10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76$ dB.
- ✅ **Pipeline / sub-ranging ADCs** use resistor-string as the *segment* sub-DAC — high-yield KTU link.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R-2R Ladder Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** R-2R Ladder Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **R-2R Ladder Network** is a passive resistor network comprising only two standardized resistance values — a base resistance **R** and its exact double **2R** — arranged in a repetitive cascaded topology. In mixed-signal integrated circuit design, it functions as the fundamental building block of a **current-steering Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)**, where each binary-weighted bit current is precisely halved as it propagates toward the output node, producing an analog output voltage proportional to the digital input word.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> *R-2R ladder networks are resistor networks employing only two resistance values (R and 2R) configured in a ladder topology. They are extensively used in DAC architectures to perform binary-weighted current division with high precision, requiring only matched resistor ratios rather than absolute resistance values — making them ideal for monolithic IC fabrication.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water-distribution network** in a high-rise apartment:
- Every apartment gets a **standardized pipe size (2R)** for its water supply.
- At every floor junction, a **reducer pipe (R)** is installed that splits the flow into two equal halves — one going to the current floor and one passing down to the next floor.
- The result: even if 10, 20, or 100 apartments are connected, you **only ever need pipes of two standard sizes** (R and 2R), but the total water flow at the main inlet is a *weighted sum* of all the apartment demands, where apartments on higher floors (MSB) contribute twice as much flow as the one below them (next bit).

This is exactly how an R-2R ladder works — every binary bit is converted into a current, and at each ladder node, the current is **binary-weighted** by halving, producing a precise analog sum at the output.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **R (base resistance)** | **2.5 kΩ to 10 kΩ** | Common in CMOS DACs |
| **2R (ladder resistance)** | **2 × R** | Always exactly double of R |
| **Bit resolution (n)** | **8, 10, 12, 16 bits (typical)** | Determines DAC precision |
| **Reference voltage (V_ref)** | **2.5 V to 5 V** | Full-scale reference |
| **LSB voltage step** | **V_ref / 2^n** | Smallest analog increment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why R-2R and not R, 2R, 4R, 8R...?**
> In a weighted-resistor DAC, you need resistors of values R, 2R, 4R, 8R ... 2^(n-1)R. For a 12-bit DAC, the MSB resistor is **2048 × R** — meaning a 2048:1 ratio. This is virtually **impossible to manufacture accurately** on a single IC die. The R-2R ladder sidesteps this by only requiring a **2:1 ratio**, which is trivially achieved in monolithic fabrication using identical unit resistors in series/parallel combinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Composition

The R-2R ladder network consists of two interleaving resistor sets:

1. **Longitudinal (series) resistors** of value **R** — placed along the top rail, one per bit.
2. **Shunt (termination) resistors** of value **2R** — connected from each ladder node to ground (or to a virtual ground op-amp input).
3. **A final terminating 2R** resistor at the end of the ladder to ensure the input impedance remains constant at **2R** from any node looking right.

### 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Golden Rules of R-2R Ladder Analysis:**
> 1. **Input Resistance Invariance:** Looking into the ladder from *any* node toward the right, the equivalent resistance is always **2R** (independent of bit count).
> 2. **Binary Halving:** At each node, the current splits into two equal halves (since the right-side equivalent resistance is 2R and the shunt 2R forms a 1:1 current divider).
> 3. **Current Summation:** All binary-weighted currents converge at the summing node (virtual ground of op-amp) and are linearly summed by the I-V converter.

#### Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Bit-to-Current Conversion:**
  Each digital bit $b_i$ controls a switch that connects its corresponding 2R shunt resistor either to the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ (logic '1') or to ground (logic '0').

- **Step 2 — Binary-Weighted Current Generation:**
  The current injected by the MSB switch is $I_{MSB} = V_{ref} / (2R)$, the next bit contributes $V_{ref} / (4R)$, the next $V_{ref} / (8R)$, and so on.

- **Step 3 — Current Steering via Ladder:**
  As these currents traverse the ladder, each R-2R junction divides the current by 2, ensuring the MSB contribution is always exactly double the next bit.

- **Step 4 — Summation at Output Node:**
  An inverting op-amp configured as a transimpedance amplifier (current-to-voltage converter) sums all currents and converts them to an output voltage.

- **Step 5 — Analog Output Generation:**
  The output voltage is a precise analog replica of the digital input word.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equivalent input resistance | $R_{eq} = 2R$ | Constant at any ladder node looking right | Ω (Ohms) |
| Current division ratio at each node | $I_{right} / I_{in} = 1/2$ | Binary halving factor | dimensionless |
| MSB injected current | $I_{MSB} = V_{ref} / (2R)$ | Current from most significant bit | A (Amperes) |
| LSB injected current | $I_{LSB} = V_{ref} / (2^n \cdot 2R)$ | Current from least significant bit | A (Amperes) |
| Decimal value of digital word | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{n-i}$ | Binary-to-decimal conversion | dimensionless |
| Output voltage (current-mode) | $V_{out} = -I_{sum} \cdot R_f$ | From transimpedance amplifier | V (Volts) |
| Output voltage (closed form) | $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{R} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ | Complete DAC transfer equation | V (Volts) |
| LSB voltage (step size) | $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$ | Resolution of DAC | V (Volts) |
| Full-scale output (all 1s) | $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot (1 - 1/2^n)$ | Maximum analog output | V (Volts) |
| Matching requirement | $R_{MSB} : R_{LSB} = 1 : 2^{n-1}$ | For equivalent weighted-resistor DAC | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

R-2R ladder networks are the workhorse of:
- **Audio DACs** in smartphones, sound cards, and hearing aids (16-24 bit resolution).
- **Video DACs** in graphics processing and display drivers.
- **Programmable Gain Amplifiers (PGAs)** where digital control of attenuation is required.
- **Digital Potentiometers** (digipots) used as electronically controlled variable resistors.
- **Function Generators** and arbitrary waveform generators.
- **Industrial Process Control** systems requiring precise analog setpoints from microcontrollers.
- **Automotive ECU** sensor calibration and actuator control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance:** Nearly every commercial CMOS DAC (Analog Devices AD5541, TI DAC8560, Maxim MAX521) uses an R-2R architecture because it can be fabricated in standard CMOS processes without laser trimming, and its linearity depends only on **resistor matching** (which modern processes achieve to < 0.1%).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Resistance of the R-2R Ladder (Right-Looking Impedance)

**Objective:** Prove that looking into any node of the R-2R ladder toward the right (toward the LSB end), the equivalent resistance is always $2R$, regardless of the ladder's length.

**Step 1 — Start at the LSB End (Termination):**

The rightmost node has the terminating resistor $2R$ connected from the node to ground, with the next resistor $R$ in series to the right (no further bits). Looking into the last node, the resistance is simply $2R \parallel 2R$:

$$R_{last} = \frac{2R \cdot 2R}{2R + 2R} = \frac{4R^2}{4R} = R$$

**Step 2 — Add the Series R to Get Node-N Equivalent:**

Now moving one node to the left, we have a series resistor $R$ followed by the previously calculated $R_{last} = R$, all in parallel with the shunt $2R$ at the new node:

$$R_{node-N} = 2R \parallel (R + R_{last}) = 2R \parallel 2R = \frac{2R \cdot 2R}{2R + 2R} = R$$

**Step 3 — General Recursive Formulation:**

By induction, for the $k$-th node from the right:

$$R_{node-k} = 2R \parallel (R + R_{node-(k+1)})$$

If $R_{node-(k+1)} = R$ (induction hypothesis), then:

$$R_{node-k} = 2R \parallel (R + R) = 2R \parallel 2R = R$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

The equivalent resistance looking into any node toward the LSB end is **R**, which means the total resistance from the node to ground (via the shunt 2R) is $2R \parallel 2R = R$, but the input impedance (the R in series with the rest) is:

$$Z_{in} = R + R_{eq-right} = R + R = 2R$$

Hence, the **input impedance of the entire ladder is always 2R** — a constant that does not depend on the number of bits.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current Division at a Single Node

**Objective:** Show that the current entering any ladder node is split equally (1:1 ratio) between the right-going branch and the shunt branch.

At any internal node, the current $I_{in}$ arriving from the left encounters:
- A **right-going path** of impedance $R + R = 2R$ (series R plus the equivalent resistance of the remaining ladder, which we just proved is R).
- A **shunt path** of impedance $2R$ (the shunt resistor to ground).

Since both paths have equal impedance $2R$:

$$I_{right} = I_{in} \cdot \frac{2R}{2R + 2R} = \frac{I_{in}}{2}$$

$$I_{shunt} = I_{in} \cdot \frac{2R}{2R + 2R} = \frac{I_{in}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 1:1 current splitting is the heart of the R-2R principle.** It guarantees perfect binary weighting without requiring any precision resistor values beyond a 2:1 ratio.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Voltage of an N-bit R-2R Ladder DAC

**Objective:** Derive the closed-form expression for $V_{out}$ in terms of the digital input word.

**Step 1 — Define the Digital Input Word:**

Let the n-bit digital input be $b_1 b_2 b_3 \ldots b_n$, where $b_1$ is the MSB and $b_n$ is the LSB. The decimal equivalent is:

$$D = b_1 \cdot 2^{n-1} + b_2 \cdot 2^{n-2} + \ldots + b_n \cdot 2^{0} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{n-i}$$

**Step 2 — Current Generated by Each Bit:**

When bit $b_i = 1$, the corresponding 2R resistor is switched to $V_{ref}$, injecting a current of $V_{ref} / (2R)$ into the ladder. When $b_i = 0$, the switch connects to ground, injecting zero current.

**Step 3 — Propagation Through the Ladder:**

The current from bit $b_i$ traverses $(i-1)$ ladder nodes before reaching the output. At each node, it is halved. Therefore, the current reaching the output node from bit $b_i$ is:

$$I_i^{out} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot b_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{i-1} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot b_i}{2R \cdot 2^{i-1}} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot b_i}{R \cdot 2^{i}}$$

**Step 4 — Total Current at the Output Node:**

$$I_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i^{out} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{V_{ref} \cdot b_i}{R \cdot 2^{i}}$$

$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2^{i}} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R \cdot 2^{n}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{n-i}$$

$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R \cdot 2^{n}} \cdot D$$

**Step 5 — Conversion to Voltage via Transimpedance Amplifier:**

The op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f$ in an inverting configuration produces:

$$V_{out} = -I_{total} \cdot R_f = -\frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{R \cdot 2^{n}} \cdot D$$

If we choose $R_f = R$ (a common design choice to simplify):

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^{n}}}$$

**Step 6 — Verification with Special Cases:**

- **Case 1 — All bits = 0 (D = 0):** $V_{out} = 0$ ✓ (zero input gives zero output)
- **Case 2 — All bits = 1 (D = $2^n - 1$):** $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot (1 - 1/2^n) \approx -V_{ref}$ ✓ (full-scale)
- **Case 3 — Only MSB = 1 (D = $2^{n-1}$):** $V_{out} = -V_{ref}/2$ ✓ (half-scale for single MSB)
- **Case 4 — Only LSB = 1 (D = 1):** $V_{out} = -V_{ref}/2^n$ ✓ (smallest step)

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — R-2R DAC vs. Weighted Resistor DAC

| Parameter | R-2R Ladder DAC | Weighted Resistor DAC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of distinct resistor values** | **2 (R and 2R)** | **n (R, 2R, 4R, ..., 2^(n-1)R)** |
| **Resistor ratio requirement** | **2 : 1 (easy)** | **2^(n-1) : 1 (extremely hard)** |
| **IC fabrication suitability** | **Excellent (monolithic)** | **Poor (requires trimming)** |
| **Cost** | **Low** | **High** |
| **Speed** | **Fast (parasitic capacitance uniform)** | **Slower (MSB has large parasitic cap)** |
| **Accuracy** | **High (depends on matching)** | **Limited by large-value resistor accuracy** |
| **Power dissipation** | **Constant (advantage)** | **Varies with input code** |
| **Scalability to high bits** | **Easy (just add more R-2R sections)** | **Impractical beyond 8 bits** |
| **Typical resolution** | **8 to 16 bits** | **Limited to 4-6 bits practically** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — N-bit R-2R Ladder DAC Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    DIG["Digital Input Word b1 b2 b3 ... bn"] --> SWBANK["CMOS Switch Bank"]
    VREF["Vref Reference Source"] --> SWBANK
    GND["Ground Rail"] --> SWBANK
    SWBANK --> LADDER["R-2R Ladder Network"]
    LADDER --> OPAMP["Summing Op-Amp I-to-V Converter"]
    OPAMP --> VOUT["Analog Output Vout"]
    LADDER -.->|R 2R nodes| N1["Node 1 MSB"]
    LADDER -.->|R 2R nodes| N2["Node 2"]
    LADDER -.->|R 2R nodes| N3["Node 3"]
    LADDER -.->|R 2R nodes| Nn["Node n LSB"]
```


### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Current Flow Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Bit_Control_Logic["Bit Control and Switching"]
        B1["Bit 1 MSB"] --> S1["Analog Switch S1"]
        B2["Bit 2"] --> S2["Analog Switch S2"]

        Bn["Bit n LSB"] --> Sn["Analog Switch Sn"]
    end

```

### 4.4 Current Path Diagram — MSB vs LSB Contribution

```mermaid
graph LR
    MSB["MSB Current IMSB equals Vref over 2R"] --> ATTEN1["Attenuation factor 1 over 2 at Node 1"]
    ATTEN1 --> ATTEN2["Attenuation factor 1 over 2 at Node 2"]
    ATTEN2 --> ATTEN3["Attenuation factor 1 over 2 at Node 3"]
    ATTEN3 --> OUTNODE["Reaches Output Node as Vref over R times 2 to the n"]
    LSB["LSB Current ILSB equals Vref over 2R"] --> OUTNODED["Reaches Output Node directly as Vref over R times 2 to the n"]
    OUTNODE --> RATIO["Ratio IMSB to ILSB equals 2 to the n minus 1 perfect binary weighting"]
    OUTNODED --> RATIO
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

#### Question 1: Define an R-2R ladder network and list its two primary advantages over a weighted-resistor DAC.

**Model Answer:**

An R-2R ladder network is a passive resistor network constructed using only two standardized resistance values, $R$ and $2R$, arranged in a cascaded ladder configuration. It is used in digital-to-analog converters to perform binary-weighted current division.

**Two primary advantages:**

1. **Ease of Fabrication:** Only a 2:1 resistor ratio is required (between R and 2R), as opposed to a $2^{n-1}$:1 ratio in a weighted-resistor DAC. This makes it ideal for monolithic IC integration.

2. **Constant Input Impedance:** The equivalent resistance looking into any node is always $2R$, making the circuit behavior predictable and simplifying switch design.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for two advantages)*

---

#### Question 2: What is the role of the terminating 2R resistor in an R-2R ladder?

**Model Answer:**

The terminating 2R resistor, placed at the LSB end of the ladder, ensures that the equivalent resistance looking into the last node is exactly $2R$. This maintains the **binary halving property** at every internal node, including the last one, thereby preserving correct binary weighting across all bits.

*(3 marks — 1 for stating the role, 1 for the equivalence, 1 for binary weighting impact)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023 Style] — Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### Question A (14 Marks):

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 10-bit R-2R ladder DAC with $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ and $R = R_f = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, calculate the output voltage for the input codes (i) 0000000000, (ii) 1000000000, (iii) 1111111111. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Working Principle:**

In a 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC, four CMOS switches $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ corresponding to bits $b_1$ (MSB) through $b_4$ (LSB) connect each 2R shunt resistor either to $V_{ref}$ (logic 1) or to ground (logic 0).

**Node-by-Node Current Flow:**

- The MSB switch, when ON, injects a current of $I_1 = V_{ref} / (2R)$ into the first node.
- At each subsequent node, the current is halved: $I_2 = I_1/2 = V_{ref}/(4R)$, $I_3 = V_{ref}/(8R)$, $I_4 = V_{ref}/(16R)$.

**Summation:**

All currents reach the virtual ground of the inverting op-amp. The total output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^4} \left( b_1 \cdot 2^3 + b_2 \cdot 2^2 + b_3 \cdot 2^1 + b_4 \cdot 2^0 \right) \cdot \frac{R_f}{R}$$

With $R_f = R$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ref}}{16} \left( 8b_1 + 4b_2 + 2b_3 + b_4 \right)$$

##### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Given:** $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$, $R = R_f = 5\text{ k}\Omega$.

The general output equation is:

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^{n}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R}$$

Since $R_f = R$:

$$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^{n}} = -5 \cdot \frac{D}{1024}$$

**(i) Input code 0000000000:** $D = 0$

$$V_{out} = -5 \cdot \frac{0}{1024} = 0\text{ V}$$

**(ii) Input code 1000000000:** $D = 2^9 = 512$

$$V_{out} = -5 \cdot \frac{512}{1024} = -2.5\text{ V}$$

**(iii) Input code 1111111111:** $D = 2^{10} - 1 = 1023$

$$V_{out} = -5 \cdot \frac{1023}{1024} = -4.9951\text{ V} \approx -5\text{ V}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:

**(a)** Compare the R-2R ladder DAC with the weighted-resistor DAC in terms of (i) number of resistor values, (ii) matching requirements, (iii) speed, and (iv) suitability for IC fabrication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the input impedance of an R-2R ladder is always $2R$, irrespective of the number of bits. Derive the equivalent resistance at the second node from the LSB end. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Question B(a):

| Criterion | R-2R Ladder DAC | Weighted Resistor DAC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Number of resistor values | Only **2** (R and 2R) | **n** distinct values (R, 2R, ..., 2^(n-1)R) |
| (ii) Matching requirements | **2:1 ratio** (easy to achieve) | **$2^{n-1}$:1 ratio** (very difficult for n > 8) |
| (iii) Speed | **Fast** (uniform parasitic capacitance) | **Slower** (MSB has large parasitic capacitance) |
| (iv) IC fabrication suitability | **Excellent** (monolithic CMOS compatible) | **Poor** (requires laser trimming for large resistors) |

##### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Proof that input impedance is always 2R:**

Starting from the LSB end, the terminating 2R resistor makes the last node's right-looking impedance equal to $2R \parallel 2R = R$. Adding the series R gives $R + R = 2R$ at the second-to-last node, which in parallel with its own 2R shunt gives $2R \parallel 2R = R$, and so on recursively. This recursion yields an input impedance of **2R at every node** including the leftmost input terminal.

**Derivation at second node from LSB:**

At the LSB end: $R_{LSB} = 2R \parallel 2R = R$

At the second node from LSB: Looking right, we see $R$ (from LSB computation) in series with $R$ (the series resistor), giving $R + R = 2R$. In parallel with the local $2R$ shunt:

$$R_{node-2} = 2R \parallel 2R = \frac{2R \cdot 2R}{2R + 2R} = R$$

Adding the series R to this gives the **input impedance at that node** as $R + R = 2R$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the output voltage when using an inverting op-amp configuration. The KTU answer key is strict on sign conventions — always state "assuming an inverting topology" or "assuming a non-inverting topology" explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula** $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot (D / 2^n)$ with the full-scale formula $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot (1 - 1/2^n)$ — these are DIFFERENT (the former is for an arbitrary code, the latter only for all-1s input).
> 3. **Not mentioning the terminating 2R resistor** in the circuit diagram — this is a mandatory 1-mark item in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Failing to justify the 2R input impedance** in descriptive answers — merely stating the result without the recursive proof loses 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Confusing the R-2R ladder DAC** with a flash ADC or a sigma-delta converter — these are architecturally unrelated and examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — R-2R Ladder Networks**

- [ ] **R-2R ladder = only two resistor values** (R and 2R), arranged in a cascaded current-divider topology.
- [ ] **Input impedance is ALWAYS 2R** at every node — this is the key invariant property.
- [ ] **Binary halving at every node** — current splits 1:1 between right-going path and shunt path.
- [ ] **Advantage over weighted-resistor DAC:** only a 2:1 resistor ratio is needed (vs. $2^{n-1}$:1).
- [ ] **Output voltage formula (inverting, with $R_f = R$):** $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$.
- [ ] **Output voltage formula (general):** $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot (R_f / R) \cdot (D / 2^n)$.
- [ ] **LSB step size:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$.
- [ ] **Full-scale output (all 1s):** $V_{FS} = -V_{ref} \cdot (1 - 1/2^n)$.
- [ ] **Terminating 2R resistor** at the LSB end is **mandatory** — it preserves the 2R input impedance property.
- [ ] **Digital switches** (typically CMOS transmission gates) connect each 2R to $V_{ref}$ or GND based on bit value.
- [ ] **Summing element** is an inverting op-amp acting as a transimpedance (I-to-V) converter.
- [ ] **IC fabrication friendly** — only resistor matching (not absolute accuracy) determines linearity.
- [ ] **Common bit resolutions:** 8, 10, 12, 14, 16 bits in commercial DACs.
- [ ] **Power dissipation is constant** — independent of input code (unlike weighted-resistor DAC).
- [ ] **Applications:** Audio DACs, video DACs, digital potentiometers, PGAs, function generators, process control.
- [ ] **KTU-favorite derivations:** (1) Input impedance = 2R proof, (2) Current division at a node, (3) $V_{out}$ closed form.
- [ ] **Numerical problem pattern:** Given $n$, $V_{ref}$, $R$, $R_f$, find $V_{out}$ for a specific digital code.
- [ ] **Comparison question pattern:** R-2R vs. weighted-resistor DAC (4-6 parameters, tabulated answer expected).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Charge Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Charge Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charge Scaling DAC (CSDAC)** is a Digital-to-Analog Converter architecture that exploits the **principle of charge redistribution** across a **binary-weighted capacitor array** (values $C,\ 2C,\ 4C,\ \ldots,\ 2^{N-1}C$) switched by CMOS transmission gates. The digital input word controls the position of each capacitor's bottom plate between the reference voltage $V_{REF}$ and analog ground, thereby producing a proportional analog output voltage at the output of a switched-capacitor (SC) operational amplifier.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, charge scaling is treated as the **capacitor-based analog counterpart of the R-2R ladder DAC**, offering superior **CMOS compatibility**, **zero static power dissipation**, and excellent **monotonicity** when implemented in modern sub-micron processes.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water-Bucket" Analogy** 🪣
> Imagine a row of buckets placed on a perfectly balanced seesaw. The buckets have sizes in a binary ratio: **1 L, 2 L, 4 L, 8 L**, … Each bucket is connected to a switch that either fills it from a **water tap (Vref)** or leaves it **empty (GND)**. When you fill the buckets according to a binary code (e.g., 1 0 1 1), you only fill buckets at positions where the digital bit is '1'. The **total weight of water** on the seesaw corresponds to the **total stored charge**, and the **tilt of the seesaw** corresponds to the **analog output voltage**. Larger buckets contribute more, exactly mirroring how larger capacitors ($2^{N-i}C$) dominate the charge redistribution.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Standard |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|
| Reference Voltage | $V_{REF}$ | **2.5 V to 5.0 V** (single supply) |
| Unit Capacitance | $C$ | **10 fF to 1 pF** (CMOS MIM/MOS cap) |
| Switch ON-Resistance | $R_{ON}$ | **< 1 kΩ** (CMOS transmission gate) |
| Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **1 MHz to 100 MHz** |
| Resolution | $N$ | **8 to 16 bits** (typical) |
| Settling Accuracy | $\varepsilon$ | **0.5 LSB** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 explicitly links charge scaling to **Switched-Capacitor (SC) circuits**, where the equivalent resistance $R_{eq} = 1/(f_{clk} C)$ replaces physical resistors — making charge-scaling DACs **fully integrable in pure CMOS technology**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Two-Phase Operation

Every charge-scaling DAC operates in **two non-overlapping clock phases** to avoid signal-dependent charge injection errors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Φ₁ (Reset / Sampling / Zeroing Phase)**
> - All bottom plates of the capacitor array are tied to **analog ground**.
> - The op-amp is configured in **unity-gain feedback** (switch S_FB closed).
> - All capacitors are **completely discharged** → voltage across each capacitor = 0.
> - Op-amp output is **forced to 0 V** (virtual short).
>
> **Phase Φ₂ (Conversion / Redistribution / Hold Phase)**
> - Unity-feedback switch is opened; feedback capacitor $C_F$ engages.
> - Bottom plate of capacitor $C_i$ is switched to **$V_{REF}$ if bit $b_i = 1$**, else to **GND**.
> - Charge redistribution occurs; the op-amp generates a stable analog voltage.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why binary weighting?** Capacitance ratios in CMOS are defined by **photolithographic geometry** — making $2:1$ ratios highly accurate ($\pm 0.1\%$), unlike absolute capacitance values which can vary by $\pm 20\%$.
- **Why two phases?** Charge redistribution requires the system to start from a **known reference state** (Φ₁) to achieve **deterministic, repeatable conversion** (Φ₂).
- **Why an op-amp?** It isolates the capacitor array from the **load**, buffers the high-impedance SC node, and enforces **virtual ground** at the summing junction — eliminating charge redistribution errors due to parasitic loading.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following markdown table contains **all the high-yield formulas** required for KTU 2024 ESE/IA evaluations. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `\|` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Remarks |
|---|--------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Output Voltage (Ideal CSDAC) | $V_{OUT} = -V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i \, 2^{N-i}}{2^{N}-1}$ | Volts; $b_i \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 2 | Output Voltage (Simplified) | $V_{OUT} = -\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{N}-1} \cdot D$ | Volts; $D$ = decimal equivalent of digital word |
| 3 | LSB Step Size | $\Delta V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{N}-1}$ | Volts |
| 4 | Full-Scale Output | $V_{FS} = -V_{REF}$ | Volts (when all bits = 1) |
| 5 | MSB Capacitor Value | $C_{MSB} = 2^{N-1} \cdot C$ | Farads |
| 6 | Feedback Capacitor | $C_F = (2^{N}-1) \cdot C$ | Farads |
| 7 | Total Array Capacitance | $C_{TOT} = (2^{N}-1) \cdot C$ | Farads |
| 8 | Equivalent Resistance | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk} \cdot C}$ | Ohms; SC principle |
| 9 | Capacitor Area (Poly-Poly) | $A_i = \dfrac{C_i}{C_{ox}^{''}}$ | $\mu m^2$; $C_{ox}^{''} \approx 1\text{ fF}/\mu m^2$ |
| 10 | Settling Time Requirement | $t_s \geq \tau \cdot \ln(2^{N})$ | seconds; $\tau = R_{ON} \cdot C_{eq}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where are Charge-Scaling DACs used in industry?**
> - **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADCs** — The DAC inside a SAR ADC is **almost always** a charge-scaling (charge-redistribution) DAC, used in millions of microcontrollers, ADCs (e.g., AD7980, ADS8860).
> - **CMOS Image Sensors** — Column-level charge-redistribution ADCs use this technique.
> - **Audio DACs** — High-precision audio converters leverage the inherent monotonicity.
> - **Biomedical Implants** — Ultra-low-power pacemakers and neural interfaces exploit the **zero static current** advantage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Output Voltage

> **Objective:** Derive the closed-form expression for $V_{OUT}$ of an **N-bit charge-scaling DAC** with binary-weighted capacitors $C_i = 2^{N-i} C$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ and feedback capacitor $C_F = (2^N - 1) C$.

**Step 1 — Circuit Setup and Initial Conditions (End of Φ₁):**

At the end of the **reset phase Φ₁**, all capacitors have their top plates at the virtual ground (0 V) and bottom plates at analog ground (0 V). Hence, the charge stored on every capacitor is **zero**.

$$
Q_i(\Phi_1) = 0 \quad \forall \, i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}
$$

$$
Q_F(\Phi_1) = 0
$$

The op-amp output settles to $V_{OUT}(\Phi_1) = 0$ V due to the unity-gain feedback.

---

**Step 2 — Switching Action (Beginning of Φ₂):**

During **conversion phase Φ₂**, the bottom plate of capacitor $C_i$ is driven to:

$$
V_{b,i} = 
\begin{cases}
V_{REF} & \text{if } b_i = 1 \\
0 & \text{if } b_i = 0
\end{cases}
$$

Equivalently: $V_{b,i} = b_i \cdot V_{REF}$.

The unity-gain switch is opened, and $C_F$ is now in the feedback path. The op-amp continues to enforce **virtual ground (0 V)** at the inverting input.

---

**Step 3 — Express Final Charges on Each Capacitor:**

Since the top plate of every capacitor remains at virtual ground (0 V), and the bottom plate is at $V_{b,i}$, the voltage across capacitor $C_i$ (defined as $V_{top} - V_{bottom}$) is:

$$
V_{C_i} = 0 - V_{b,i} = -b_i \cdot V_{REF}
$$

The charge on the **top plate** of $C_i$ at the end of Φ₂ is:

$$
Q_i(\Phi_2) = C_i \cdot V_{C_i} = -C_i \cdot b_i \cdot V_{REF}
$$

Similarly, the feedback capacitor sees voltage $V_{OUT} - 0 = V_{OUT}$:

$$
Q_F(\Phi_2) = C_F \cdot V_{OUT}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Apply Charge Conservation at the Inverting Input Node:**

The inverting input is a **high-impedance node** (no DC current flows into the ideal op-amp). Therefore, the **net charge flowing into the node must be zero**. Equivalently, the **total charge change** at this node sums to zero:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[Q_i(\Phi_2) - Q_i(\Phi_1)\right] + \left[Q_F(\Phi_2) - Q_F(\Phi_1)\right] = 0
$$

Substituting $Q_i(\Phi_1) = Q_F(\Phi_1) = 0$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} Q_i(\Phi_2) + Q_F(\Phi_2) = 0
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(-C_i \, b_i \, V_{REF}\right) + C_F \cdot V_{OUT} = 0
$$

---

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{OUT}$:**

$$
C_F \cdot V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \, b_i
$$

$$
V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \, b_i}{C_F}
$$

Substitute $C_i = 2^{N-i} C$ and $C_F = (2^N - 1) C$:

$$
V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} 2^{N-i} C \, b_i}{(2^N - 1) C}
$$

The $C$ cancels:

$$
V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i \, 2^{N-i}}{2^N - 1}
$$

Define the **decimal equivalent** $D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i \, 2^{N-i}$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{OUT} = -\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1} \cdot D\,}
$$

The negative sign appears because of the **inverting op-amp configuration**; if the array is connected to the **non-inverting input**, the polarity flips.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 3-Bit Charge-Scaling DAC

**Given:**
- $N = 3$, $V_{REF} = 5$ V, $C = 1$ pF (unit capacitor).
- Digital input $b_1 b_2 b_3 = 1\,0\,1$ (binary 5).

**Step A — Identify Capacitor Values:**

$$
C_1 = 2^{3-1} C = 4C = 4\ \text{pF} \quad (\text{MSB})
$$

$$
C_2 = 2^{3-2} C = 2C = 2\ \text{pF}
$$

$$
C_3 = 2^{3-3} C = C = 1\ \text{pF} \quad (\text{LSB})
$$

$$
C_F = (2^3 - 1) C = 7C = 7\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step B — Apply Formula:**

$$
D = b_1 \cdot 2^2 + b_2 \cdot 2^1 + b_3 \cdot 2^0 = (1)(4) + (0)(2) + (1)(1) = 5
$$

$$
V_{OUT} = -\frac{5}{7} \cdot 5\ \text{V} = -\frac{25}{7}\ \text{V} \approx -3.571\ \text{V}
$$

**Step C — LSB Verification:**

$$
\Delta V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714\ \text{V}
$$

Full transfer characteristics for all 8 codes:

| Digital Input $b_1 b_2 b_3$ | Decimal $D$ | $V_{OUT}$ |
|:---------------------------:|:-----------:|:---------:|
| 0 0 0 | 0 | 0 V |
| 0 0 1 | 1 | $-0.714$ V |
| 0 1 0 | 2 | $-1.429$ V |
| 0 1 1 | 3 | $-2.143$ V |
| 1 0 0 | 4 | $-2.857$ V |
| 1 0 1 | 5 | $-3.571$ V |
| 1 1 0 | 6 | $-4.286$ V |
| 1 1 1 | 7 | $-5.000$ V |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Edge Cases & Limiting Behavior

| Condition | Effect on $V_{OUT}$ | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| All bits = 0 | $V_{OUT} = 0$ | **Zero-scale error** — defines offset |
| All bits = 1 | $V_{OUT} = -V_{REF}$ | **Full-scale error** — defines gain |
| Single MSB | $V_{OUT} = -V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{2^{N-1}}{2^{N}-1}$ | Approaches $-V_{REF}/2$ for large $N$ |
| Single LSB | $V_{OUT} = -\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{N}-1}$ | Defines **DNL/INL measurement point** |
| Capacitor mismatch $\delta C$ | $V_{OUT} \to V_{OUT} \cdot (1 + \delta)$ | Dominant source of **INL** |
| Charge injection $Q_{inj}$ | $V_{OUT} \to V_{OUT} + \dfrac{Q_{inj}}{C_F}$ | Mitigated by **dummy switches** |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture (3-Bit Charge-Scaling DAC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CLK["Clock Generator\nNon-Overlapping Phi1, Phi2"]
    DREG["3-Bit Digital Input Register\nb1 b2 b3"]
    SARR["Binary-Weighted Capacitor Array\n4C  2C  1C"]
    TERM["Terminator / Dummy Cap C"]
    FBCAP["Feedback Capacitor CF = 7C"]
    OPA["CMOS Operational Amplifier\nInverting Configuration"]
    SW1["Switch S1 - Reset Phase Phi1"]
    SW2["Switch S2 - Conversion Phase Phi2"]
    OUT["Analog Output VOUT"]
    VREF["Reference Voltage VREF"]
    GND["Analog Ground GND"]

    CLK --> SW1
    CLK --> SW2
    DREG --> SARR
    VREF --- SARR
    GND --- SARR
    SARR --> OPA
    SW1 --- OPA
    SW2 --- OPA
    TERM --- SARR
    FBCAP --- OPA
    OPA --> OUT

    classDef analog fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef digital fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class OPA,FBCAP,SARR,TERM,OUT analog
    class CLK,DREG,VREF,GND digital
    class SW1,SW2 switch
```

### 4.2 Two-Phase Switch Control Logic Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ResetPhase
    ResetPhase: Phi1 - Reset Phase\nAll bottom plates to GND\nOp-Amp in unity feedback\nVOUT forced to 0
    ResetPhase --> ConversionPhase
    ConversionPhase: Phi2 - Conversion Phase\nBottom plates to VREF or GND\nbased on bi\nFeedback engaged\nCharge redistribution occurs
    ConversionPhase --> HoldOutput
    HoldOutput: Hold and Settle\nVOUT stable\nReady for next cycle
    HoldOutput --> ResetPhase
    ResetPhase --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Block Diagram — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DIN["Digital Input Word D\n3-bit to 16-bit"]
    DEC["Binary Decoder\nBit-Slice Activation"]
    SWARR["CMOS Switch Array\nTransmission Gates"]
    CAPARR["Weighted Capacitor Bank\nBinary Ratio Geometry"]
    CHARGE["Charge Redistribution Engine\nVirtual Ground Node"]
    OPAMP["High-Gain Op-Amp\nSC Summing Junction"]
    ANALOG["Analog Output VOUT\nBipolar or Unipolar"]

    DIN --> DEC
    DEC --> SWARR
    SWARR --> CAPARR
    CAPARR --> CHARGE
    CHARGE --> OPAMP
    OPAMP --> ANALOG

    REF["VREF Source\nBandgap Derived"] -.-> SWARR
    GND2["Analog GND\nClean Substrate"] -.-> SWARR
    CLOCK["Two-Phase Clock\nPhi1 Phi2 Non-Overlap"] -.-> SWARR

    classDef block fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sup fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    class DIN,DEC,SWARR,CAPARR,CHARGE,OPAMP,ANALOG block
    class REF,GND2,CLOCK sup
```

### 4.4 Pin-Level Functional Description (Verbal Schematic)

> **Top-Level Pin Architecture for N-bit Charge-Scaling DAC:**
>
> | Pin | Symbol | Direction | Description |
> |-----|--------|-----------|-------------|
> | 1 | $V_{DD}$ | Power | Positive supply (e.g., 3.3 V) |
> | 2 | $V_{SS}$ | Power | Negative supply / GND |
> | 3 | $V_{REF}$ | Input | Precision reference voltage |
> | 4 | $D[0:N-1]$ | Input | N-bit digital word |
> | 5 | $\Phi_1$ | Input | Reset phase clock |
> | 6 | $\Phi_2$ | Input | Conversion phase clock |
> | 7 | $V_{OUT}$ | Output | Analog voltage output |
> | 8 | $V_{CM}$ | Input | Common-mode voltage (optional) |
