## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a charge-scaling DAC. List two advantages it has over an R-2R ladder DAC.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Charge-Scaling DAC (CSDAC)** is a digital-to-analog converter that uses a **binary-weighted capacitor array** ($C, 2C, 4C, \ldots, 2^{N-1}C$) switched between a reference voltage $V_{REF}$ and ground, with charge redistribution through an op-amp producing an analog output proportional to the digital input.

**Two advantages over R-2R ladder DAC:**

1. **Full CMOS Compatibility:** CSDAC uses only capacitors, MOSFET switches, and an op-amp — **no precision resistors** required. This makes it directly fabricable in standard digital CMOS processes, whereas R-2R requires laser-trimmed or thin-film resistors.

2. **Zero Static Power Dissipation:** Since capacitors do not draw DC current, the CSDAC consumes **only dynamic switching power**, making it ideal for **battery-operated and low-power applications**.

*(Acceptable: inherent monotonicity, excellent matching via lithography, suitability for SAR ADCs)* — **\[3 Marks\]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*In a 3-bit charge-scaling DAC with $V_{REF} = 7$ V, find the analog output for digital input '101'.*

**Model Answer:**

Given: $N = 3$, $V_{REF} = 7$ V, $D = 101_2 = 5_{10}$.

Using the formula:

$$
V_{OUT} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1} \cdot D = -\frac{7}{2^3 - 1} \cdot 5 = -\frac{7 \cdot 5}{7} = -5\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{OUT} = -5\ \text{V}}
$$

**\[3 Marks\]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks]** *Explain the working of an N-bit charge-scaling DAC with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the two clock phases.*

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Section 2.1 and Section 4.1 of these notes for full circuit description.)*

**Working Principle:**

The N-bit charge-scaling DAC consists of an array of N binary-weighted capacitors ($C_1 = 2^{N-1}C$, $C_2 = 2^{N-2}C$, …, $C_N = C$), a feedback capacitor $C_F = (2^N - 1)C$, an op-amp, and CMOS switches.

**Two Clock Phases:**

**Phase Φ₁ (Reset/Sampling Phase):**
- All bottom plates of the capacitor array are connected to **analog ground (GND)** via reset switches.
- The op-amp is configured in **unity-gain feedback** (switch S_FB closed across the op-amp).
- Result: All capacitors are **fully discharged**, and $V_{OUT}$ settles to **0 V**.
- Purpose: Establishes a **known, repeatable reference state** for deterministic conversion.

**Phase Φ₂ (Conversion/Redistribution Phase):**
- The unity-gain switch is opened, and feedback capacitor $C_F$ is engaged.
- Each bit $b_i$ controls a switch that routes the bottom plate of $C_i$ to either $V_{REF}$ (if $b_i = 1$) or GND (if $b_i = 0$).
- The op-amp enforces **virtual ground** at its inverting input.
- **Charge conservation** at the virtual-ground node causes charge to redistribute, generating:

$$
V_{OUT} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i \cdot 2^{N-i}
$$

**Role of Two Phases:** They ensure **deterministic conversion**, **eliminate memory of prior samples**, and **prevent signal-dependent errors** due to charge injection and switch feedthrough.

**\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]** *For a 4-bit charge-scaling DAC with $V_{REF} = 10$ V and unit capacitor $C = 5$ pF, calculate:*
*(i) the values of all capacitors in the array,*
*(ii) the feedback capacitor value,*
*(iii) the analog output for digital input '1010',*
*(iv) the LSB step size.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 4$, $V_{REF} = 10$ V, $C = 5$ pF.

**(i) Capacitor Array Values:**

$$
C_1 = 2^{4-1} \cdot C = 8C = 40\ \text{pF} \quad (\text{MSB})
$$

$$
C_2 = 2^{4-2} \cdot C = 4C = 20\ \text{pF}
$$

$$
C_3 = 2^{4-3} \cdot C = 2C = 10\ \text{pF}
$$

$$
C_4 = 2^{4-4} \cdot C = 1C = 5\ \text{pF} \quad (\text{LSB})
$$

**(ii) Feedback Capacitor:**

$$
C_F = (2^4 - 1) \cdot C = 15C = 75\ \text{pF}
$$

**(iii) Output for '1010':**

$$
D = (1)(2^3) + (0)(2^2) + (1)(2^1) + (0)(2^0) = 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 10
$$

$$
V_{OUT} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1} \cdot D = -\frac{10}{15} \cdot 10 = -\frac{100}{15} \approx -6.667\ \text{V}
$$

**(iv) LSB Step Size:**

$$
\Delta V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{10}{15} = 0.667\ \text{V}
$$

**\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**Q4(a). [7 Marks]** *Derive the output voltage expression for an N-bit charge-scaling DAC. Clearly state the assumptions made.*

**Model Answer:**

*(Full derivation is provided in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

**Assumptions Made:**

1. **Ideal op-amp:** Infinite input impedance, infinite open-loop gain, zero offset voltage, zero input bias current.
2. **Ideal switches:** Zero ON-resistance, infinite OFF-resistance, zero charge injection.
3. **Ideal capacitors:** No parasitic capacitance, perfect matching (ratios exactly $2:1$), no leakage.
4. **Virtual ground maintained** throughout Φ₂ at the inverting input.
5. **Charge conservation** at the high-impedance summing node (no charge loss to substrate).
6. **Non-overlapping clocks** with sufficient settling time for $V_{OUT}$ to stabilize within 0.5 LSB.

**Final Derived Expression:**

$$
V_{OUT} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1} \cdot D = -V_{REF} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i \cdot 2^{N-i}}{2^N - 1}
$$

**\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]** *Compare the charge-scaling DAC with the R-2R ladder DAC and weighted-resistor DAC in terms of: (i) CMOS compatibility, (ii) monotonicity, (iii) component count for an N-bit converter, (iv) typical resolution achievable.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Weighted-Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC | Charge-Scaling DAC |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **(i) CMOS Compatibility** | Poor — requires large resistors (MΩ range) | Moderate — needs matched poly resistors | **Excellent** — only capacitors, MOSFETs, op-amp |
| **(ii) Monotonicity** | Not guaranteed (resistor mismatch) | Guaranteed (passive structure) | **Guaranteed** (binary capacitor ratios) |
| **(iii) Component Count** | $N$ precision resistors + $N$ switches | $2N$ resistors (2R + R) + $N$ switches | $N$ capacitors + $N+1$ switches + 1 op-amp |
| **(iv) Typical Resolution** | 6 to 8 bits (resistor matching limit) | 8 to 12 bits | **8 to 16 bits** |

**Key Insights:**
- The CSDAC has the **largest component count** but offers the **highest resolution** and **best CMOS compatibility**.
- For high resolution ($N > 10$), the **total capacitance ratio** $2^N : 1$ becomes impractical (e.g., 1024:1 for $N=10$), prompting the use of **split-capacitor (C-2C)** or **C-2C-4C** hybrid arrays.
- **Monotonicity** in CSDAC is guaranteed because the largest capacitor always corresponds to the MSB — any mismatch only degrades **linearity (INL/DNL)**, not monotonicity.

**\[7 Marks\]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** ⚠️
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign:** Many students omit the minus sign in $V_{OUT}$. This is a **2-mark penalty** in KTU evaluations.
> 2. **Wrong feedback capacitor:** Using $C_F = C$ instead of $C_F = (2^N - 1)C$ leads to **saturated, incorrect outputs**. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Confusing MSB/LSB indexing:** Always define $b_1$ = MSB; the largest capacitor $2^{N-1}C$ corresponds to $b_1$.
> 4. **Ignoring the terminator capacitor:** In some textbook variants, an extra grounded "dummy" capacitor of value $C$ is added. State whether your derivation includes it.
> 5. **Phase confusion:** Writing that charge redistribution occurs in Φ₁ instead of Φ₂ — instant **1-mark loss**.
> 6. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing pF and fF in numerical problems — always include units.
> 7. **Skipping "why" in derivations:** KTU 2024 scheme rewards conceptual justification, not just formula application.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Charge Scaling (PEECT754 — Module 4)**

- ⭐ **Definition:** CSDAC = Binary-weighted capacitor array + op-amp + CMOS switches; uses **charge redistribution**.
- ⭐ **Output Formula:** $V_{OUT} = -\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1} \cdot D$
- ⭐ **LSB Step:** $\Delta V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N - 1}$
- ⭐ **Full Scale:** $V_{FS} = -V_{REF}$ (when $D = 2^N - 1$)
- ⭐ **MSB Capacitor:** $C_{MSB} = 2^{N-1} \cdot C$
- ⭐ **Feedback Capacitor:** $C_F = (2^N - 1) \cdot C$ (sum of all weighted caps)
- ⭐ **Two Phases:** Φ₁ = Reset (zero charge), Φ₂ = Conversion (charge redistribution)
- ⭐ **Virtual Ground:** Op-amp inverting input held at 0 V; charge conservation applies at this node.
- ⭐ **Sign Convention:** Output is **negative** for inverting configuration; flip polarity for non-inverting.
- ⭐ **Advantages:** CMOS-compatible, zero static power, inherent monotonicity, ideal for SAR ADCs.
- ⭐ **Disadvantages:** Large capacitor spread ($2^N : 1$), slow settling, sensitive to parasitics, switch charge injection.
- ⭐ **Variants:** Split-capacitor (C-2C), C-2C-4C hybrid — used when $N > 10$ bits.
- ⭐ **KTU Key Linkage:** Charge scaling is the **DAC core** of every **SAR ADC** (ADC ↔ DAC duality).
- ⭐ **Memorize:** The denominator $(2^N - 1)$, **not** $2^N$ — this is a frequent exam trap.
- ⭐ **Industrial Relevance:** AD7980 (Analog Devices), ADS8860 (Texas Instruments) — both use charge-scaling SAR architectures.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cyclic ADC (Algorithmic / Recycling ADC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Cyclic ADC** (also called an *Algorithmic ADC* or *Recycling ADC*) is a Nyquist-rate analog-to-digital converter that resolves **one bit per clock cycle** by recursively reusing the same analog hardware (one op-amp, a few capacitors, and switches). To obtain an N-bit digital word, the input sample is processed through the identical analog stage **N times**, with the residue (the "left-over" analog signal after extracting the most significant bit) being fed back to the same input for the next iteration.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Water Pouring Game":**
> Imagine a large beaker of unknown water volume (analog input). You only have a single half-sized measuring cup (the single analog stage). 
> 1. **Cycle 1 (MSB):** You scoop up water. If the beaker is more than half full, you pour it into the "MSB = 1" bucket and keep the leftover half in the cup. If it's less than half, you mark "MSB = 0" and just keep the whole beaker's contents. 
> 2. **Cycle 2 (MSB-1):** You take the leftover water (which is always < 1 cup), and repeat the half-cup test to get the next bit. 
> 3. **Cycle N (LSB):** After N such "scoops," you have the complete binary word.
> 
> The Cyclic ADC works exactly like this: **one hardware stage, used N times, gets N bits.**

**Key Performance Metrics:**
- **Conversion time:** $T_{conv} = N \times T_{clk}$ (where **N = resolution in bits**, $T_{clk}$ = clock period)
- **Hardware complexity:** O(1) — constant with respect to N (only one op-amp, ~5 capacitors)
- **Throughput:** 1 sample per $N \times T_{clk}$
- **Typical resolution range:** 8 to 16 bits

---

## 1.2 Pipeline ADC

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Pipeline ADC** is a Nyquist-rate converter consisting of **N cascaded low-resolution stages** (typically 1 to 4 bits per stage), where each stage concurrently processes a *different* sample. Each stage contains a sub-ADC, a sub-DAC, a subtractor, and a residue amplifier (gain stage). The bits from all stages are combined via a digital error-correction (delay-alignment) network to produce the final N-bit output.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Assembly Line for Digits":**
> Think of a car factory. The car body moves down a conveyor belt, and at each station a *different* worker installs a specific part (engine, wheels, seats…). Station 1 adds the MSB, Station 2 the next bit, and so on. While Station 1 is working on **Car #101**, Station 2 is finishing **Car #100**, and Station 3 is closing **Car #99**. 
> 
> The Pipeline ADC does the same: each "station" (stage) adds a few bits, and **all stations work simultaneously on different samples** — that's why it's so fast after warm-up.

**Key Performance Metrics:**
- **Latency:** $N \times T_{clk}$ (time for a single sample to traverse all stages)
- **Throughput:** **1 sample per clock cycle** (after the pipeline is filled)
- **Hardware complexity:** O(N) — proportional to number of stages
- **Typical resolution range:** 8 to 18+ bits at sampling rates up to hundreds of MS/s

---

## 1.3 Flash ADC (Parallel / Direct ADC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Flash ADC** (also known as a *Parallel ADC* or *Direct-conversion ADC*) is the fastest Nyquist-rate ADC architecture. It simultaneously compares the analog input against $2^N - 1$ threshold voltages generated by a precision resistor ladder, using an array of identical high-speed comparators. The resulting **thermometer code** is then converted to a standard binary output by a priority encoder, completing the entire conversion in essentially **one clock cycle**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Height-Sort Race":**
> Suppose 100 students line up against a wall with 99 pre-painted height marks (1.50 m, 1.51 m, … 2.48 m). In **one instant**, every student simultaneously looks at which mark is closest below their head. Student #1 (shortest) might match mark 1.50 m, while Student #100 (tallest) matches all 99 marks. The result is a "thermometer" of lit marks, which an "encoder person" instantly translates into a rank (binary). 
> 
> The Flash ADC does this in *one step* using comparators instead of students — this is why it is the **fastest ADC ever built**.

**Key Performance Metrics:**
- **Conversion time:** ~1 clock cycle (one comparator decision + encoder delay)
- **Hardware complexity:** **O($2^N$)** — doubles with every additional bit
- **Number of comparators:** $2^N - 1$
- **Number of ladder resistors:** $2^N$
- **Typical resolution range:** 4 to 8 bits (8-bit = 255 comparators; practical limit)

> [!WARNING]
> **Why is 8 bits the practical limit?** Each additional bit **doubles** the chip area, power, and input capacitance. A 10-bit flash would need 1023 comparators, consuming watts of power and creating massive loading at the input — making it impractical for most integrated designs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cyclic ADC — Operational Block-by-Block Analysis

A typical 1-bit-per-cycle Cyclic ADC block diagram is shown in **Section 4**. The recurrence relation is the heart of the converter.

### Why the Residue Must Be Multiplied by 2

After the comparator decides the MSB, the residue (the analog "leftover") lies in a smaller voltage range. To maximize the dynamic range of the next comparison, this residue is **amplified by exactly 2** so the next cycle sees a full-scale-like input. This is called the **"multiply-by-two"** residue transfer.

### Generalized Residue Equation

For a 1-bit-per-stage cyclic converter with input $V_{in,k}$ at the $k^{th}$ cycle:

$$
V_{in,k+1} = 2 \cdot V_{in,k} - b_k \cdot V_{ref}
$$

where:
- $V_{in,k}$ = residue input to the $k^{th}$ cycle
- $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ = digital bit decided at cycle $k$
- $V_{ref}$ = full-scale reference voltage

### How the Architecture Reuses Hardware

The Multiply-by-Two (M×2) block, comparator, and 1-bit DAC are shared; switches (MOS transmission gates) reroute the residue capacitor back to the input at the end of each cycle. This **time-multiplexing** of hardware is why cyclic ADCs are **silicon-area-efficient**.

---

## 2.2 Pipeline ADC — Operational Block-by-Block Analysis

### Stage-Level Operation

Each 1-bit pipeline stage consists of four sub-blocks (analyzed in order):

1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Holds the previous stage's residue at a stable value so the sub-ADC has a clean input to compare.
2. **Sub-ADC (1-bit flash):** A single comparator decides the bit $b_k$ (1 if $V_{in,k} > V_{ref}/4$, 0 otherwise, for standard 1.5-bit stage).
3. **Sub-DAC (1-bit):** Converts the digital bit back to an analog level (either $V_{ref}/4$ or $-V_{ref}/4$).
4. **Subtractor + Gain (×2):** Computes the residue: $V_{res,k} = 2 \cdot V_{in,k} - b_k \cdot V_{ref}/2$
5. **Output of stage $k$** = bit $b_k$, passed with appropriate delay to the digital correction block.

### Why "1.5-bit per stage" is the Industry Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 1-bit stage cannot correct comparator offset errors. A **1.5-bit stage** (with thresholds at $-V_{ref}/4$, 0, $+V_{ref}/4$ and 3 comparators) has built-in **redundancy** that allows digital error correction of up to $\pm V_{ref}/4$ offset at each stage. This dramatically relaxes the comparator design tolerances and is the cornerstone of modern pipeline ADCs.

### Throughput vs. Latency

- **Throughput** depends only on **one stage's settling time** $T_{stage}$, not the entire chain: $f_s = 1/T_{stage}$
- **Latency** = $N \times T_{clk}$ (sum of delays through all stages)

---

## 2.3 Flash ADC — Operational Block-by-Block Analysis

### Resistor Ladder Network

A string of $2^N$ **equal-value resistors** is connected between $+V_{ref}$ and $-V_{ref}$ (or $+V_{ref}$ and GND for unipolar). Each node of the ladder provides a unique threshold voltage:

$$
V_{th,k} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{k}{2^N}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^N - 1
$$

### Comparator Array

Each of the $2^N - 1$ comparators has:
- **Non-inverting input** ← Analog input $V_{in}$
- **Inverting input** ← Ladder threshold $V_{th,k}$

**Output of comparator $k$:**
$$
C_k = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } V_{in} > V_{th,k} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This generates a **thermometer code** — a string of 1s at the bottom and 0s at the top, with the transition point indicating the binary value.

### Priority Encoder

The encoder converts the $2^N - 1$ thermometer-code bits into an $N$-bit binary word. A typical implementation is a **fat-tree encoder** or a **Wallace-tree encoder**, both of which propagate in $O(\log N)$ gate delays.

### Comparator Offset & Bubbles

Real comparators have input-referred offset voltages $V_{os}$, which can cause **thermometer-code bubbles** (a 0 appearing between 1s). To prevent encoder metastability, a **bubble-correction latch** or **gray-coding** is often inserted between the comparator array and the encoder.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for KTU University Exam — must be memorized.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Flash ADC — Number of comparators | $N_{comp} = 2^N - 1$ | N = resolution in bits |
| 2 | Flash ADC — Number of ladder resistors | $N_R = 2^N$ | Series string |
| 3 | Flash ADC — Ladder threshold | $V_{th,k} = V_{ref} \cdot k / 2^N$ | k = 1 to $2^N - 1$ |
| 4 | Flash ADC — LSB (quantization step) | $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^N$ | Volts |
| 5 | Flash ADC — Quantization SNR (ideal) | $SNR_q = 6.02N + 1.76$ | dB |
| 6 | Flash ADC — Input capacitance | $C_{in} \approx 2^N \cdot C_{comp}$ | Farads |
| 7 | Flash ADC — Power consumption | $P \propto 2^N$ | Watts |
| 8 | Flash ADC — Conversion time | $T_{conv} \approx 1 \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| 9 | Cyclic ADC — Conversion time | $T_{conv} = N \cdot T_{clk}$ | N cycles for N bits |
| 10 | Cyclic ADC — Residue recurrence | $V_{in,k+1} = 2V_{in,k} - b_k V_{ref}$ | Per cycle |
| 11 | Cyclic ADC — Hardware count | 1 op-amp, ~5 caps, ~10 switches | Constant |
| 12 | Pipeline ADC — Throughput | $f_s = 1 / T_{stage}$ | After pipeline fills |
| 13 | Pipeline ADC — Latency | $T_{lat} = N \cdot T_{clk}$ | Per sample |
| 14 | Pipeline ADC — 1.5-bit stage residue | $V_{res} = 2V_{in} - d_k \cdot V_{ref}/2$ | $d_k \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ |
| 15 | Flash ADC — Encoder gate delay | $t_{enc} \propto \log_2(2^N) = N$ | Wallace tree |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application Insight:**
> - **Flash ADCs** dominate in oscilloscopes, radar, and high-speed serial links (e.g., 6-bit Flash in a 10-GS/s oscilloscope front-end).
> - **Pipeline ADCs** are the workhorse of communications base-stations, digital video (HDMI, DisplayPort), CCD imaging, and software-defined radios.
> - **Cyclic ADCs** appear in low-power instrumentation, sensor read-out ICs, and biomedical implants where silicon area is the binding constraint.

---

| Parameter | Flash ADC | Cyclic ADC | Pipeline ADC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed (samples/sec) | Highest (GS/s) | Lowest (KS/s to MS/s) | High (MS/s) |
| Conversion cycles/sample | 1 | N | 1 (after latency) |

| Input loading | Very high | Low | Medium |

| Suitability for CMOS integration | Poor (large area) | Excellent | Excellent |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Comparators in an N-bit Flash ADC

### Statement
For an N-bit flash ADC with a resistor ladder spanning the full-scale input range, derive the number of comparators and ladder resistors.

### Step 1 — Define the Resolution
The full-scale input range $V_{FS}$ must be divided into $2^N$ equally spaced quantization levels, so that the smallest distinguishable voltage step is:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}
$$

> **Logic:** With N bits, there are $2^N$ distinct binary codes (000…0 to 111…1), each representing a different voltage slot.

### Step 2 — Identify the Thresholds
The $2^N$ slots create **$2^N - 1$ internal threshold voltages** (the boundaries between adjacent slots). Each threshold must be physically realized as a node on the resistor ladder. The ladder therefore needs:

$$
N_R = 2^N \text{ resistors}
$$

to produce the $2^N + 1$ nodes (including the two end terminals at $+V_{ref}$ and $-V_{ref}$), of which the $2^N - 1$ internal nodes are useful as threshold taps.

### Step 3 — Match Thresholds to Comparators
Each threshold requires one comparator to test "Is $V_{in} > V_{th,k}$?" Since we have $2^N - 1$ thresholds, we need:

$$
\boxed{N_{comp} = 2^N - 1}
$$

### Step 4 — Example Numerical Check
For a **3-bit flash ADC**:
- Comparators = $2^3 - 1 = 7$
- Resistors = $2^3 = 8$
- Thresholds at: $V_{ref}/8,\ 2V_{ref}/8,\ 3V_{ref}/8,\ 4V_{ref}/8,\ 5V_{ref}/8,\ 6V_{ref}/8,\ 7V_{ref}/8$

For an **8-bit flash ADC**:
- Comparators = $2^8 - 1 = 255$
- Resistors = $2^8 = 256$

This **exponential growth** justifies the practical 8-bit limit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cyclic ADC Residue Transfer Function

### Statement
Show that the analog hardware of a 1-bit-per-cycle cyclic ADC implements the recurrence $V_{in,k+1} = 2V_{in,k} - b_k V_{ref}$.

### Step 1 — Comparator Decision
At cycle $k$, the input to the stage $V_{in,k}$ is compared against $V_{ref}/2$. The bit is:

$$
b_k = 
\begin{cases}
1, & V_{in,k} > V_{ref}/2 \\
0, & V_{in,k} \le V_{ref}/2
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Subtract the Reference
The DAC outputs $b_k \cdot V_{ref}/2$ (or the chosen level). The subtractor computes:

$$
V_{sub} = V_{in,k} - \frac{b_k V_{ref}}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by Two
The residue amplifier applies a gain of 2 to the subtracted value:

$$
V_{in,k+1} = 2 \cdot V_{sub} = 2V_{in,k} - b_k V_{ref}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify the Range
For the cyclic ADC to function correctly, the residue must always lie in the range $\left[ -V_{ref}, +V_{ref} \right]$ so the next comparison is meaningful. The choice of gain = 2 ensures this when $V_{in,k}$ lies in $\left[ -V_{ref}, +V_{ref} \right]$ and the comparison threshold is $V_{ref}/2$. **Proof (bounds check):**

- **Worst case positive:** $V_{in,k} = +V_{ref}$, then $b_k = 1$, so $V_{in,k+1} = 2V_{ref} - V_{ref} = +V_{ref}$ ✓
- **Worst case negative:** $V_{in,k} = -V_{ref}$, then $b_k = 0$, so $V_{in,k+1} = -2V_{ref}$ ✗ (out of range!)

This is why practical cyclic ADCs use a **folded input range** and a **redundant signed-digit** internal representation, which extends the allowable input range to $\pm V_{ref}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pipeline ADC 1.5-bit Stage Residue and Digital Error Correction

### Statement
A 1.5-bit pipeline stage uses three comparators with thresholds at $-V_{ref}/4$, $0$, $+V_{ref}/4$. Show that comparator offsets of up to $\pm V_{ref}/4$ can be corrected digitally.

### Step 1 — Sub-ADC Decision

The 1.5-bit sub-ADC produces a digital code $d_k \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$:

$$
d_k = 
\begin{cases}
+1, & V_{in} > +V_{ref}/4 \\
0, & -V_{ref}/4 < V_{in} \le +V_{ref}/4 \\
-1, & V_{in} \le -V_{ref}/4
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Residue Generation
The sub-DAC outputs $d_k \cdot V_{ref}/4$, and the residue is:

$$
V_{res,k} = 2 \left( V_{in,k} - \frac{d_k V_{ref}}{4} \right) = 2V_{in,k} - \frac{d_k V_{ref}}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Range of the Residue
For $V_{in,k}$ in the valid range $\left[ -V_{ref}/2, +V_{ref}/2 \right]$, the residue stays within $\left[ -V_{ref}, +V_{ref} \right]$ so the next stage is not over-ranged.

### Step 4 — Tolerance to Comparator Offset
If a comparator has an offset $V_{os}$ with $\vert V_{os} \vert \le V_{ref}/4$, the decision boundary shifts, but the residue is still bounded. The "wrong" decision produces a residue that, when combined with the wrong bit in the digital correction block, **cancels out** because the overlapping decision regions of adjacent stages ensure that an over-range from one stage is "wrapped around" by the next stage's code.

### Step 5 — Digital Correction
The raw bits from all stages are summed with appropriate weights (effectively $b_k$ and $d_k$ being combined) to produce a clean $N$-bit output. This **redundant signed-digit (RSD)** addition tolerates sub-ADC offsets up to $\pm V_{ref}/4$ at every stage — a major reason 1.5-bit stages are industry-standard.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are the typical numerical problems KTU sets in Part A (3-mark) questions.**

### Example 1: Flash ADC Component Count
**Q:** A 6-bit flash ADC is designed for a 2 V full-scale range. Find: (a) number of comparators, (b) number of ladder resistors, (c) LSB size, (d) the threshold voltage of the 25th comparator.

**Solution:**

**(a)** $N_{comp} = 2^6 - 1 = 63$

**(b)** $N_R = 2^6 = 64$

**(c)** $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^6 = 2 / 64 = 31.25$ mV

**(d)** $V_{th,25} = V_{ref} \cdot 25 / 2^6 = 2 \cdot 25 / 64 = 0.78125$ V

### Example 2: Cyclic ADC Conversion Time
**Q:** A 12-bit cyclic ADC operates with a 1 MHz clock. Find the total conversion time and the maximum input signal frequency that satisfies the Nyquist criterion.

**Solution:**

$T_{conv} = N \cdot T_{clk} = 12 \cdot 1\ \mu s = 12\ \mu s$

Sampling rate $f_s = 1 / T_{conv} \approx 83.33$ kS/s

Maximum input frequency $f_{in,max} = f_s / 2 \approx 41.67$ kHz

### Example 3: Pipeline ADC Latency vs. Throughput
**Q:** A 10-stage, 1-bit-per-stage pipeline ADC has a 100 MHz clock. Find: (a) throughput after pipeline fill, (b) latency of one sample, (c) throughput 1 ms after reset.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Throughput = 1 sample per clock = $100$ MS/s

**(b)** Latency = 10 × 10 ns = 100 ns

**(c)** After 1 ms = $10^6$ ns, the pipeline has been clocked $10^6 / 10\ \text{ns} = 10^5$ cycles, far more than 10 fill cycles, so **throughput = 100 MS/s** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **Mermaid block-level functional architecture flows** (per KTU-PE-ENGINE V10 directives). Physical circuit schematics (resistor ladders, switch-capacitor cells) are abstracted to functional blocks to remain Mermaid-compatible and 100% rendering-safe.

## 4.1 Flash ADC — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVin["ANALOG INPUT VIN"] --> nodeLA["RESISTOR LADDER 2N RESISTORS"]
    nodeVrefP["VREF POSITIVE"] --> nodeLA
    nodeVrefN["VREF NEGATIVE OR GND"] --> nodeLA
    nodeLA --> nodeT1["THRESHOLD T1"]
    nodeLA --> nodeT2["THRESHOLD T2"]
    nodeLA --> nodeT3["THRESHOLD T3"]
    nodeLA --> nodeTd["THRESHOLD T 2N-1"]
    nodeVin --> nodeC1["COMPARATOR 1"]
    nodeVin --> nodeC2["COMPARATOR 2"]
    nodeVin --> nodeC3["COMPARATOR 3"]
    nodeVin --> nodeCd["COMPARATOR 2N-1"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeC1
    nodeT2 --> nodeC2
    nodeT3 --> nodeC3
    nodeTd --> nodeCd
    nodeC1 --> nodeTC["THERMOMETER CODE BITS 1 TO 2N-1"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeTC
    nodeC3 --> nodeTC
    nodeCd --> nodeTC
    nodeTC --> nodeBC["BUBBLE CORRECTION LATCH OPTIONAL"]
    nodeBC --> nodePE["PRIORITY OR WALLACE TREE ENCODER"]
    nodePE --> nodeOUT["N BIT BINARY OUTPUT D0 TO DN-1"]
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
1. The analog input $V_{in}$ fans out to **all comparators** in parallel.
2. The resistor ladder generates $2^N - 1$ distinct thresholds.
3. Each comparator outputs 1 bit; together they form a **thermometer code**.
4. The encoder collapses the thermometer code into an N-bit binary word in $O(\log N)$ gate delays.

---

## 4.2 Pipeline ADC — Multi-Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIN["ANALOG INPUT VIN"] --> nodeSH0["SAMPLE AND HOLD STAGE 0"]
    nodeSH0 --> nodeS1["STAGE 1 SUB ADC 1.5 BIT"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeD1["SUB DAC 1.5 BIT"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeB1["DIGIT B1"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeSUB1["SUBTRACTOR"]
    nodeSH0 --> nodeSUB1
    nodeSUB1 --> nodeG1["GAIN STAGE X2"]
    nodeG1 --> nodeSH1["SAMPLE AND HOLD STAGE 1"]
    nodeSH1 --> nodeS2["STAGE 2 SUB ADC 1.5 BIT"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeD2["SUB DAC 1.5 BIT"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeB2["DIGIT B2"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeSUB2["SUBTRACTOR"]
    nodeSH1 --> nodeSUB2
    nodeSUB2 --> nodeG2["GAIN STAGE X2"]
    nodeG2 --> nodeSH2["SAMPLE AND HOLD STAGE 2"]
    nodeSH2 --> nodeSN["STAGE N SUB ADC"]
    nodeSN --> nodeBN["DIGIT BN"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeDC["DIGITAL CORRECTION AND DELAY ALIGNMENT BLOCK"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeDC
    nodeBN --> nodeDC
    nodeDC --> nodeOUT["N BIT BINARY OUTPUT D0 TO DN-1"]
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
1. The input is sample-held, then each stage extracts 1.5 bits and a residue.
2. The residue is amplified ×2 and passed to the next stage.
3. All stages operate on **different samples** in parallel (pipelined).
4. The digital correction block aligns bits in time and combines them into the final N-bit output.

---

## 4.3 Cyclic ADC — Recursive Single-Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIN["ANALOG INPUT VIN AT CYCLE 0"] --> nodeSH["SAMPLE AND HOLD CAPACITOR"]
    nodeSH --> nodeCMP["SINGLE COMPARATOR THRESHOLD VREF DIV 2"]
    nodeCMP --> nodeBIT["OUTPUT BIT BK OF CYCLE K"]
    nodeCMP --> nodeDAC["1 BIT SUB DAC OUTPUT BK TIMES VREF DIV 2"]
    nodeSH --> nodeSUB["SUBTRACTOR"]
    nodeDAC --> nodeSUB
    nodeSUB --> nodeM2["MULTIPLY BY TWO AMPLIFIER GAIN 2"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeFB["FEEDBACK SWITCH TO SAMPLE HOLD INPUT"]
    nodeFB --> nodeSH
    nodeBIT --> nodeREG["SHIFT REGISTER COLLECTS B0 B1 B2 B3 ... BN-1"]
    nodeREG --> nodeOUT["N BIT BINARY OUTPUT D0 TO DN-1"]
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
1. The same input node is **time-multiplexed** with the feedback residue.
2. One comparator decides bit $b_k$ each cycle.
3. The residue $V_{in,k+1} = 2V_{in,k} - b_k V_{ref}$ is computed and fed back.
4. After N cycles, the shift register contains the complete digital word.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Compare the conversion speed and hardware complexity of Flash, Pipeline, and Cyclic ADCs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Architecture | Speed | Hardware Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| **Flash ADC** | Fastest (1 clock cycle) | Highest: $2^N - 1$ comparators, $2^N$ resistors |
| **Pipeline ADC** | High (1 sample/clock after latency) | Moderate: N stages × (1 op-amp + few comparators) |
| **Cyclic ADC** | Slowest (N clock cycles per sample) | Lowest: 1 op-amp, ~5 capacitors, switches only |

**Concluding line (1 mark):** Flash is fastest but area-hungry; Cyclic is area-efficient but slow; Pipeline is the best compromise for medium-to-high resolution and speed.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is a 1.5-bit stage preferred over a 1-bit stage in modern pipeline ADCs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A 1-bit pipeline stage has only one comparator and cannot correct for the comparator's input-referred offset voltage. In contrast, a **1.5-bit stage** uses three comparators with thresholds at $-V_{ref}/4$, 0, and $+V_{ref}/4$ and produces a ternary output $d_k \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$.

This redundancy provides **digital error correction** — comparator offsets up to $\pm V_{ref}/4$ are tolerated because overlapping decision regions allow the digital correction block to recover the correct final code. This dramatically **relaxes the comparator design** and is the cornerstone of modern high-resolution pipeline ADCs.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. The structure below mirrors the actual board pattern: a main question with two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a 3-bit Flash ADC. Derive the expression for the number of comparators and ladder resistors.

**(b)** Discuss the merits, demerits, and applications of the Flash ADC. Explain the concept of "thermometer code" and how a priority encoder converts it to binary.

#### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Reference:** See *Section 4.1 (Flash ADC Architecture Flow)* above.

**Operation (4 marks):**
1. A reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied across a resistor ladder of $2^N = 8$ equal resistors for a 3-bit ADC. This creates 7 internal nodes at voltages $V_{ref}/8, 2V_{ref}/8, \ldots, 7V_{ref}/8$.
2. Each of the **7 comparators** receives the analog input $V_{in}$ at its non-inverting terminal and one ladder node at its inverting terminal.
3. The comparator outputs form a **thermometer code**: if $V_{in} = 3.5V_{ref}/8$, comparators 1, 2, 3 output "1" and 4, 5, 6, 7 output "0".
4. The **priority encoder** converts the position of the 1-to-0 transition into a 3-bit binary word: e.g., thermometer code `0000111` → binary `011`.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
- Quantization levels = $2^N = 8$
- Thresholds between levels = $2^N - 1 = 7$
- Therefore, **comparators = $2^N - 1 = 7$** and **resistors = $2^N = 8$** ✓

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Merits (2 marks):**
- Extremely fast: 1 clock cycle conversion → sampling rates of **GS/s** are possible.
- Simple control logic: no clock-sequencing or state machine needed.
- Excellent for high-speed, low-resolution applications.

**Demerits (2 marks):**
- Component count grows **exponentially** with resolution: $2^N - 1$ comparators.
- High input capacitance $\approx 2^N \cdot C_{comp}$ loads the previous stage.
- High power consumption (each comparator draws static current).
- **Practical resolution limited to ~8 bits** because of area, power, and input loading.

**Applications (1.5 marks):** Digital oscilloscopes, radar, high-speed data acquisition, satellite communication, disk-drive read channels, flash ADC front-ends in oscilloscope instruments.

**Thermometer Code and Encoder (1.5 marks):**
- The thermometer code is a unary representation where the bit position of the 1-to-0 transition directly encodes the analog amplitude.
- A **priority encoder** (e.g., a fat-tree or Wallace-tree structure) detects the highest-order "1" in the thermometer code and outputs the corresponding binary index in $O(\log_2(2^N)) = O(N)$ gate delays.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Pipeline ADC. Define *latency* and *throughput* and explain why this architecture is preferred for high-speed medium-resolution applications.

**(b)** Explain the operation of a 1.5-bit pipeline stage with a residue transfer curve. Show how digital error correction is achieved. Compare Pipeline and Cyclic ADCs in terms of hardware, speed, and resolution.

#### **Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Reference:** See *Section 4.2 (Pipeline ADC Multi-Stage Architecture)* above.

**Working (4 marks):**
1. The analog input is sample-held at Stage 0.
2. Stage 1's 1.5-bit sub-ADC produces a ternary digit $d_1 \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ by comparing the input against $-V_{ref}/4$, 0, and $+V_{ref}/4$.
3. The 1.5-bit sub-DAC reconstructs the analog equivalent of $d_1 \cdot V_{ref}/4$.
4. The subtractor computes the error, and the gain stage (×2) amplifies the residue by 2 to span the full input range of the next stage.
5. The residue is passed to the next stage, which works on the **next sample** (already in the pipeline). All stages operate concurrently on different samples.
6. After $N$ stages, the digital correction block aligns all bits in time (compensating for the latency) and combines them into the final $N$-bit output.

**Definitions (1.5 marks):**
- **Latency** = time for one sample to propagate from input to digital output = $N \times T_{clk}$.
- **Throughput** = number of samples processed per second after the pipeline is full = $1 / T_{stage}$.

**Why preferred (1.5 marks):** For high-speed medium-resolution (10–14 bits at 100+ MS/s) applications, the Pipeline ADC offers the best trade-off: high throughput (1 sample/clock) without the exponential area penalty of a Flash ADC and without the slow conversion of a Cyclic ADC.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1.5-bit Stage Operation (2.5 marks):**

Refer to *Section 3.3 (Pipeline ADC 1.5-bit stage derivation)* for the residue equation:
$V_{res,k} = 2V_{in,k} - d_k V_{ref}/2$

The **residue transfer curve** is a 3-segment piecewise-linear plot:
- Slope = 2 in the middle region ($|V_{in}| \le V_{ref}/4$).
- Slope = 2 in the outer regions, with the curve shifted by $\pm V_{ref}/2$ to provide $\pm V_{ref}/4$ of overlap.

**Digital Error Correction (1.5 marks):**
- Comparator offsets up to $\pm V_{ref}/4$ cause a wrong $d_k$, but the residue stays within bounds.
- Adjacent stages' overlapping decision regions allow the digital correction logic to add shifted versions of $d_k$ with proper weights, effectively cancelling the offset error.
- The final binary output is obtained by adding all $d_k$ with appropriate sign and weight.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pipeline ADC | Cyclic ADC |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware | N stages × op-amp + 3 comparators | 1 op-amp + 1 comparator |
| Throughput | 1 sample/clock (after fill) | 1 sample / (N × clock) |
| Latency | N clocks | N clocks |
| Resolution | 8–18 bits | 10–16 bits |
| Power | Medium | Low |
| Calibration need | Digital correction | Capacitor matching |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1"** in the comparator count formula. The most common error: writing "$2^N$ comparators for an N-bit Flash ADC" — this is **WRONG**. The correct formula is $2^N - 1$ comparators and $2^N$ ladder resistors. **(–2 marks if missed)**
> 2. **Confusing latency with throughput** in Pipeline ADCs. Many students write "Pipeline ADC has 1 clock conversion time" — actually **throughput** is 1 sample/clock but **latency** is N clocks. Use the correct term! **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Not explaining digital error correction** in 1.5-bit Pipeline stages. Merely stating "1.5-bit is used" is worth 0 marks — the key is the *redundancy* that enables offset correction. **(–3 marks)**
> 4. **Omitting the residue equation derivation** in Cyclic ADCs. KTU examiners reward the recurrence $V_{in,k+1} = 2V_{in,k} - b_k V_{ref}$ explicitly. **(–2 marks)**
> 5. **In Flash ADC, not drawing the resistor ladder** as part of the block diagram. A block-only answer is incomplete. **(–2 marks)**
> 6. **Misnaming the encoder.** A "decoder" converts binary to thermometer; an "**encoder**" converts thermometer to binary. Many students reverse these. **(–1 mark)**

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density bullet summary — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **Flash ADC:** Fastest, $2^N - 1$ comparators, $2^N$ ladder resistors, 1 clock conversion, thermometer code + priority encoder, practical limit ~8 bits.
- ☐ **Flash ADC LSB:** $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^N$. Threshold $V_{th,k} = V_{ref} \cdot k / 2^N$.
- ☐ **Cyclic ADC:** 1 op-amp + ~5 caps, N clocks per sample, residue recurrence $V_{in,k+1} = 2V_{in,k} - b_k V_{ref}$, lowest area.
- ☐ **Pipeline ADC:** N stages, 1.5-bit per stage is industry standard, throughput = 1 sample/clock, latency = N clocks, **digital error correction** is the key feature.
- ☐ **1.5-bit stage:** 3 comparators, ternary digit $d_k \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$, tolerates $\pm V_{ref}/4$ comparator offset.
- ☐ **Throughput vs. Latency:** Pipeline = high throughput, finite latency; Cyclic = low throughput, finite latency; Flash = high throughput, 1-clock latency.
- ☐ **Hardware scaling:** Flash = $\mathcal{O}(2^N)$ — exponential. Pipeline = $\mathcal{O}(N)$ — linear. Cyclic = $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — constant.
- ☐ **Power scaling:** Flash dominates power consumption; Cyclic is best for low-power IoT; Pipeline is balanced.
- ☐ **Resolution range:** Flash (4–8 bits) → Pipeline (8–18 bits) → Cyclic (10–16 bits).
- ☐ **Application map:** Oscilloscopes/雷达 → Flash; Communications/Video → Pipeline; Sensors/Medical → Cyclic.
- ☐ **Formula you must memorize verbatim:** $N_{comp} = 2^N - 1$, $N_R = 2^N$, $T_{conv,cyclic} = N \cdot T_{clk}$, $V_{res,pipeline} = 2V_{in} - d_k V_{ref}/2$.
- ☐ **Common examiner traps:** the "-1" in comparator count; latency ≠ throughput; 1.5-bit is about *error correction*, not just "1.5 bits".
- ☐ **Mnemonic for architectures:** **"FPC"** — **F**lash = Fast & Fat, **P**ipeline = Parallel & Practical, **C**yclic = Compact & Cautious.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Successive Approximation Type and Oversampling ADCs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT754 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Successive Approximation Type and Oversampling ADCs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is a *Nyquist-rate*, *closed-loop* analog-to-digital converter that converts an analog input signal into a digital output code by performing a **binary search** on the input voltage using a single comparator, a digital-to-analog converter (DAC), and a successive approximation register. An **N-bit** SAR ADC completes one full conversion in exactly **N clock cycles** by successively testing each bit, beginning from the **Most Significant Bit (MSB)** and proceeding to the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The SAR ADC is the workhorse of medium-to-high resolution (8–18 bits) data acquisition systems where power efficiency, moderate speed (10 kS/s to 100 MS/s), and compact silicon area are required. It forms the core of microcontroller embedded ADCs in KTU lab kits (e.g., STM32, MSP430).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Weighing Scale
Imagine you have a sealed black box and **8 reference weights** (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128 g) on a balance scale. You must determine the unknown mass inside the box using the *fewest* weighings.

A **naïve approach** would try every combination — slow and inefficient. The **binary search strategy** is smarter:

1. First, place the **heaviest** weight (128 g). If the scale tilts UP (object heavier), keep it; if DOWN, remove it.
2. Move to the next heaviest weight, repeat the test.
3. After **8 weighings**, you have isolated the exact mass.

The SAR ADC does exactly this: each "weighing" is a **single comparator decision** in one clock cycle, and the "weights" are binary-weighted voltage levels generated by the internal DAC. The heaviest test (MSB) is performed **first**, and the lightest (LSB) is performed **last**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because it only needs N clock cycles regardless of input value, the SAR ADC's **conversion time is constant and predictable** — a property highly valued in real-time control loops (e.g., motor control, power inverters).

---

## 1.2 Oversampling ADC (Sigma-Delta / ΔΣ ADC)

### Formal Definition
An **Oversampling ADC**, more specifically a **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) Modulator followed by a Decimation Filter**, is a converter that achieves **high resolution (16–24 bits)** by sampling the input signal at a rate **far greater than the Nyquist rate** (typically 32× to 256× oversampling), trading off **speed for resolution**. It uses a technique called **noise shaping** that pushes the bulk of the quantization noise energy toward higher frequencies (outside the signal band), where it is then removed by a digital low-pass decimation filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The sigma-delta ADC dominates the audio (44.1 kHz / 96 kHz / 192 kHz), precision measurement (weighing scales, DVMs, thermocouple readers), and seismic instrumentation markets. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically covers **first-order** Σ-Δ modulators.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Crowd Averaging
Imagine you are trying to estimate the **average height** of students in a college.

- **Without oversampling:** You measure only 10 students (a small, "coarse" sample). Your estimate has large random error.
- **With oversampling:** You measure 10,000 students and take the **average**. The random fluctuations cancel out, and your estimate becomes extremely precise.

A sigma-delta ADC operates on the same principle but adds a twist — it **shapes the noise** away from where you care about. Imagine a noisy classroom: rather than just averaging over more students, you **strategically time** the noise bursts (so they happen when students are between classes, not during lectures). This is **noise shaping** — pushing quantization noise out of the signal band so it can be filtered away without harming the desired signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** A *plain* oversampling ADC (without noise shaping) gains only **3 dB of SNR per doubling of sampling rate (each octave of OSR)**. With **first-order noise shaping**, this gain jumps to **9 dB per octave** — a threefold improvement for the same silicon complexity.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | SAR ADC | Oversampling (Σ-Δ) ADC |
|-----------|---------|------------------------|
| Sampling Rate | At Nyquist | 32×–256× Nyquist |
| Resolution | 8–18 bits | 16–24 bits |
| Conversion Time | N clock cycles | OSR cycles (much longer) |
| Latency | Very low | High (due to decimation) |
| Architecture | Feedback (closed loop) | Feedback modulator + filter |
| Typical Application | Microcontrollers, data loggers | Audio DACs, precision DVMs |
| Power Consumption | Low | Moderate (analog + DSP) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SAR ADC — Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Architecture (Block-Level)
The SAR ADC contains four primary blocks:

1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit** — Acquires and freezes the analog input voltage V_in during the conversion window.
2. **Comparator** — A 1-bit ADC that decides whether V_in > V_DAC (output HIGH) or V_in < V_DAC (output LOW).
3. **Internal DAC** — Typically an R-2R ladder or charge-redistribution C-2C array that generates an analog voltage corresponding to the current SAR register code.
4. **SAR Logic + Control** — A finite state machine that performs the binary search algorithm.

### 2.1.2 Binary Search Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic
For an N-bit conversion, the algorithm proceeds as follows:

- **Cycle 1 (MSB test):** Set MSB = 1, all other bits = 0. The DAC produces V_DAC = V_REF / 2.
   - If V_in ≥ V_DAC → keep MSB = 1
   - If V_in < V_DAC → reset MSB = 0
- **Cycle 2 (MSB-1 test):** Set MSB-1 = 1, keeping previously decided bits. DAC = (b_N-1 · 2^(N-1) + b_N-2 · 2^(N-2) + … + 0 + … ) · V_REF / 2^N
- …
- **Cycle N (LSB test):** Set LSB = 1. The final DAC output differs from V_in by at most **1 LSB**.

### 2.1.3 Mathematical Foundation
The reconstructed DAC voltage at any cycle *k* is given by:

$$V_{\text{DAC}}(k) = \frac{V_{\text{REF}}}{2^{N}} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$$

where $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ are the bits decided up to cycle *k*. The final digital output $D$ for input $V_{\text{in}}$ is:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{in}} \cdot 2^{N}}{V_{\text{REF}}} \right\rfloor$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** SAR ADCs dominate **battery-powered IoT sensors, biomedical instrumentation (ECG front-ends), and industrial process control** because they consume power only during the brief N-cycle conversion window and remain idle otherwise. A leading example is the **AD7980 (18-bit, 1 MSPS)** from Analog Devices.

---

## 2.2 Oversampling ADC — Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Oversampling Concept
The **Oversampling Ratio (OSR)** is defined as the ratio of the actual sampling frequency $f_s$ to the Nyquist frequency $2 f_B$, where $f_B$ is the bandwidth of the input signal:

$$\text{OSR} = \frac{f_s}{2 f_B}$$

In the absence of noise shaping, oversampling by a factor OSR spreads the **quantization noise power** over a wider frequency range, reducing the in-band noise power by a factor of OSR:

$$e_q^2(\text{in-band}) = \frac{\Delta^2}{12 \cdot \text{OSR}}$$

### 2.2.2 Noise Shaping in Sigma-Delta Modulator
The sigma-delta modulator consists of an **integrator**, a **1-bit quantizer**, and a **feedback DAC** in a closed loop. The signal transfer function (STF) and noise transfer function (NTF) for a **first-order** modulator are:

$$\text{STF}(z) = z^{-1}$$

$$\text{NTF}(z) = (1 - z^{-1})$$

The magnitude response of the NTF at low frequencies (z → 1) approaches zero, **suppressing in-band quantization noise**. Specifically:

$$\vert \text{NTF}(f) \vert = 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi f}{f_s}\right)$$

At low frequencies ($f \ll f_s$), this approximates $\vert \text{NTF}(f) \vert \approx 2\pi f / f_s$, giving high-pass shaping to the noise.

### 2.2.3 Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) — The KTU Critical Formula

For a **first-order sigma-delta modulator** with B-bit internal quantizer (typically B = 1), the in-band SQNR is:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \, N + 1.76 + 30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR}) - 5.17 \;\; \text{dB}$$

where N is the effective resolution in bits. Equivalently, the **effective resolution** for a first-order Σ-Δ modulator:

$$N_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR}) - 5.17}{6.02} + N_{\text{quantizer}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Σ-Δ ADCs are the heart of **digital audio (DSD, FLAC playback chains), seismology (exploration geophones), and precision weighing scales (0.001 g resolution)**. The TI ADS1271 (24-bit, 105 kS/s) and Cirrus Logic CS5381 (24-bit, 192 kS/s) are industry benchmarks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | LSB Voltage (SAR/DAC) | $\Delta = V_{\text{REF}} / 2^{N}$ | Volts | Smallest resolvable step |
| 2 | Quantization Noise Power | $e_q^{2} = \Delta^{2} / 12$ | $V^{2}$ | Uniform distribution assumption |
| 3 | SAR Conversion Time | $T_{\text{conv}} = N \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ | Seconds | N clock cycles for N-bit |
| 4 | Oversampling Ratio | $\text{OSR} = f_s / (2 f_B)$ | Dimensionless | $f_B$ = signal bandwidth |
| 5 | In-band noise (oversampling only) | $e_q^{2} = \Delta^{2} / (12 \cdot \text{OSR})$ | $V^{2}$ | Without noise shaping |
| 6 | SNR gain (oversampling only) | $\Delta\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{OSR})$ | dB | ~3 dB per octave doubling |
| 7 | SNR gain (1st-order Σ-Δ) | $\Delta\text{SNR} = 30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR}) - 5.17$ | dB | ~9 dB per octave doubling |
| 8 | SNR gain (Lth-order Σ-Δ) | $\Delta\text{SNR} = (6L + 3) \log_{2}(\text{OSR}) - C_{L}$ | dB | $C_L$ is order-dependent constant |
| 9 | Total SQNR (1st-order) | $6.02N + 1.76 + 30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR}) - 5.17$ | dB | Full expression |
| 10 | Effective no. of bits | $N_{\text{eff}} = (\text{SNR} - 1.76) / 6.02$ | bits | Used for ENOB specification |
| 11 | NTF magnitude (1st-order) | $\vert 1 - z^{-1} \vert = 2 \sin(\pi f / f_s)$ | Linear | At frequency $f$ |
| 12 | STF (1st-order) | $z^{-1}$ | Linear | Pure delay, unity gain |
| 13 | Decimation filter factor | $M = f_s / f_{s,\text{out}}$ | Integer | Equal to OSR typically |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** "**3-6-9 Rule**" — Oversampling alone gives **3 dB** per octave; first-order Σ-Δ gives **9 dB**; second-order Σ-Δ gives **15 dB** per octave. Each additional order of the modulator adds 6 dB/octave.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Architecture Choices

- **Why binary search in SAR?** — It minimizes the number of decisions (and hence the conversion time) from a worst-case $2^{N}$ trials down to exactly $N$ trials. This is the *optimal* decision tree for finding an unknown value in a sorted range.

- **Why oversample?** — Because quantization noise is **white** (spectrally flat), spreading it over a wider bandwidth reduces the noise density per Hertz in the band of interest.

- **Why noise-shape?** — Without shaping, oversampling is wasteful — most of the noise is at high frequencies anyway. Shaping pushes noise *away* from the signal band, so a low-pass filter can eliminate it without distorting the signal.

- **Why a 1-bit quantizer in sigma-delta?** — A 1-bit quantizer is **inherently linear** (only two possible outputs), eliminating the DAC non-linearity problem that plagues multi-bit designs. Resolution is recovered through oversampling and noise shaping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Noise Power (Uniform Distribution Model)

**Given:** A quantizer with step size $\Delta$ produces an error $e_q$ uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.

**Find:** Mean-square value $E[e_q^{2}]$.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Deduction

Step 1 — Express the mean-square error as the second moment about zero:

$$E[e_q^{2}] = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e_q^{2} \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q$$

Step 2 — Substitute the uniform probability density function $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$ over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, and zero elsewhere:

$$E[e_q^{2}] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q$$

Step 3 — Extract the constant $1/\Delta$:

$$E[e_q^{2}] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^{2} \, de_q$$

Step 4 — Evaluate the antiderivative $\int e_q^{2} \, de_q = e_q^{3}/3$:

$$E[e_q^{2}] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^{3}}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

Step 5 — Apply the limits:

$$E[e_q^{2}] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left( \frac{\Delta}{2} \right)^{3} - \left( -\frac{\Delta}{2} \right)^{3} \right]$$

Step 6 — Simplify the cube difference $\Delta^{3}/8 - (-\Delta^{3}/8) = \Delta^{3}/4$:

$$E[e_q^{2}] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{\Delta^{3}}{4}$$

Step 7 — Cancel $\Delta$ to obtain the final quantization noise power:

$$E[e_q^{2}] = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$$

**Conversion logic explained:** The factor $1/\Delta$ in step 2 comes from the normalization of the uniform PDF (its integral over the support $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$ must equal 1). Steps 4–6 perform standard definite integration of the polynomial $x^{2}$. Step 7 collapses units: $(\Delta^{2}) \cdot (1/\Delta) \cdot \Delta^{3} / 12 = \Delta^{2}/12$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SNR Improvement from Oversampling (Without Noise Shaping)

**Given:** Quantization noise power $\Delta^{2}/12$ is uniformly distributed over the frequency range $[-f_s/2, +f_s/2]$.

**Find:** In-band noise power when signal bandwidth is $f_B$ and oversampling ratio is $\text{OSR} = f_s / (2 f_B)$.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Deduction

Step 1 — Compute the two-sided noise spectral density (PSD), assuming uniform distribution:

$$S_{e}(f) = \frac{e_q^{2}}{f_s} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12 \, f_s} \quad \text{for } \vert f \vert \leq f_s/2$$

Step 2 — The total noise power is the integral of PSD over the full Nyquist band:

$$e_q^{2} = \int_{-f_s/2}^{+f_s/2} S_{e}(f) \, df = S_{e}(f) \cdot f_s = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$$

This step confirms Step 1 is correctly normalized.

Step 3 — When a low-pass filter of bandwidth $f_B$ is applied, only the noise in $[-f_B, +f_B]$ survives:

$$e_{q,\text{in-band}}^{2} = \int_{-f_B}^{+f_B} S_{e}(f) \, df = S_{e}(f) \cdot 2 f_B = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12 \, f_s} \cdot 2 f_B$$

Step 4 — Substitute $2 f_B = f_s / \text{OSR}$:

$$e_{q,\text{in-band}}^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12 \, f_s} \cdot \frac{f_s}{\text{OSR}}$$

Step 5 — Cancel $f_s$:

$$e_{q,\text{in-band}}^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12 \cdot \text{OSR}}$$

Step 6 — Compute SNR for a full-scale sinusoid with amplitude $V_{\text{FS}}/2$ (RMS = $V_{\text{FS}} / (2\sqrt{2})$):

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{V_{\text{signal,rms}}^{2}}{e_{q,\text{in-band}}^{2}} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{2} / 8}{\Delta^{2} / (12 \cdot \text{OSR})}$$

Step 7 — Substitute $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^{N}$:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{2} / 8}{V_{\text{FS}}^{2} / (2^{2N} \cdot 12 \cdot \text{OSR})} = \frac{2^{2N} \cdot 12 \cdot \text{OSR}}{8}$$

Step 8 — Simplify:

$$\text{SNR} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N} \cdot \text{OSR}$$

Step 9 — Convert to decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 N \log_{10}(2) + 10 \log_{10}(\text{OSR})$$

Step 10 — Substitute $10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$ and $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02$:

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 N + 1.76 + 10 \log_{10}(\text{OSR})}$$

**Conversion logic explained:** The SNR scales linearly with OSR (in linear domain) because doubling OSR halves the in-band noise (step 4–5). In dB this is $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3$ dB per octave — the famous "**3 dB per octave**" rule for plain oversampling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SQNR of First-Order Sigma-Delta Modulator

**Given:** A first-order Σ-Δ modulator with NTF $= (1 - z^{-1})$, quantizer step $\Delta$, and OSR.

**Find:** The in-band SQNR expression.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Deduction

Step 1 — The quantization noise $e_q$ injected at the quantizer output is shaped by the NTF. In the z-domain, the output noise is $E_q(z) \cdot (1 - z^{-1})$:

$$E_{q,\text{out}}(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot E_q(z)$$

Step 2 — Substitute $z = e^{j 2 \pi f / f_s}$ and find the magnitude-squared response:

$$\vert \text{NTF}(f) \vert^{2} = \vert 1 - e^{-j 2 \pi f / f_s} \vert^{2} = 2 - 2 \cos\left(\frac{2 \pi f}{f_s}\right) = 4 \sin^{2}\left(\frac{\pi f}{f_s}\right)$$

Step 3 — The shaped noise PSD is:

$$S_{e,\text{shaped}}(f) = S_e(f) \cdot 4 \sin^{2}\left(\frac{\pi f}{f_s}\right)$$

Step 4 — For $f \ll f_s$, use the small-angle approximation $\sin(\pi f / f_s) \approx \pi f / f_s$:

$$\vert \text{NTF}(f) \vert^{2} \approx 4 \left( \frac{\pi f}{f_s} \right)^{2}$$

Step 5 — Integrate the shaped PSD over the signal band $[-f_B, +f_B]$:

$$e_{q,\text{shaped}}^{2} = \int_{-f_B}^{+f_B} \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12 \, f_s} \cdot 4 \sin^{2}\left(\frac{\pi f}{f_s}\right) df$$

Step 6 — Using the small-angle approximation:

$$e_{q,\text{shaped}}^{2} \approx \int_{-f_B}^{+f_B} \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12 \, f_s} \cdot 4 \left( \frac{\pi f}{f_s} \right)^{2} df$$

Step 7 — Extract constants:

$$e_{q,\text{shaped}}^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2} \pi^{2}}{3 f_s^{3}} \int_{-f_B}^{+f_B} f^{2} \, df$$

Step 8 — Evaluate the definite integral $\int_{-f_B}^{+f_B} f^{2} \, df = (2 f_B^{3})/3$:

$$e_{q,\text{shaped}}^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2} \pi^{2}}{3 f_s^{3}} \cdot \frac{2 f_B^{3}}{3} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \Delta^{2} f_B^{3}}{9 f_s^{3}}$$

Step 9 — Substitute $f_s = 2 \, \text{OSR} \cdot f_B$:

$$e_{q,\text{shaped}}^{2} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \Delta^{2} f_B^{3}}{9 (2 \, \text{OSR} \cdot f_B)^{3}} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} \Delta^{2}}{9 \cdot 8 \cdot \text{OSR}^{3}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \Delta^{2}}{36 \, \text{OSR}^{3}}$$

Step 10 — Form the SQNR (signal power = $V_{\text{FS}}^{2}/8$, $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^{N}$):

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{2}/8}{\pi^{2} \Delta^{2} / (36 \, \text{OSR}^{3})} = \frac{36 \cdot 2^{2N} \cdot \text{OSR}^{3}}{8 \pi^{2}}$$

Step 11 — Convert to decibels:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{36}{8 \pi^{2}}\right) + 20 N \log_{10}(2) + 30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR})$$

Step 12 — Evaluate $10 \log_{10}(36 / (8 \pi^{2})) = 10 \log_{10}(0.4559) \approx -3.41$ dB. Combined with the standard full-scale sinusoid correction $10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$ dB:

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 N + 1.76 + 30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR}) - 5.17$$

Step 13 — Since $30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR}) = 10 \log_{2}(\text{OSR}) \cdot \log_{10}(2) \cdot 3 / \log_{2}(10) \approx 9.97 \log_{2}(\text{OSR})$:

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02 N + 1.76 + 9.03 \log_{2}(\text{OSR}) - 5.17}$$

**Conversion logic explained:** Each doubling of OSR (one octave) increases SQNR by $30 \log_{10}(2) \approx 9.03$ dB — the **"9 dB per octave"** rule for first-order Σ-Δ. This is **3× better** than oversampling alone (3 dB/octave) because the noise shaping amplifies high-frequency noise, which is then removed by the decimation filter.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — 4-bit SAR ADC Conversion

**Given:** A 4-bit SAR ADC with $V_{\text{REF}} = 5.0$ V samples $V_{\text{in}} = 3.45$ V.

**Find:** All bit decisions and final output code.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Step 1 — Compute the LSB step:

$$\Delta = V_{\text{REF}} / 2^{4} = 5.0 / 16 = 0.3125 \text{ V}$$

Step 2 — Compute the expected output code:

$$D = \lfloor 3.45 \cdot 16 / 5.0 \rfloor = \lfloor 11.04 \rfloor = 11 = (1011)_{2}$$

Step 3 — Now simulate the binary search:

| Cycle | Bits Set to Test | $V_{\text{DAC}}$ (V) | Compare $V_{\text{in}}$ vs $V_{\text{DAC}}$ | Decision |
|-------|------------------|----------------------|---------------------------------------------|----------|
| 1 | b3=1, rest=0 → 1000 | 2.500 | 3.45 > 2.50 | b3 = 1 |
| 2 | b2=1, rest=0 → 1100 | 3.750 | 3.45 < 3.75 | b2 = 0 |
| 3 | b1=1, rest=0 → 1010 | 3.125 | 3.45 > 3.125 | b1 = 1 |
| 4 | b0=1, rest=0 → 1011 | 3.4375 | 3.45 > 3.4375 | b0 = 1 |

Step 4 — Final SAR output code: **1011** = 11, giving reconstructed $V_{\text{DAC}} = 11 \cdot 0.3125 = 3.4375$ V.

Step 5 — Quantization error:

$$e_q = V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{DAC}} = 3.45 - 3.4375 = 0.0125 \text{ V}$$

This is well within $\pm \Delta/2 = \pm 0.15625$ V — confirming the conversion is valid.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — First-Order Σ-Δ SQNR Calculation

**Given:** A first-order sigma-delta modulator with $N = 1$ bit internal quantizer, OSR = 64, full-scale input sinusoid.

**Find:** Theoretical SQNR and effective number of bits (ENOB).

### Step-by-Step Solution

Step 1 — Apply the SQNR formula for first-order Σ-Δ:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot 1 + 1.76 + 30 \log_{10}(64) - 5.17$$

Step 2 — Compute $30 \log_{10}(64)$:

$$\log_{10}(64) = \log_{10}(2^{6}) = 6 \log_{10}(2) = 6 \cdot 0.3010 = 1.806$$

$$30 \log_{10}(64) = 30 \cdot 1.806 = 54.18 \text{ dB}$$

Step 3 — Sum all terms:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 + 1.76 + 54.18 - 5.17 = 56.79 \text{ dB}$$

Step 4 — Compute ENOB:

$$\text{ENOB} = (\text{SNR} - 1.76) / 6.02 = (56.79 - 1.76) / 6.02 = 55.03 / 6.02 \approx 9.14 \text{ bits}$$

Step 5 — Interpretation: A **1-bit** internal quantizer achieves **>9 effective bits** at OSR = 64, demonstrating the power of oversampling + noise shaping. Doubling OSR to 128 would add ~9 dB, raising ENOB to ~10.6 bits.

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional flow** of the SAR ADC's binary search algorithm, including the comparator decision feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START Conversion]) --> sampleA["S/H Sample V_in<br/>Freeze on Hold Capacitor"]
    sampleA --> initA["Initialize SAR Register<br/>b3 b2 b1 b0 = 1 0 0 0"]
    initA --> cycleA1["Cycle 1: MSB Test<br/>V_DAC = V_REF / 2 = 2.50 V"]
    cycleA1 --> compA1{"Comparator:<br/>V_in > V_DAC?"}
    compA1 -->|YES| keepA1["Keep b3 = 1<br/>Update b2 = 1"]
    compA1 -->|NO| dropA1["Reset b3 = 0<br/>Update b2 = 1"]
    keepA1 --> cycleA2["Cycle 2: b2 Test<br/>V_DAC = 3.75 V"]
    dropA1 --> cycleA2
    cycleA2 --> compA2{"Comparator:<br/>V_in > V_DAC?"}
    compA2 -->|YES| keepA2["Keep b2 = 1<br/>Update b1 = 1"]
    compA2 -->|NO| dropA2["Reset b2 = 0<br/>Update b1 = 1"]

    dropA2 --> cycleA3
    cycleA3 --> compA3{"Comparator:<br/>V_in > V_DAC?"}
    compA3 -->|YES| keepA3["Keep b1 = 1<br/>Update b0 = 1"]
    compA3 -->|NO| dropA3["Reset b1 = 0<br/>Update b0 = 1"]
    keepA3 --> cycleA4["Cycle 4: LSB Test<br/>V_DAC = 3.4375 V"]
    dropA3 --> cycleA4
    cycleA4 --> compA4{"Comparator:<br/>V_in > V_DAC?"}
    compA4 -->|YES| keepA4["Keep b0 = 1"]
    compA4 -->|NO| dropA4["Reset b0 = 0"]
    keepA4 --> outputA["Final Code = b3 b2 b1 b0<br/>= 1 0 1 1 = 11"]
    dropA4 --> outputA
```

```mermaid
gantt
    title SAR ADC vs Sigma-Delta ADC Conversion Timeline
    dateFormat X

    Bit 6 Test         :a3, 2, 1
    Bit 5 Test         :a4, 3, 1
    Bit 4 Test         :a5, 4, 1
    Bit 3 Test         :a6, 5, 1
    Bit 2 Test         :a7, 6, 1
    Bit 1 Test         :a8, 7, 1
    Bit 0 LSB Test     :a9, 8, 1
    Output Latch       :a10, 9, 1

    Modulator Run (64 cycles)  :b1, 0, 64
    Decimation + Output       :b2, 64, 8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The SAR ADC completes its work in **9 clock cycles** for 8-bit resolution, whereas the first-order Σ-Δ modulator must run for **64 cycles** (OSR = 64) followed by a decimation filter. This is precisely why the SAR is the **speed champion** while the Σ-Δ is the **resolution champion**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC. State one advantage and one disadvantage.**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Set B]`

**Model Answer:**
A SAR ADC is a type of analog-to-digital converter that performs conversion by **binary search algorithm**, using a single comparator, a DAC, and a successive approximation register. An N-bit conversion completes in **N clock cycles**.

**Advantage:** Low power consumption and constant conversion time independent of input.
**Disadvantage:** Limited sampling rate compared to flash ADCs; requires a high-precision internal DAC.

---

### Question A2
**Q: What is oversampling? Why is it used in ADC design?**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Quiz]`

**Model Answer:**
**Oversampling** is the technique of sampling an analog signal at a frequency $f_s$ significantly higher than twice the maximum signal frequency (Nyquist rate). The ratio $f_s / (2 f_B)$ is called the **Oversampling Ratio (OSR)**.

**Why used:**
1. **Reduces in-band quantization noise** — noise power spreads over a wider band, lowering noise spectral density.
2. **Enables noise shaping** — when combined with a sigma-delta modulator, quantization noise is pushed to high frequencies, where it can be filtered out.
3. **Relaxes analog anti-aliasing filter requirements** — a gentler roll-off is sufficient.

---

## Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion** in SQNR derivations — the constant **5.17 dB** comes from $10 \log_{10}(\pi^{2}/3)$ combined with the full-scale sinusoid correction; do not omit it.
> 2. **Confusing oversampling SNR gain with noise-shaping SNR gain** — plain oversampling gives 3 dB/octave, while first-order Σ-Δ gives 9 dB/octave. Examiners deduct 2 marks if these are interchanged.
> 3. **In the SAR ADC, students often skip the comparator decision step** in the table — always write the comparison result explicitly.
> 4. **In sigma-delta derivations, do not forget** the STF $= z^{-1}$ signal passes through unaffected — only the noise is shaped.
> 5. **For LSB formula**, write $\Delta = V_{\text{REF}} / 2^{N}$, not $V_{\text{REF}} / N$.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. Show clearly how an N-bit conversion is performed in N clock cycles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **4-bit SAR ADC** has $V_{\text{REF}} = 5.0$ V and input $V_{\text{in}} = 3.2$ V. Determine all bit decisions and the final output code. Also compute the quantization error. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram Description:**

The SAR ADC consists of:
1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit
2. **Comparator** (1-bit ADC)
3. **Internal DAC** (R-2R ladder or charge-scaling capacitor array)
4. **SAR register** (N-bit shift register with parallel output)
5. **Control logic and clock generator**

**Operation (Binary Search Algorithm):**

| Step | Action | Significance |
|------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | S/H acquires V_in and holds it constant | Prevents input variation during decision |
| 2 | Initialize SAR register to **100…0** (MSB = 1, rest 0) | DAC output = V_REF/2 |
| 3 | Comparator tests V_in vs V_DAC | Decides bit value |
| 4 | If V_in > V_DAC → keep bit 1; else → reset to 0 | One bit decided |
| 5 | Shift "1" to next bit position, repeat step 3–4 | Iterative binary search |
| 6 | After N cycles, latch output as digital code | Conversion complete |

**Conversion Time:** $T_{\text{conv}} = N \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$

**Key Features:**
- **Constant conversion time** regardless of input magnitude.
- **Low latency** — output available after exactly N clock cycles.
- **Power scales with sampling rate** — ideal for power-gated systems.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — LSB Step Size:**

$$\Delta = V_{\text{REF}} / 2^{4} = 5.0 / 16 = 0.3125 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Expected Output Code:**

$$D = \lfloor 3.2 \cdot 16 / 5.0 \rfloor = \lfloor 10.24 \rfloor = 10 = (1010)_{2}$$

**Step 3 — Cycle-by-Cycle Binary Search Table:**

| Cycle | Bits Under Test | $V_{\text{DAC}}$ (V) | Comparator Decision | Bit Set |
|-------|-----------------|----------------------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | b3 = 1, b2b1b0 = 000 | 2.5000 | 3.2 > 2.5 → keep | **b3 = 1** |
| 2 | b2 = 1, code = 1100 | 3.7500 | 3.2 < 3.75 → reset | **b2 = 0** |
| 3 | b1 = 1, code = 1010 | 3.1250 | 3.2 > 3.125 → keep | **b1 = 1** |
| 4 | b0 = 1, code = 1011 | 3.4375 | 3.2 < 3.4375 → reset | **b0 = 0** |

**Step 4 — Final Output Code:** $(1010)_2$ = **10**

**Step 5 — Reconstructed DAC Voltage:**

$$V_{\text{DAC,final}} = 10 \cdot 0.3125 = 3.1250 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Quantization Error:**

$$e_q = V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{DAC,final}} = 3.2 - 3.125 = 0.075 \text{ V}$$

This is within the acceptable range of $\pm \Delta/2 = \pm 0.15625$ V. ✓

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **oversampling** and **noise shaping** in a first-order **sigma-delta (Σ-Δ) modulator**. Derive the expression for in-band SQNR. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A first-order sigma-delta ADC uses a 1-bit internal quantizer with $V_{\text{REF}} = 2.5$ V and oversampling ratio OSR = 128. Calculate the **theoretical SQNR** and **effective number of bits (ENOB)**. Also calculate the sampling frequency if the input signal bandwidth is 20 kHz. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Oversampling:**
Sampling at $f_s = \text{OSR} \cdot 2 f_B$ (where $f_B$ is signal bandwidth) distributes the quantization noise power $\Delta^{2}/12$ over a wider frequency range, reducing in-band noise density.

**Noise Shaping:**
In a first-order sigma-delta modulator, the architecture consists of an **integrator**, a **1-bit quantizer**, and a **1-bit feedback DAC** in a closed loop. The signal transfer function is:

$$\text{STF}(z) = z^{-1}$$

This is a pure unit-gain delay — the signal passes through unmodified.

The noise transfer function is:

$$\text{NTF}(z) = (1 - z^{-1})$$

This is a high-pass response. The magnitude is:

$$\vert \text{NTF}(f) \vert = 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi f}{f_s}\right)$$

**Derivation of SQNR:**

The quantization noise PSD after shaping:

$$S_{e,\text{shaped}}(f) = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12 \, f_s} \cdot 4 \sin^{2}\left(\frac{\pi f}{f_s}\right)$$

For low frequencies ($f \ll f_s$), $\sin(\pi f/f_s) \approx \pi f / f_s$:

$$S_{e,\text{shaped}}(f) \approx \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12 \, f_s} \cdot \frac{4 \pi^{2} f^{2}}{f_s^{2}}$$

In-band noise power (integrating over $[-f_B, +f_B]$):

$$e_{q,\text{shaped}}^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2} \pi^{2}}{9 f_s^{3}} \cdot \frac{2 f_B^{3}}{3} = \frac{\pi^{2} \Delta^{2}}{36 \cdot \text{OSR}^{3}}$$

For a full-scale sinusoid (signal RMS = $V_{\text{FS}}/(2\sqrt{2})$):

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{2}/8}{\pi^{2} \Delta^{2} / (36 \, \text{OSR}^{3})} = \frac{36 \cdot 2^{2N} \cdot \text{OSR}^{3}}{8 \pi^{2}}$$

Converting to dB:

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 N + 1.76 + 30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR}) - 5.17}$$

**Conclusion:** Each octave of OSR improves SQNR by 9 dB (3× better than oversampling alone).

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR})$:**

$$30 \log_{10}(128) = 30 \cdot \log_{10}(2^{7}) = 30 \cdot 7 \cdot 0.3010 = 63.21 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply SQNR formula** (for a 1-bit internal quantizer, $N = 1$):

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot 1 + 1.76 + 63.21 - 5.17$$

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 + 1.76 + 63.21 - 5.17 = 65.82 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ENOB:**

$$\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SNR} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{65.82 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{64.06}{6.02} \approx 10.64 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Sampling Frequency:**

$$f_s = \text{OSR} \cdot 2 f_B = 128 \cdot 2 \cdot 20{,}000 = 5{,}120{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 5.12 \text{ MHz}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| OSR | 128 |
| SQNR | 65.82 dB |
| ENOB | ≈ 10.64 bits |
| Sampling frequency | 5.12 MHz |
| Nyquist frequency | 40 kHz |

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### SAR ADC — Core Takeaways
- ☐ Uses **binary search** algorithm — tests bits from **MSB to LSB**.
- ☐ Conversion completes in **exactly N clock cycles** (N-bit ADC).
- ☐ Conversion time $T_{\text{conv}} = N \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ — **constant and input-independent**.
- ☐ Building blocks: S/H + Comparator + DAC + SAR register + Control logic.
- ☐ LSB voltage: $\Delta = V_{\text{REF}} / 2^{N}$.
- ☐ Advantages: **low power, low latency, compact area**.
- ☐ Disadvantage: **sampling rate limited by DAC settling time**.

### Oversampling (Sigma-Delta) ADC — Core Takeaways
- ☐ Oversampling Ratio: $\text{OSR} = f_s / (2 f_B)$.
- ☐ Quantization noise (in-band) reduces by factor of OSR (plain oversampling).
- ☐ **Noise shaping** via NTF = $(1 - z^{-1})$ for first-order.
- ☐ **Signal passes unchanged** (STF = $z^{-1}$).
- ☐ First-order Σ-Δ SQNR: $6.02 N + 1.76 + 30 \log_{10}(\text{OSR}) - 5.17$ dB.
- ☐ **3 dB/octave** for plain oversampling; **9 dB/octave** for first-order Σ-Δ; **15 dB/octave** for second-order.
- ☐ Internal quantizer is **1-bit** (inherently linear).
- ☐ Requires a **digital decimation filter** at the output to low-pass and downsample.

### Critical Numerical Benchmarks (Memorize for KTU)

| OSR | Plain Oversampling Gain | 1st-Order Σ-Δ Gain | 2nd-Order Σ-Δ Gain |
|-----|------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 4 | 6 dB | 18 dB | 30 dB |
| 16 | 12 dB | 36 dB | 60 dB |
| 64 | 18 dB | 54 dB | 90 dB |
| 256 | 24 dB | 72 dB | 120 dB |

### Common Mistakes to Avoid in KTU Exam
- ☐ Don't confuse **OSR = $f_s / 2 f_B$** with OSR = $f_s / f_B$.
- ☐ Remember the **5.17 dB constant** in the first-order SQNR formula — examiners love testing this.
- ☐ For SAR ADC, always show the **comparator decision** explicitly in your table.
- ☐ The 1-bit quantizer of Σ-Δ is **inherently linear** — this is why we use it!
- ☐ Higher-order modulators (L > 1) have stability concerns not present in first-order — mention this if asked about design tradeoffs.
- ☐ Sigma-delta ADCs have **high latency** due to decimation — unsuitable for control loops.
